topics.title: Data Transfer/Exchange Between Specified Memory Locations Using 8015
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGL507 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Data Transfer/Exchange Between Specified Memory Locations Using 8085

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Nomenclature:** The experiment title references "**8015**", which in the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme **PCCGL507** syllabus is universally interpreted as the **Intel 8085** 8-bit microprocessor. All instructions, opcodes, and timing references below belong to the **8085 instruction set**.

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Data Transfer / Data Exchange** in the context of the **Intel 8085 microprocessor** refers to the set of operations used to **copy (transfer)** the contents of one memory location (source) into another memory location (destination), or to **swap (exchange)** the contents between two memory locations using the 8085's internal register set as a temporary buffer.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme lab manual for **PCCGL507**, this experiment falls under **Module 2: 8085 Assembly Language Programming** and tests the following Course Outcomes:

- **CO2:** Develop and execute 8085 assembly language programs for data manipulation, memory operations, and I/O interfacing.
- **CO3:** Analyze the timing and execution behavior of 8085 instructions using T-state and machine cycle calculations.

The 8085 supports two principal categories of data movement:

| Operation Type | Meaning | Source Affected? | Destination Affected? |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Transfer (Copy)** | Source $\rightarrow$ Destination | **No** (original preserved) | **Yes** (overwritten) |
| **Exchange (Swap)** | Source $\leftrightarrow$ Destination | **Yes** (now holds old destination) | **Yes** (now holds old source) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key 8085 Restriction:** The 8085 has **no direct memory-to-memory** data transfer instruction. All data movements between memory locations must pass through the **Accumulator (Register A)** as an intermediary, or through the **HL/DE register pairs** for block operations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **The "Two-Box" Analogy**
>
> Imagine you have **Box A** (source memory location) and **Box B** (destination memory location) sitting on a table. You are the **Accumulator (A)** — you are the only one who can pick up and put down items.
>
> - **Data Transfer (Copy):** You open Box A, pick up the item, walk over to Box B, and put a *copy* of the item inside Box B. **Box A is untouched.**
> - **Data Exchange (Swap):** You pick up the item from Box A, then walk to Box B, take out its item, walk back, and put Box B's item into Box A, then put Box A's item into Box B. **Both boxes are now modified.**

The 8085 microprocessor behaves **identically**: the Accumulator acts as the *human hands* that mediate all inter-memory data movement.

## 1.3 Standard Hardware & Software Constants Used

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard 8085 Lab Parameters (KTU 8085 Trainer Kit)**
>
> - **Clock Frequency ($f_{\text{clk}}$):** $\mathbf{3 \text{ MHz}}$ (most KTU labs) or $\mathbf{2 \text{ MHz}}$ (older kits)
> - **Clock Period ($T$):** $T = 1/f_{\text{clk}}$
>   - At **3 MHz**: $T \approx \mathbf{0.333 \ \mu s}$
>   - At **2 MHz**: $T = \mathbf{0.5 \ \mu s}$
> - **Data Bus Width:** **8 bits**
> - **Address Bus Width:** **16 bits** $\rightarrow$ Addressable memory = $\mathbf{2^{16} = 65{,}536 \text{ bytes} = 64 \text{ KB}}$
> - **Memory Map used in this experiment:**
>   - Source block: starting address **2000H**
>   - Destination block: starting address **3000H**
>   - Block length (N): typically **05H** (5 bytes) for lab demonstration

## 1.4 Listing of 8085 Instructions Used in This Experiment

| Mnemonic | Opcode | Bytes | T-States | Operation |
| :--- | :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| `LXI Rp, data16` | 00Rp0001 + 2 bytes | 3 | 10 | Load 16-bit data into register pair |
| `MVI r, data8` | 00DDD110 + 1 byte | 2 | 7 | Move immediate 8-bit data into register |
| `MOV A, M` | 7EH | 1 | 7 | Copy data from memory[HL] into A |
| `MOV M, A` | 77H | 1 | 7 | Copy data from A into memory[HL] |
| `STAX D` | 12H | 1 | 7 | Store A into memory[DE] |
| `LDAX D` | 1AH | 1 | 7 | Load memory[DE] into A |
| `INX Rp` | 00RP0011 | 1 | 6 | Increment 16-bit register pair |
| `DCR r` | 00DDD101 | 1 | 4 | Decrement 8-bit register |
| `JNZ addr` | C2H + 2 bytes | 3 | 10/7 | Jump if Zero Flag = 0 |
| `HLT` | 76H | 1 | 5 | Halt the processor |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" Behind Memory-to-Memory Transfer

The 8085's **internal architecture** has only one data path between the registers and the external memory bus — it always passes through the **Accumulator (A)**. The reasons are:

1. **Hardware simplicity:** A single 8-bit internal data bus connecting A to memory reduces transistor count.
2. **Instruction encoding economy:** A `MOV` instruction is **1 byte** (e.g., `MOV A, M` = 7EH) because it implicitly uses A as the operand.
3. **Opcode space conservation:** With 246 opcodes available, dedicating one to a memory-to-memory copy would waste encoding space.

> [!NOTE]
> **The implication for programming:** Any "block transfer" program in 8085 is fundamentally a **three-step loop**: `READ → WRITE → INCREMENT → DECREMENT → CHECK`. The Accumulator is the universal "scratch register."

## 2.2 Structured Logic Flow — Data Transfer Algorithm

The 8085 block-transfer algorithm executes in this exact order:

1. **Initialize** the HL register pair with the **source base address** (`LXI H, 2000H`).
2. **Initialize** the DE register pair with the **destination base address** (`LXI D, 3000H`).
3. **Initialize** counter register C with the **block length N** (`MVI C, 05H`).
4. **LOOP Begin:**
   - **Step 4a:** Load data from memory[HL] into Accumulator: `MOV A, M`.
   - **Step 4b:** Store Accumulator contents into memory[DE]: `STAX D`.
   - **Step 4c:** Increment source pointer: `INX H`.
   - **Step 4d:** Increment destination pointer: `INX D`.
   - **Step 4e:** Decrement counter: `DCR C`.
   - **Step 4f:** If Zero Flag is NOT set, **jump back to Step 4a** (`JNZ LOOP`).
5. **Stop** the processor: `HLT`.

## 2.3 Structured Logic Flow — Data Exchange Algorithm

Because the 8085 has **no native memory-to-memory exchange**, the exchange must be performed via one of these techniques:

### Method 1: Three-Step via Accumulator and B Register
1. Load source $\rightarrow$ A: `MOV A, M` (or `LDAX D` for DE-pointered source).
2. Save A into B: `MOV B, A`.
3. Load destination $\rightarrow$ A: `LDAX D`.
4. Store A into source: `MOV M, A`.
5. Move B $\rightarrow$ A: `MOV A, B`.
6. Store A into destination: `STAX D`.

### Method 2: Using the `XCHG` Instruction
- `XCHG` (opcode **EBH**, 4 T-states) swaps the **HL** and **DE** register pairs *themselves*, not memory contents.
- Useful for swapping **pointers**, not for swapping memory *values* directly.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Confusing `XCHG` (which swaps register pairs HL $\leftrightarrow$ DE) with a memory-content swap. `XCHG` does **NOT** touch memory at all.

## 2.4 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table 2.1 — Master Formula & Instruction Cheat Sheet for Experiment 2**

| Quantity | Formula / Rule | Units | Typical Lab Value |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Clock Period $T$** | $T = \dfrac{1}{f_{\text{clk}}}$ | seconds (or $\mu$s) | $0.333 \ \mu$s @ 3 MHz |
| **Execution Time $t_{\text{ex}}$** | $t_{\text{ex}} = T_{\text{states}} \times T$ | seconds | depends on instruction |
| **Total Loop Time $T_{\text{loop}}$** | $T_{\text{loop}} = \sum T_{\text{states inside loop}}$ | T-states | computed below |
| **Total Program Time** | $t_{\text{program}} = T_{\text{setup}} + (N \times T_{\text{loop}}) + T_{\text{end}}$ | seconds | computed below |
| **Memory Address Range** | $0000\text{H}$ to $\text{FFFFH}$ | hex | 64 KB addressable |
| **Block Transfer Count** | $N = (\text{end address} - \text{start address}) + 1$ | bytes | 05H for this lab |
| **Address Bus Width** | $n = 16$ bits | bits | $2^{16} = 65{,}536$ locations |
| **Data Bus Width** | $m = 8$ bits | bits | 1 byte per transfer |

### 2.4.1 Loop T-State Calculation (Block Transfer)

The five instructions inside the loop sum to:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{loop}} &= T_{\text{MOV A,M}} + T_{\text{STAX D}} + T_{\text{INX H}} + T_{\text{INX D}} + T_{\text{DCR C}} + T_{\text{JNZ (taken)}} \\
&= 7 + 7 + 6 + 6 + 4 + 10 \\
&= \mathbf{40 \ \text{T-states}}
\end{aligned}
$$

For the **last iteration** when the Zero Flag is set, `JNZ` takes only **7 T-states**, so the final loop cycle consumes **37 T-states**.

### 2.4.2 Total Execution Time Formula

For a block of **N** bytes at clock frequency **$f_{\text{clk}}$**:

$$
t_{\text{program}} = \left[ T_{\text{setup}} + (N - 1) \cdot T_{\text{loop}} + T_{\text{last loop}} + T_{\text{end}} \right] \cdot \frac{1}{f_{\text{clk}}}
$$

Where:
- $T_{\text{setup}} = T_{\text{LXI H}} + T_{\text{LXI D}} + T_{\text{MVI C}} = 10 + 10 + 7 = 27$ T-states
- $T_{\text{last loop}} = 7 + 7 + 6 + 6 + 4 + 7 = 37$ T-states
- $T_{\text{end}} = T_{\text{HLT}} = 5$ T-states

**Numerical Example (N = 5, $f_{\text{clk}}$ = 3 MHz):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{program}} &= \left[ 27 + (5 - 1)(40) + 37 + 5 \right] \times \frac{1}{3 \times 10^6} \\
&= \left[ 27 + 160 + 37 + 5 \right] \times 0.333 \ \mu\text{s} \\
&= 229 \times 0.333 \ \mu\text{s} \\
&\approx \mathbf{76.3 \ \mu s}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Block data transfer between memory locations is the **foundational operation** underlying:

- **DMA (Direct Memory Access) controllers** — perform the same conceptual copy between buffers.
- **Memory-to-memory `MOVSB` instruction** in x86 (the 8086+ successor) — descended directly from this 8085 pattern.
- **Array initialization** in embedded C — a compiler ultimately emits a similar loop.
- **String copy** functions (`strcpy` in C) — fundamentally a `READ-WRITE-INCREMENT` loop.
- **Embedded firmware bootloaders** — copy application code from Flash to RAM at startup.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Problem Statement (Standard KTU Experiment 2)

> **Problem:** Write an 8085 assembly language program to **transfer** a block of **5 bytes** of data stored in memory locations starting at **2000H** to memory locations starting at **3000H**. The program should then **halt**. Also, write a second program to **exchange** the contents of the two blocks.

### 3.1.1 Given Data (Sample Memory Contents)

| Source Address | Initial Data (Hex) |
| :---: | :---: |
| 2000H | 11H |
| 2001H | 22H |
| 2002H | 33H |
| 2003H | 44H |
| 2004H | 55H |

| Destination Address | Initial Data (Hex) |
| :---: | :---: |
| 3000H | AAH |
| 3001H | BBH |
| 3002H | CCH |
| 3003H | DDH |
| 3004H | EEH |

## 3.2 Complete Assembly Program — Data Transfer

### 3.2.1 Algorithm

1. Point HL pair to source (2000H).
2. Point DE pair to destination (3000H).
3. Set counter C = 05H.
4. Repeat N times: `MOV A,M` $\rightarrow$ `STAX D` $\rightarrow$ `INX H` $\rightarrow$ `INX D`.
5. Decrement C; if not zero, repeat.
6. Halt.

### 3.2.2 Assembly Language Code (with Addresses)

Assume program is loaded starting at memory location **4000H**:

| Address | Opcode (Hex) | Label | Mnemonic | Comment |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 4000H | 21 | START: | `LXI H, 2000H` | HL $\leftarrow$ source pointer |
| 4001H | 00 |  |  |  |
| 4002H | 20 |  |  |  |
| 4003H | 11 |  | `LXI D, 3000H` | DE $\leftarrow$ destination pointer |
| 4004H | 00 |  |  |  |
| 4005H | 30 |  |  |  |
| 4006H | 0E |  | `MVI C, 05H` | Counter $\leftarrow$ 5 |
| 4007H | 05 |  |  |  |
| 4008H | 7E | LOOP: | `MOV A, M` | A $\leftarrow$ M[HL] (read source) |
| 4009H | 12 |  | `STAX D` | M[DE] $\leftarrow$ A (write dest) |
| 400AH | 23 |  | `INX H` | HL $\leftarrow$ HL + 1 |
| 400BH | 13 |  | `INX D` | DE $\leftarrow$ DE + 1 |
| 400CH | 0D |  | `DCR C` | C $\leftarrow$ C - 1 |
| 400DH | C2 |  | `JNZ LOOP` | If ZF = 0, go to 4008H |
| 400EH | 08 |  |  |  |
| 400FH | 40 |  |  |  |
| 4010H | 76 |  | `HLT` | Stop processor |

### 3.2.3 Detailed Hand Execution Trace Table

| Iteration | Instruction | HL (before) | DE (before) | C (before) | A (after) | ZF | T-States |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Init | `LXI H, 2000H` | 2000H | xxxxH | xxH | xxH | 1 | 10 |
| Init | `LXI D, 3000H` | 2000H | 3000H | xxH | xxH | 1 | 10 |
| Init | `MVI C, 05H` | 2000H | 3000H | 05H | xxH | 1 | 7 |
| **1** | `MOV A, M` | 2000H | 3000H | 05H | **11H** | 1 | 7 |
| **1** | `STAX D` | 2000H | 3000H | 05H | 11H | 1 | 7 |
| **1** | `INX H` | **2001H** | 3000H | 05H | 11H | 1 | 6 |
| **1** | `INX D` | 2001H | **3001H** | 05H | 11H | 1 | 6 |
| **1** | `DCR C` | 2001H | 3001H | **04H** | 11H | 0 | 4 |
| **1** | `JNZ LOOP` | 2001H | 3001H | 04H | 11H | 0 | 10 |
| **2** | `MOV A, M` | 2001H | 3001H | 04H | **22H** | 0 | 7 |
| **2** | `STAX D` | 2001H | 3001H | 04H | 22H | 0 | 7 |
| **2** | `INX H` | **2002H** | 3001H | 04H | 22H | 0 | 6 |
| **2** | `INX D` | 2002H | **3002H** | 04H | 22H | 0 | 6 |
| **2** | `DCR C` | 2002H | 3002H | **03H** | 22H | 0 | 4 |
| **2** | `JNZ LOOP` | 2002H | 3002H | 03H | 22H | 0 | 10 |
| **3** | `MOV A, M` | 2002H | 3002H | 03H | **33H** | 0 | 7 |
| **3** | `INX D` | 2003H | **3003H** | 03H | 33H | 0 | 6 |
| **3** | `DCR C` | 2003H | 3003H | **02H** | 33H | 0 | 4 |
| **3** | `JNZ LOOP` | 2003H | 3003H | 02H | 33H | 0 | 10 |
| **4** | `MOV A, M` | 2003H | 3003H | 02H | **44H** | 0 | 7 |
| **4** | `STAX D` | 2003H | 3003H | 02H | 44H | 0 | 7 |
| **4** | `INX H` | **2004H** | 3003H | 02H | 44H | 0 | 6 |
| **4** | `INX D` | 2004H | **3004H** | 02H | 44H | 0 | 6 |
| **4** | `DCR C` | 2004H | 3004H | **01H** | 44H | 0 | 4 |
| **4** | `JNZ LOOP` | 2004H | 3004H | 01H | 44H | 0 | 10 |
| **5** | `MOV A, M` | 2004H | 3004H | 01H | **55H** | 0 | 7 |
| **5** | `STAX D` | 2004H | 3004H | 01H | 55H | 0 | 7 |
| **5** | `INX H` | **2005H** | 3004H | 01H | 55H | 0 | 6 |
| **5** | `INX D` | 2005H | **3005H** | 01H | 55H | 0 | 6 |
| **5** | `DCR C` | 2005H | 3005H | **00H** | 55H | **1** | 4 |
| **5** | `JNZ LOOP` | 2005H | 3005H | 00H | 55H | 1 | **7 (not taken)** |
| End | `HLT` | 2005H | 3005H | 00H | 55H | 1 | 5 |

### 3.2.4 Final Memory State (After Execution)

| Source Address | Contents (Before) | Contents (After) | Changed? |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 2000H | 11H | **11H** | No (transfer preserves source) |
| 2001H | 22H | **22H** | No |
| 2002H | 33H | **33H** | No |
| 2003H | 44H | **44H** | No |
| 2004H | 55H | **55H** | No |

| Destination Address | Contents (Before) | Contents (After) | Changed? |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 3000H | AAH | **11H** | Yes (overwritten) |
| 3001H | BBH | **22H** | Yes |
| 3002H | CCH | **33H** | Yes |
| 3003H | DDH | **44H** | Yes |
| 3004H | EEH | **55H** | Yes |

## 3.3 Complete Assembly Program — Data Exchange

### 3.3.1 Algorithm (Block Exchange Using A and B as Buffers)

1. Point HL to source (2000H), DE to destination (3000H), set counter C = 05H.
2. **LOOP Begin:**
   - Load source $\rightarrow$ A: `MOV A, M`.
   - Save A $\rightarrow$ B: `MOV B, A`.
   - Load destination $\rightarrow$ A: `LDAX D`.
   - Store A $\rightarrow$ source: `MOV M, A`.
   - Move B $\rightarrow$ A: `MOV A, B`.
   - Store A $\rightarrow$ destination: `STAX D`.
   - Increment both pointers: `INX H`, `INX D`.
   - Decrement counter: `DCR C`.
   - If not zero, jump back.
3. Halt.

### 3.3.2 Assembly Code

| Address | Mnemonic | T-States |
| :---: | :--- | :---: |
| 4000H | `LXI H, 2000H` | 10 |
| 4003H | `LXI D, 3000H` | 10 |
| 4006H | `MVI C, 05H` | 7 |
| 4008H | `MOV A, M` | 7 |
| 4009H | `MOV B, A` | 4 |
| 400AH | `LDAX D` | 7 |
| 400BH | `MOV M, A` | 7 |
| 400CH | `MOV A, B` | 4 |
| 400DH | `STAX D` | 7 |
| 400EH | `INX H` | 6 |
| 400FH | `INX D` | 6 |
| 4010H | `DCR C` | 4 |
| 4011H | `JNZ 4008H` | 10/7 |
| 4014H | `HLT` | 5 |

### 3.3.3 Exchange Trace Table (First Iteration)

| Step | Instruction | M[2000H] before | M[3000H] before | A | B | M[2000H] after | M[3000H] after |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | `MOV A, M` | 11H | AAH | **11H** | xx | 11H | AAH |
| 2 | `MOV B, A` | 11H | AAH | 11H | **11H** | 11H | AAH |
| 3 | `LDAX D` | 11H | AAH | **AAH** | 11H | 11H | AAH |
| 4 | `MOV M, A` | 11H | AAH | AAH | 11H | **AAH** | AAH |
| 5 | `MOV A, B` | AAH | AAH | **11H** | 11H | AAH | AAH |
| 6 | `STAX D` | AAH | AAH | 11H | 11H | AAH | **11H** |

After the full 5-iteration loop completes, the **source block (2000H–2004H)** will contain the original destination values (**AAH, BBH, CCH, DDH, EEH**), and the **destination block (3000H–3004H)** will contain the original source values (**11H, 22H, 33H, 44H, 55H**).

### 3.3.4 Total T-States Calculation for Exchange Loop

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{loop (exchange)}} &= 7 + 4 + 7 + 7 + 4 + 7 + 6 + 6 + 4 + 10 \\
&= \mathbf{62 \ \text{T-states per iteration}}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Derived Generalization — N-Byte Block Transfer Time

For a transfer of N bytes at clock $f_{\text{clk}}$ Hz:

$$
t_{\text{program}}(N) = \frac{1}{f_{\text{clk}}} \cdot \left[ 27 + 40(N - 1) + 37 + 5 \right]
$$

Simplifying:

$$
t_{\text{program}}(N) = \frac{40N + 29}{f_{\text{clk}}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check** for $N = 5$, $f_{\text{clk}} = 3 \text{ MHz}$:
> $t = (40 \cdot 5 + 29) / (3 \times 10^6) = 229 / 3 \times 10^{-6} \approx 76.3 \ \mu s$. ✓

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — 8085 Data Path

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Accumulator A\n8-bit] -->|MOV A, M| MEM[(Memory\n2000H onwards)]
    MEM -->|STAX D| DREG[DE Register Pair\npoints to 3000H]
    A --> BREG[B Register\nBuffer]
    BREG --> A
    PC[Program Counter] -->|fetch opcode| A
    CREG[Counter Register C\n05H] -->|DCR C, JNZ| PC
    HLP[HL Register Pair\npoints to 2000H] -->|INX H| HLP
    DREG -->|INX D| DREG
    MEM --> HLP
    DREG --> MEM
```

## 4.2 Block Transfer Program Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1([Start]) --> I1[LXI H, 2000H\nLoad source pointer]
    I1 --> I2[LXI D, 3000H\nLoad dest pointer]
    I2 --> I3[MVI C, 05H\nSet counter = 5]
    I3 --> L1{MOV A, M\nRead from source}
    L1 --> L2[STAX D\nWrite to destination]
    L2 --> L3[INX H\nSource++]
    L3 --> L4[INX D\nDest++]
    L4 --> L5[DCR C\nCounter--]
    L5 --> D{ZF = 0?}
    D -->|Yes| L1
    D -->|No| E1[HLT\nStop CPU]
    E1 --> S2([End])
```

## 4.3 Block Exchange Program Flowchart (Detailed)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1([Start]) --> X1[LXI H, 2000H]
    X1 --> X2[LXI D, 3000H]
    X2 --> X3[MVI C, 05H]
    X3 --> X4[MOV A, M\nRead source]
    X4 --> X5[MOV B, A\nSave source in B]
    X5 --> X6[LDAX D\nRead destination]
    X6 --> X7[MOV M, A\nStore dest into source]
    X7 --> X8[MOV A, B\nRestore source from B]
    X8 --> X9[STAX D\nStore source into dest]
    X9 --> XA[INX H]
    XA --> XB[INX D]
    XB --> XC[DCR C]
    XC --> XD{ZF = 0?}
    XD -->|Yes| X4
    XD -->|No| XE[HLT]
    XE --> S2([End])
```

## 4.4 Memory Mapping Diagram (Before & After Transfer)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SRC_BEFORE["Memory 2000H-2004H (Source - BEFORE)"]
        S1[2000H: 11H]
        S2[2001H: 22H]
        S3[2002H: 33H]
        S4[2003H: 44H]
        S5[2004H: 55H]
    end
    subgraph DST_BEFORE["Memory 3000H-3004H (Destination - BEFORE)"]
        D1[3000H: AAH]
        D2[3001H: BBH]
        D3[3002H: CCH]
        D4[3003H: DDH]
        D5[3004H: EEH]
    end
    S1 -.->|transfer| D1
    S2 -.->|transfer| D2
    S3 -.->|transfer| D3
    S4 -.->|transfer| D4
    S5 -.->|transfer| D5
    subgraph SRC_AFTER["Source AFTER (unchanged)"]
        SA1[2000H: 11H]
        SA2[2001H: 22H]
        SA3[2002H: 33H]
        SA4[2003H: 44H]
        SA5[2004H: 55H]
    end
    subgraph DST_AFTER["Destination AFTER (overwritten)"]
        DA1[3000H: 11H]
        DA2[3001H: 22H]
        DA3[3002H: 33H]
        DA4[3003H: 44H]
        DA5[3004H: 55H]
    end
```
