# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
> **Q1.** Differentiate between data **transfer** and data **exchange** operations in the 8085 microprocessor. Can the 8085 perform both directly? Justify.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - **Data transfer** copies the contents of a source location into a destination location; the source value is **preserved** (1 mark).
> - **Data exchange** swaps the contents of two locations; **both** source and destination are modified (1 mark).
> - The 8085 has **no direct memory-to-memory** transfer or exchange instruction. All such operations must be performed using the **Accumulator (A)** as an intermediary, often with the **B register** as a secondary buffer for exchanges (1 mark).

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**
> **Q2.** Calculate the **total execution time** of an 8085 program that transfers a block of 10 bytes from memory location 4000H to 5000H, assuming a clock frequency of 2 MHz. Show all steps.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - Setup T-states: $T_{\text{setup}} = 10 + 10 + 7 = 27$ (1 mark)
> - Loop T-states (transfer): $T_{\text{loop}} = 7 + 7 + 6 + 6 + 4 + 10 = 40$ per iteration (1 mark)
> - Total T-states $= 27 + (10-1)(40) + 37 + 5 = 429$ T-states
> - $t = 429 / (2 \times 10^6) = 214.5 \ \mu s$ (1 mark)

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 2, ESE Pattern]**
> **Question A (14 Marks):**
> **(a)** [7 marks] Write an 8085 assembly language program to **transfer** a block of **8 bytes** of data from memory location **2500H** to memory location **3500H**. Draw the **flowchart** and provide the **complete hand-execution trace** for the first two iterations.
>
> **(b)** [7 marks] If the 8085 clock frequency is **2.5 MHz**, calculate the **total execution time** of the program. Also explain why the last iteration takes fewer T-states than the others.

> **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**
> **(a)** [7 marks] Write an 8085 ALP to **exchange** the contents of two memory blocks, each of length **6 bytes**, starting at **2100H** and **4100H** respectively. List the registers used and their roles.
>
> **(b)** [7 marks] Compare the `XCHG` instruction with the manual exchange technique. Why is `XCHG` alone **insufficient** for memory-content exchange? Provide a memory map showing the state **before and after** the exchange.

---

### 5.2.1 Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) Solution [7 marks]

**Flowchart** (1 mark) — See Section 4.2.

**Assembly Program** (3 marks):

| Address | Mnemonic | Comment |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 4000H | `LXI H, 2500H` | HL $\leftarrow$ source pointer |
| 4003H | `LXI D, 3500H` | DE $\leftarrow$ destination pointer |
| 4006H | `MVI C, 08H` | Counter $\leftarrow$ 8 |
| 4008H | `MOV A, M` | Read source |
| 4009H | `STAX D` | Write destination |
| 400AH | `INX H` | Source pointer++ |
| 400BH | `INX D` | Destination pointer++ |
| 400CH | `DCR C` | Counter-- |
| 400DH | `JNZ 4008H` | Loop if not zero |
| 4010H | `HLT` | Stop |

**Hand Trace — First Two Iterations** (3 marks):

| Iteration | HL | DE | C | A | Memory[DE] after |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Start | 2500H | 3500H | 08H | xxH | (unchanged) |
| Iter 1 | 2501H | 3501H | 07H | M[2500H] | M[3500H] updated |
| Iter 2 | 2502H | 3502H | 06H | M[2501H] | M[3501H] updated |

#### Part (b) Solution [7 marks]

**Total T-states formula** (2 marks):
- $T_{\text{setup}} = 27$ T-states
- $T_{\text{loop}} = 40$ T-states (per iteration)
- $T_{\text{last loop}} = 37$ T-states (JNZ not taken)
- $T_{\text{end}} = 5$ T-states (HLT)
- Total $= 27 + (8 - 1)(40) + 37 + 5 = 27 + 280 + 37 + 5 = 349$ T-states (1 mark)

**Execution time** (2 marks):
- $T = 1 / f = 1 / (2.5 \times 10^6) = 0.4 \ \mu s$
- $t_{\text{program}} = 349 \times 0.4 \ \mu s = \mathbf{139.6 \ \mu s}$ (1 mark)

**Why last iteration is shorter** (2 marks):
- In all iterations except the last, the Zero Flag is reset (C $\neq$ 0), so `JNZ` performs a jump. The 8085 documentation specifies that **a taken conditional jump consumes 10 T-states** (2 machine cycles: opcode fetch + memory read of jump address). (1 mark)
- On the final iteration, after `DCR C` sets C = 0, the Zero Flag becomes 1, and `JNZ` **does not take the branch**, consuming only **7 T-states** (1 machine cycle: opcode fetch only, no memory read of the unused address). (1 mark)

### 5.2.2 Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) Solution [7 marks]

**Assembly Program** (5 marks):

| Address | Mnemonic | Comment |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 4000H | `LXI H, 2100H` | HL $\leftarrow$ source |
| 4003H | `LXI D, 4100H` | DE $\leftarrow$ destination |
| 4006H | `MVI C, 06H` | Counter $\leftarrow$ 6 |
| 4008H | `MOV A, M` | A $\leftarrow$ source |
| 4009H | `MOV B, A` | B $\leftarrow$ A (buffer) |
| 400AH | `LDAX D` | A $\leftarrow$ destination |
| 400BH | `MOV M, A` | M[HL] $\leftarrow$ A |
| 400CH | `MOV A, B` | A $\leftarrow$ original source |
| 400DH | `STAX D` | M[DE] $\leftarrow$ A |
| 400EH | `INX H` | source++ |
| 400FH | `INX D` | dest++ |
| 4010H | `DCR C` | counter-- |
| 4011H | `JNZ 4008H` | loop |
| 4014H | `HLT` | stop |

**Registers Used & Roles** (2 marks):
- **HL** — pointer to source block (1 mark)
- **DE** — pointer to destination block
- **A** — primary accumulator for data movement
- **B** — secondary buffer to retain the source value during exchange
- **C** — loop counter

#### Part (b) Solution [7 marks]

**Comparison Table** (4 marks):

| Feature | `XCHG` Instruction | Manual Block Exchange |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Opcode | EBH | Multiple instructions |
| T-states | 4 | 62 per byte |
| Operands | HL and DE register pairs only | Any memory locations |
| Effect | Swaps pointer values in HL $\leftrightarrow$ DE | Swaps actual memory data |
| Memory modification | **None** | **Both blocks modified** |
| Use case | Pointer swapping for indirect addressing | Block content exchange |

**Why `XCHG` is insufficient** (2 marks):
- `XCHG` only swaps the **16-bit pointer values** held inside the HL and DE register pairs (1 mark).
- It does **not access the memory** locations those pointers address; therefore, the actual data stored at 2100H and 4100H remains unchanged (1 mark).

**Memory Map (Before vs. After)** (1 mark): Same structure as Section 4.4, but with both blocks fully swapped.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Typically Lose Marks in This Experiment:**
>
> 1. **Confusing `MOV M, A` with `STAX D`** (–1 to –2 marks): `MOV M, A` stores A at memory[HL], while `STAX D` stores A at memory[DE]. Mixing them up produces wrong memory mappings.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to use `INX D`** (–1 mark): The destination pointer will not advance, causing all 5 bytes to overwrite the same location (3000H).
>
> 3. **Using `DCR B` or `DCR D` for the counter** (–1 to –2 marks): The counter must be a **single 8-bit register** (B, C, D, E, H, or L). `DCR D` decrements only the lower 8 bits of DE without affecting the upper 8 bits and is rarely the intended operation.
>
> 4. **T-state miscalculation for the last `JNZ`** (–1 mark): The last `JNZ` takes **7 T-states**, not 10. Total T-state calculations must reflect this.
>
> 5. **Confusing `JNZ` with `JNC`, `JZP`, or `JC`** (–1 mark): Only `JNZ` (Jump if Not Zero) correctly tests the Zero Flag set by `DCR C`. Using the wrong conditional jump will cause the loop to terminate prematurely or never.
>
> 6. **Forgetting to write `HLT`** (–1 mark): Without `HLT`, the 8085 will execute garbage opcodes following the program, producing erratic lab-kit behavior.
>
> 7. **Wrong byte-count for `LXI` and `JNZ`** (–1 mark): Both are **3-byte** instructions. The opcode alone is not sufficient — the two address bytes must be included in the manual hand-assembly address table.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Data Transfer/Exchange using 8085**
>
> - **Core 8085 limitation:** No direct memory-to-memory data movement. Accumulator (A) is the **mandatory intermediary**.
> - **Transfer = Copy; Exchange = Swap.** Source is preserved in transfer; both blocks change in exchange.
> - **HL pair** traditionally points to the source; **DE pair** to the destination; **C** holds the count.
> - **Loop body** (transfer): `MOV A,M` $\rightarrow$ `STAX D` $\rightarrow$ `INX H` $\rightarrow$ `INX D` $\rightarrow$ `DCR C` $\rightarrow$ `JNZ`.
> - **Loop body** (exchange): adds `MOV B, A` (buffer) and `LDAX D` (read destination) steps.
> - **`XCHG`** swaps HL and DE **register pairs**, not memory contents — useless for block exchange.
> - **T-states** of key instructions: `LXI`=10, `MVI`=7, `MOV`=4 or 7, `STAX/LDAX`=7, `INX`=6, `DCR`=4, `JNZ`=10/7, `HLT`=5.
> - **Last `JNZ` consumes 7 T-states** (not taken) — crucial for exact timing calculations.
> - **Execution time formula:** $t = (\text{Total T-states}) / f_{\text{clk}}$.
> - **Standard lab values:** Clock = 3 MHz (or 2 MHz); Source = 2000H; Destination = 3000H; Length = 05H.
> - **Always end with `HLT`** to prevent the 8085 from running off into invalid memory.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Largest/Smallest from a Series Using 8051
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGL507 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Largest/Smallest from a Series Using 8051

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module Experiment 2: Largest/Smallest from a Series Using 8051

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Series Searching Operation:** A fundamental data-processing task in 8051 assembly language programming where a contiguous block of data (numbers) stored in the internal RAM (typically in the **30H–7FH** general-purpose region) is traversed sequentially to identify either the **largest (maximum)** or **smallest (minimum)** element. The operation is implemented using the **conditional compare-jump** instruction set of the MCS-51 family, primarily the `CJNE` (Compare and Jump if Not Equal) instruction combined with the **CY (Carry) flag** of the Program Status Word (PSW).

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme PCCGL507 laboratory syllabus, this experiment is designed to reinforce the student's understanding of:

- **Internal RAM organization** of the 8051 microcontroller
- **Addressing modes** — particularly *register indirect* addressing using `@R0`/`@R1`
- **Conditional branching** via the `CJNE`, `JC`, and `JNC` instructions
- **Loop control** using the `DJNZ` (Decrement and Jump if Not Zero) instruction
- **PSW (Program Status Word) flag manipulation**, especially the **Carry Flag (CY)**

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy — The Class Topper Problem**
> Imagine you are a class teacher with 50 mark sheets piled on your desk, and you need to find the **highest scorer**.
>
> 1. You pick up the **first mark sheet** and write that number on the board as your *current best* (let's call it `MAX`).
> 2. You then pick up the **next mark sheet** and *compare* it with the value on the board.
> 3. If the new mark sheet is **higher**, you *erase* the board value and write this new, larger number.
> 4. If it is **lower or equal**, you *ignore* it and move on.
> 5. You repeat steps 2–4 until you have checked every mark sheet.
>
> The 8051 microcontroller does **exactly the same thing**, but it uses register `A` (Accumulator) instead of your eyes, internal RAM location `40H` (for example) instead of the board, and the `CJNE` instruction instead of your mental comparison.

The 8051's `CJNE` instruction is the hardware equivalent of a digital weighing scale that, after comparing two numbers, also rings a small **Carry bell (CY flag)**:
- **CY = 1** → the new number is **smaller** than the reference
- **CY = 0** → the new number is **greater than or equal to** the reference

### 1.3 The 8051 Internal RAM Map (Essential Background)

| Address Range | Function | Count |
|---------------|----------|-------|
| **00H – 1FH** | 4 Register Banks (R0–R7 × 4) | 32 bytes |
| **20H – 2FH** | Bit-Addressable Area | 16 bytes (128 bits) |
| **30H – 7FH** | General-Purpose RAM (Scratchpad) | 80 bytes |
| **80H – FFH** | Special Function Registers (SFRs) | 128 bytes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For this experiment, we will store our **series of numbers in 30H–3FH** (up to 16 numbers) and use **40H** as the destination register for the result (`MAX` or `MIN`).

### 1.4 The PSW (Program Status Word) Register

The PSW is an **8-bit SFR at address D0H** that contains the critical flag bits used in this experiment.

| Bit | Name | Address (Bit) | Function |
|-----|------|---------------|----------|
| PSW.7 | **CY** | D7H | **Carry Flag** — Set by `CJNE` when A < operand |
| PSW.6 | AC | D6H | Auxiliary Carry |
| PSW.5 | F0 | D5H | User-defined Flag 0 |
| PSW.4 | RS1 | D4H | Register Bank Select 1 |
| PSW.3 | RS0 | D3H | Register Bank Select 0 |
| PSW.2 | OV | D2H | Overflow Flag |
| PSW.1 | – | D1H | Reserved |
| PSW.0 | P | D0H | Parity Flag |

> The single most important flag for this experiment is the **CY flag (PSW.7)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The `CJNE` Instruction — The Heart of Comparison

The `CJNE` (Compare and Jump if Not Equal) instruction performs a **subtraction operation internally** (without storing the result) and sets the **Carry Flag (CY)** based on the outcome.

**Syntax variants used in this experiment:**

```
CJNE  A, direct, rel    ; Compare A with internal RAM byte
CJNE  A, #data, rel     ; Compare A with immediate data
CJNE  Rn, #data, rel    ; Compare register Rn with immediate data
CJNE  @Ri, #data, rel   ; Compare RAM content (pointed by Ri) with immediate
```

**Truth Table for `CJNE A, source, rel`:**

| Condition (Mathematical) | A vs Source | CY Flag | Jump? |
|--------------------------|-------------|---------|-------|
| A = source | Equal | 0 (Reset) | **Yes** (jumps to `rel`) |
| A > source | Greater | 0 (Reset) | **No** (falls through) |
| A < source | Smaller | 1 (Set) | **No** (falls through) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Trick for KTU Exams:** "**CY = 1 means the COMPARED value is the LOSER**" — i.e., the value in A is smaller than the operand. This is because the internal subtraction `A - source` produces a borrow (carry) when A < source.

### 2.2 Why We Use `JC` and `JNC` With `CJNE`

The complementary nature of `CJNE` and the carry-check jumps:

```
CJNE A, 40H, NEXT    ; 1. Compare A with MAX (in 40H)
JC SKIP              ; 2. If CY=1 (A < MAX), jump over the update
MOV 40H, A           ; 3. Else (A ≥ MAX), update MAX = A
SKIP: ...
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Insight:** Students often confuse the carry logic. If you want the **largest**, use `JC SKIP` (skip the update when A is smaller). If you want the **smallest**, use `JNC SKIP` (skip the update when A is larger or equal).

### 2.3 Loop Control With `DJNZ`

`DJNZ Rn, rel` (Decrement and Jump if Not Zero) is the standard 8051 loop terminator.

**Operation:**
1. Decrement the register `Rn` by 1.
2. If the result is **not zero**, jump to the target label.
3. If the result is **zero**, fall through to the next instruction.

**Why it is used here:** A single `DJNZ` instruction replaces the three-step sequence `DEC Rn → MOV A, Rn → JNZ loop`, saving both program memory and execution cycles.

### 2.4 The Complete Algorithm (Flow Logic)

**Step-by-step pseudocode for finding the LARGEST number:**

```
STEP 1: Initialize pointer R0 = 30H (start of series)
STEP 2: Initialize counter R2 = N (number of elements)
STEP 3: Load A with the first number → A = [30H]
STEP 4: Store A as initial MAX → MAX = [40H] = A
STEP 5: Increment pointer R0 → R0 = R0 + 1
STEP 6: Decrement counter R2 → R2 = R2 - 1
STEP 7: LOOP START
STEP 8:   Load next number → A = [R0]
STEP 9:   CJNE A, MAX, CHECK    ← compare
STEP 10:  SJMP NEXT             ← (equal case, no update needed)
STEP 11: CHECK: JC NEXT         ← if A < MAX, skip update
STEP 12:  MOV MAX, A            ← else update MAX
STEP 13: NEXT: INC R0           ← advance pointer
STEP 14:  DJNZ R2, LOOP         ← loop back if more numbers
STEP 15: END (infinite loop or stop here)
```

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Application |
|---------|---------------|-------------|
| **Carry Flag from CJNE** | $\text{CY} = 1 \iff A < \text{source}$ | Decides update or skip |
| **Largest update condition** | If $\text{CY} = 0$ AND $A \neq \text{MAX}$ | Update `MAX = A` |
| **Smallest update condition** | If $\text{CY} = 1$ AND $A \neq \text{MIN}$ | Update `MIN = A` |
| **Loop exit condition** | $R2 = 0$ (after `DJNZ`) | All N elements processed |
| **Memory bytes needed** | $N + 1$ bytes (data + 1 result) | Series + 1 result cell |
| **Cycles per comparison** | $3$ (CJNE) + $2$ (JC) $+ 1$ (MOV) = 6 cycles approx. | Timing estimation |
| **Internal RAM usable** | $30\text{H}$ to $7\text{FH}$ | **80 bytes** of user RAM |
| **8051 Clock** | $11.0592 \text{ MHz}$ (standard KTU kit) | $1 \text{ machine cycle} = 12 \text{ oscillator periods} = 1.085\,\mu s$ |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> This seemingly simple algorithm is the **kernel** of many real-time embedded systems:
> - **Peak voltage detection** in a digital oscilloscope
> - **Maximum temperature alarm** in a furnace controller (using ADC + 8051)
> - **Sensor outlier rejection** in IoT edge devices
> - **Student grading systems** and **statistical analysis** in industrial data loggers
> - **Voting/priority arbitration** in multi-sensor fusion systems
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Full Annotated Assembly Program — Find LARGEST

```assembly
;==========================================================================
; PROGRAM : 8051 Assembly program to find the LARGEST number in a series
; DATA    : Series of 5 unsigned bytes stored at internal RAM 30H–34H
; RESULT  : Largest number stored at internal RAM 40H
; TOOL    : Keil µVision / EdSim51 / 8051 IDE
;==========================================================================
        ORG     0000H            ; Reset vector
        LJMP    MAIN             ; Jump to main program

        ORG     0030H            ; Main program starts here
MAIN:
        MOV     R0, #30H         ; R0 -> pointer to start of series
        MOV     R2, #05H         ; R2 = count of numbers (N = 5)
        MOV     A, @R0           ; A = first element [30H]
        MOV     40H, A           ; MAX = first element (initial guess)
        INC     R0               ; R0 -> next element [31H]
        DEC     R2               ; R2 = N-1 = 4 (one element already loaded)

LOOP:
        MOV     A, @R0           ; A = current element
        CJNE    A, 40H, CHECK    ; Compare A with MAX; jump if A != MAX
        SJMP    NEXT             ; (equal case) skip update
CHECK:
        JC      NEXT             ; If A < MAX (CY=1), skip update
        MOV     40H, A           ; Else (A > MAX), update MAX = A
NEXT:
        INC     R0               ; Move pointer to next element
        DJNZ    R2, LOOP         ; Decrement counter; loop if not zero

STOP:   SJMP    STOP             ; Infinite loop at end (program halt)
        END
```

### 3.2 Full Annotated Assembly Program — Find SMALLEST

The smallest program is **structurally identical** to the largest program; **only the carry-check logic is inverted**.

```assembly
;==========================================================================
; PROGRAM : 8051 Assembly program to find the SMALLEST number in a series
; DATA    : Series of 5 unsigned bytes stored at internal RAM 30H–34H
; RESULT  : Smallest number stored at internal RAM 41H
;==========================================================================
        ORG     0000H
        LJMP    MAIN
        ORG     0030H
MAIN:
        MOV     R0, #30H         ; Pointer to start
        MOV     R2, #05H         ; Count = 5
        MOV     A, @R0           ; Load first element
        MOV     41H, A           ; MIN = first element
        INC     R0
        DEC     R2

LOOP:
        MOV     A, @R0
        CJNE    A, 41H, CHECK
        SJMP    NEXT
CHECK:
        JNC     NEXT             ; If A >= MIN (CY=0), skip update
        MOV     41H, A           ; Else (A < MIN), update MIN = A
NEXT:
        INC     R0
        DJNZ    R2, LOOP
STOP:   SJMP    STOP
        END
```

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Trace Table (Dry Run) — Largest Program

**Initial data:** `30H = 45H, 31H = 78H, 32H = 12H, 33H = 95H, 34H = 33H`

| Iteration | R0 (ptr) | R2 (cnt) | A loaded | MAX (40H) before | CJNE outcome | CY | JC taken? | MAX (40H) after |
|-----------|----------|----------|----------|-------------------|--------------|-----|-----------|-----------------|
| Init | 30H | 05H | 45H | — | — | — | — | 45H |
| Pre-loop | 31H | 04H | — | — | — | — | — | 45H |
| **1** | 31H | 04H | 78H | 45H | 78H ≠ 45H | 0 | No | **78H** |
| **2** | 32H | 03H | 12H | 78H | 12H ≠ 78H | 1 | **Yes** | 78H |
| **3** | 33H | 02H | 95H | 78H | 95H ≠ 78H | 0 | No | **95H** |
| **4** | 34H | 01H | 33H | 95H | 33H ≠ 95H | 1 | **Yes** | 95H |
| End | — | 00H | — | 95H | — | — | — | **95H** |

**Final result:** $\text{MAX} = 95\text{H}$ (which is 149 in decimal — correctly the largest of {69, 120, 18, 149, 51}).

### 3.4 Step-by-Step Trace Table — Smallest Program

**Same data:** `30H = 45H, 31H = 78H, 32H = 12H, 33H = 95H, 34H = 33H`

| Iteration | A loaded | MIN (41H) before | CJNE outcome | CY | JNC taken? | MIN (41H) after |
|-----------|----------|-------------------|--------------|-----|-----------|-----------------|
| Init | 45H | — | — | — | — | 45H |
| **1** | 78H | 45H | ≠ | 0 | **Yes** | 45H |
| **2** | 12H | 45H | ≠ | 1 | No | **12H** |
| **3** | 95H | 12H | ≠ | 0 | **Yes** | 12H |
| **4** | 33H | 12H | ≠ | 0 | **Yes** | 12H |

**Final result:** $\text{MIN} = 12\text{H}$ (which is 18 in decimal — correctly the smallest).

### 3.5 Program Memory (ROM) Footprint Calculation

$$ \text{Bytes used} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (\text{opcode size of instruction } i) $$

For the LARGEST program, the breakdown is:

$$ \text{ROM} = 3 + 3 + 2 + 1 + 3 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 3 + 2 + 2 + 3 + 1 + 1 + 2 + 2 = 31 \text{ bytes} $$

This is well within the **4 KB on-chip ROM** of the standard 8051 (addresses `0000H` to `0FFFH`).

### 3.6 Execution Time Calculation

Using an **11.0592 MHz** crystal oscillator, one machine cycle = **12 / 11.0592 MHz = 1.085 µs**.

$$ T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{init}} + (N - 1) \times T_{\text{compare}} + T_{\text{final loop}} $$

For $N = 5$ numbers:

$$ T_{\text{total}} = 11\,\mu s + 4 \times 9.765\,\mu s + 2.17\,\mu s \approx 52.23\,\mu s $$

> [!NOTE]
> This is **imperceptibly fast** for a human — proof of why the 8051 is a real-time embedded controller, not a desktop CPU.

### 3.7 Analytical Decision Matrix — Largest vs. Smallest

| Parameter | Largest Program | Smallest Program |
|-----------|-----------------|------------------|
| Initial value source | First element | First element |
| Result register | `40H` | `41H` |
| Update condition | `A > MAX` (CY = 0) | `A < MIN` (CY = 1) |
| Skip condition | `JC NEXT` | `JNC NEXT` |
| Number of `CJNE` | $N$ | $N$ |
| Number of `MOV 40H/41H, A` | 2 (init + update) | 2 (init + update) |
| Total ROM (approx.) | 31 bytes | 31 bytes |
| Sensitivity to data type | Unsigned 8-bit | Unsigned 8-bit |
| Signed-data variant | Use `JB ACC.7, X` | Use `JB ACC.7, X` |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — LARGEST Number Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start1([START]) --> init1[Initialize R0 to 30H]
    init1 --> init2[Initialize R2 to N]
    init2 --> load1[Load first element into A]
    load1 --> max1[Store A into 40H as MAX]
    max1 --> inc1[Increment R0]
    inc1 --> dec1[Decrement R2]
    dec1 --> loopCheck{R2 = 0?}
    loopCheck -- No --> loopBody[Load next element into A]
    loopBody --> cjne1[CJNE A against 40H]
    cjne1 --> cjneEq{Equal?}
    cjneEq -- Yes --> skipA[Skip update]
    cjneEq -- No --> carryCheck{CY = 1?}
    carryCheck -- Yes --> skipA
    carryCheck -- No --> updateMax[Update 40H with A]
    updateMax --> skipA
    skipA --> incR0[Increment R0 pointer]
    incR0 --> djnz1[DJNZ R2 back to loopBody]
    djnz1 --> loopCheck
    loopCheck -- Yes --> halt1([STOP - MAX is in 40H])
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — SMALLEST Number Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start2([START]) --> i1[Initialize R0 to 30H]
    i1 --> i2[Initialize R2 to N]
    i2 --> l1[Load first element into A]
    l1 --> m1[Store A into 41H as MIN]
    m1 --> inc1[Increment R0]
    inc1 --> dec1[Decrement R2]
    dec1 --> lc{R2 = 0?}
    lc -- No --> lb[Load next element into A]
    lb --> cj[CJNE A against 41H]
    cj --> ce{Equal?}
    ce -- Yes --> sk[Skip update]
    ce -- No --> jncCheck{CY = 0?}
    jncCheck -- Yes --> sk
    jncCheck -- No --> up[Update 41H with A]
    up --> sk
    sk --> ir[Increment R0 pointer]
    ir --> dj[DJNZ R2 back to lb]
    dj --> lc
    lc -- Yes --> stp([STOP - MIN is in 41H])
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — 8051 Internals Used

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ROM["ROM 0000H-0FFFH\n4KB Program Memory"] --> ALU["ALU\nArithmetic Logic Unit"]
    RAM["Internal RAM\n00H-7FH\n80 bytes"] --> RegBanks["Register Banks\nR0-R7 x 4\n00H-1FH"]
    RAM --> BitArea["Bit Addressable\n20H-2FH"]
    RAM --> GPR["General Purpose\n30H-7FH"]
    PSW["PSW Register D0H\nCY, AC, OV, P flags"] --> ALU
    DPTR["DPTR\nData Pointer 16-bit"] --> ExtMem["External XDATA\n64KB max"]
    P0["Port 0 P0.0-P0.7"] --> ExtMem
    P2["Port 2 P2.0-P2.7"] --> ExtMem
    ALU --> A["Accumulator E0H\n8-bit"]
    ALU --> B["B Register F0H\n8-bit"]
    A --> CJNEUnit["CJNE Compare Logic\nSets CY if A less than source"]
    CJNEUnit --> PC["Program Counter\n16-bit"]
    PC --> ROM
    Series["User Data\n30H-34H\n5 numbers"] --> SeriesOps["Largest or Smallest\nSearch Algorithm"]
    SeriesOps --> Result["Result\n40H MAX or 41H MIN"]
```

### 4.4 Memory Map of 8051 (Visual)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SFR["SFR Area 80H-FFH (128 bytes)"]
        s1["80H P0"]
        s2["D0H PSW"]
        s3["E0H A Acc"]
        s4["F0H B Reg"]
    end
    subgraph GPR["General Purpose RAM 30H-7FH (80 bytes)"]
        g1["30H-34H Series Data"]
        g2["40H MAX Result"]
        g3["41H MIN Result"]
    end
    subgraph BIT["Bit Addressable 20H-2FH (16 bytes = 128 bits)"]
        b1["20H-2FH"]
    end
    subgraph RB["Register Banks 00H-1FH (32 bytes)"]
        r1["Bank 0 00H-07H"]
        r2["Bank 1 08H-0FH"]
        r3["Bank 2 10H-17H"]
        r4["Bank 3 18H-1FH"]
    end
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — 2 Mark / 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model] (3 Marks)**
**Explain the function of the `CJNE` instruction in the 8051. How does it affect the Carry flag?**

**Model Answer:**
The `CJNE` (Compare and Jump if Not Equal) instruction performs an internal **subtraction** between its two operands (Accumulator A and a direct, immediate, or register-indirect operand) **without altering either operand**. The result of this internal subtraction is used to set the **Carry Flag (CY)** of the PSW register:
- If `A < source` → **CY = 1** (Set)
- If `A ≥ source` → **CY = 0** (Reset)

If the two operands are **not equal**, the program counter jumps to the relative target address. If they are equal, execution falls through to the next sequential instruction.

In the largest/smallest algorithm, this single instruction is used to perform a comparison and set up the branch condition in one machine cycle.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model] (3 Marks)**
**What is the role of the `DJNZ` instruction in a series-search program? Why is it preferred over a manual `DEC` + `JNZ` pair?**

**Model Answer:**
The `DJNZ` (Decrement and Jump if Not Zero) instruction serves as a **combined counter-decrement and loop-continuation check** in a single opcode.

**Operation:** It decrements the specified register by 1; if the result is not zero, it jumps to the target label. If the result is zero, it falls through.

**Why it is preferred:**
1. It is a **single-byte/two-byte** instruction (e.g., `DJNZ R2, LOOP` is 2 bytes), whereas `DEC R2` (1 byte) + `MOV A, R2` (1 byte) + `JNZ LOOP` (2 bytes) totals **4 bytes**.
2. It reduces **program memory (ROM) consumption**, which is critical in 8051 systems with only 4 KB on-chip ROM.
3. It executes in **2 machine cycles** (≈ 2.17 µs at 11.0592 MHz), faster than the manual 3-instruction sequence.
4. It is the **canonical MCS-51 looping construct** recognized by all 8051 assemblers and emulators.

In the largest/smallest program, `DJNZ R2, LOOP` terminates the loop after exactly N-1 comparisons (since the first element is used to initialize the result).

---

### Part B — 14 Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — KTU Pattern**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

> **(a)** Write an 8051 assembly language program to find the **largest** number from a series of **10 unsigned bytes** stored in internal RAM starting at location `30H`. Store the result in location `50H`. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Trace the program execution for the data set: `30H = 55H, 31H = A3H, 32H = 1FH, 33H = 7EH`. Show the contents of the Accumulator, the MAX register, and the Carry flag at each step. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Solution to Part (a) — Program Listing (7 Marks):**

```assembly
        ORG     0000H
        LJMP    MAIN
        ORG    0030H
MAIN:
        MOV     R0, #30H         ; 1. Pointer to series start
        MOV     R2, #0AH         ; 2. Counter = 10 (0AH)
        MOV     A, @R0           ; 3. Load first element
        MOV     50H, A           ; 4. MAX = first element
        INC     R0               ; 5. Advance pointer
        DEC     R2               ; 6. Counter now = 9
LOOP:
        MOV     A, @R0           ; 7. Load next element
        CJNE    A, 50H, CHECK    ; 8. Compare A with MAX
        SJMP    NEXT
CHECK:
        JC      NEXT             ; 9. If A < MAX, skip
        MOV     50H, A           ; 10. Else update MAX
NEXT:
        INC     R0               ; 11. Advance pointer
        DJNZ    R2, LOOP         ; 12. Loop if counter not zero
STOP:
        SJMP    STOP
        END
```

**Explanation of key instructions:**

| Instruction | Bytes | Cycles | Purpose |
|-------------|-------|--------|---------|
| `MOV R0, #30H` | 3 | 2 | Initialize pointer |
| `MOV R2, #0AH` | 2 | 1 | Load count |
| `MOV A, @R0` | 1 | 1 | Load indirect |
| `CJNE A, 50H, CHECK` | 3 | 3 | Compare & branch |
| `JC NEXT` | 2 | 2 | Conditional skip |
| `MOV 50H, A` | 2 | 1 | Update result |
| `DJNZ R2, LOOP` | 2 | 2 | Loop control |

**Program size:** $31$ bytes; **Execution time per comparison:** $\approx 9.76\,\mu s$.

---

**Solution to Part (b) — Execution Trace (7 Marks):**

| Step | R0 | R2 | A (after load) | 50H (MAX) before | CJNE outcome | CY | JC? | 50H (MAX) after |
|------|----|----|----------------|------------------|--------------|-----|------|------------------|
| Init | 30H | 0AH | 55H | — | — | — | — | 55H |
| Pre | 31H | 09H | — | — | — | — | — | 55H |
| 1 | 31H | 09H | A3H | 55H | ≠ | 0 | No | **A3H** |
| 2 | 32H | 08H | 1FH | A3H | ≠ | 1 | **Yes** | A3H |
| 3 | 33H | 07H | 7EH | A3H | ≠ | 1 | **Yes** | A3H |
| 4 | 34H | 06H | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | (continues) |

**Detailed step-by-step analysis of Step 1:**
- `MOV A, @R0` loads A with the byte at address 31H → **A = A3H**.
- `CJNE A, 50H, CHECK` performs `A3H - 55H` internally.
- Since `A3H > 55H`, no borrow → **CY = 0**.
- Since `A3H ≠ 55H`, jump is taken to `CHECK`.
- At `CHECK`, `JC NEXT` is **not** taken (CY = 0).
- `MOV 50H, A` updates MAX → **50H = A3H**.

**Detailed step-by-step analysis of Step 2:**
- `MOV A, @R0` → A = 1FH.
- `CJNE A, 50H` → `1FH - A3H` → borrow required → **CY = 1**.
- Jump to `CHECK`, `JC NEXT` **taken** → skip update.
- **MAX remains A3H.**

**Final MAX for the given 4 numbers: A3H (= 163 decimal)**, which is the largest of {85, 163, 31, 126}.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — KTU Pattern (Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Alternative]**

> **(a)** Differentiate between the algorithms (and key instructions) used to find the **largest** versus the **smallest** number in a series using the 8051. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Write and explain an 8051 assembly program to find the **smallest** number from a series of **8 signed numbers** stored in internal RAM starting at `30H`. Store the result in `60H`. Mention the additional logic required to handle the sign bit. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Solution to Part (a) — Comparison Table (7 Marks):**

| Aspect | Largest Number Program | Smallest Number Program |
|--------|------------------------|--------------------------|
| **Result location** | `40H` (or `50H`) | `41H` (or `60H`) |
| **Initial value** | First element (assumed to be max) | First element (assumed to be min) |
| **Comparison condition for update** | `A > MAX` | `A < MIN` |
| **Carry flag check** | `JC NEXT` (skip on CY=1) | `JNC NEXT` (skip on CY=0) |
| **Carry meaning (CY=1)** | A is smaller → skip | A is smaller → **update** |
| **Carry meaning (CY=0)** | A is larger → **update** | A is larger → skip |
| **Equal case** | SJMP NEXT (no update) | SJMP NEXT (no update) |
| **ROM size** | Identical (~31 bytes) | Identical (~31 bytes) |
| **Loop instruction** | `DJNZ R2, LOOP` | `DJNZ R2, LOOP` |
| **Pointer instruction** | `MOV R0, #30H; INC R0` | `MOV R0, #30H; INC R0` |
| **Signed-data extension** | Use `JB ACC.7, X` for sign check | Use `JB ACC.7, X` for sign check |

**Conceptual difference:** The largest-number logic asks *"Is the new number bigger than my current best? If yes, replace."* The smallest-number logic asks *"Is the new number smaller than my current best? If yes, replace."* The CY flag of the 8051, being set only on borrow (A < source), provides this distinction for free.

---

**Solution to Part (b) — Signed Smallest Number Program (7 Marks):**

For **signed** 8-bit numbers, the MSB (bit 7) is the sign bit: `0 = positive`, `1 = negative`. A simple `CJNE` only handles magnitude comparison. To compare signed values, we must first check the sign bit of both numbers.

**Modified logic:**
- If both numbers have the same sign → use `CJNE` as before.
- If signs differ → the negative number is always smaller (for finding smallest).

**Program listing:**

```assembly
        ORG     0000H
        LJMP    MAIN
        ORG     0030H
MAIN:
        MOV     R0, #30H         ; Pointer to series
        MOV     R2, #08H         ; Counter = 8
        MOV     A, @R0           ; Load first element
        MOV     60H, A           ; MIN = first element
        INC     R0
        DEC     R2

LOOP:
        MOV     A, @R0           ; Load next element
        MOV     B, 60H           ; B = current MIN
        XRL     A, B             ; XOR A and B to test sign equality
        JB      ACC.7, SAME_SIGN ; If XOR sign bit = 1, signs differ
        SJMP    DIFF_SIGN

SAME_SIGN:
        MOV     A, @R0           ; Reload A (XOR destroyed it)
        CJNE    A, 60H, CHK
        SJMP    NX
CHK:
        JNC     NX               ; Same sign, both positive path:
        MOV     60H, A           ; If A < MIN, update
        SJMP    NX
DIFF_SIGN:
        MOV     A, @R0
        JNB     ACC.7, NX        ; If A is positive, MIN is negative, skip
        MOV     60H, A           ; Else A is negative, smaller than MIN
NX:
        INC     R0
        DJNZ    R2, LOOP
STOP:   SJMP    STOP
        END
```

**Key signed-comparison insight:** The XOR (`XRL`) of two numbers has its sign bit set if and only if the operands have opposite signs. This is the simplest hardware-friendly way to test sign equality in 8051 assembly.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing CY logic** — Writing `JNC` for largest or `JC` for smallest. This is the **#1 reason students lose marks**. Remember: "Carry Set = CompaRed is LOWER" (CSRL).
> 2. **Forgetting to handle the equal case** — The `CJNE` jumps only if NOT equal; you must still handle the equal case (usually with `SJMP NEXT` or simply falling through — depending on instruction order).
> 3. **Initializing with 00H or FFH** — A common error. If you initialize MAX with `00H`, a series containing only values > 0 will work, but if all values are 0, the result will be incorrect for *unsigned* comparison. Always initialize with the **first element** of the series.
> 4. **Off-by-one in counter** — Some students set `R2 = N` and decrement inside the loop using `DJNZ` *before* processing. Always decrement the count **after** using the first element and **before** the loop, or use a `MOV R2, #N-1` after loading the first element.
> 5. **Confusing R0/R1 pointer with R2-R7 counters** — Register banks can be reassigned; ensure RS0/RS1 in PSW point to the correct bank (default Bank 0).
> 6. **Sign-bit oversight in signed programs** — Using only `CJNE` for signed comparison produces wrong results when both numbers have different signs.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist**

- **Goal:** Identify max/min from a series of 8-bit numbers stored in 8051 internal RAM.
- **Memory layout:** Series in `30H–3FH`, MAX in `40H`, MIN in `41H` (or any other free GPR location).
- **Core instruction trio:** `CJNE` (compare) + `JC`/`JNC` (branch) + `DJNZ` (loop).
- **CY flag rule from `CJNE A, src`:** `CY = 1 ⟺ A < src`.
- **Largest algorithm:** Update when `CY = 0` (A ≥ current MAX but ≠ MAX).
- **Smallest algorithm:** Update when `CY = 1` (A < current MIN).
- **Initial value:** Always the **first element** of the series — never `00H` or `FFH`.
- **Pointer:** `R0` or `R1` (register indirect), incremented by `INC R0`.
- **Counter:** `R2`–`R7`, decremented by `DJNZ`.
- **ROM size:** ~31 bytes — fits easily in 4 KB.
- **8051 clock:** 11.0592 MHz typical, 1.085 µs per machine cycle.
- **Signed data:** Use `XRL` + `JB ACC.7` to detect sign-mismatch; fallback to `CJNE` if signs match.
- **KLU/KTU lab tip:** In EdSim51 simulator, the data memory view shows internal RAM contents in real time — use it to step through and verify the MAX/MIN update logic.
- **Common variants asked in exams:** (i) Largest only, (ii) Smallest only, (iii) Both in one program, (iv) Counting occurrences of a particular value, (v) Sorting in ascending/descending order (extension).
- **One-line answer for "What does CJNE do?":** *It subtracts the source from A internally, sets CY=1 if A<source, and jumps if A≠source — without altering either operand.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sorting (Ascending/Descending) of Data Using 8051
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGL507 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Sorting (Ascending/Descending) of Data Using 8051

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **Intel 8051 microcontroller family**, *sorting* refers to the process of reordering a finite collection of data bytes stored in the on-chip **Internal RAM (idata, 0x00 – 0x7F)** so that the elements satisfy either a monotonically increasing (**ascending**) or monotonically decreasing (**descending**) relationship. Because the 8051 is a resource-constrained Harvard-architecture 8-bit MCU with only **128 bytes of user RAM** and a small register file, the sorting routine must be written entirely in **8051 Assembly Language** using only the available **R0–R7 working registers** and **bit-addressable memory (0x20 – 0x2F)**. The standard KTU syllabus prescribes the **Bubble Sort algorithm** because it is in-place, deterministic, requires no recursion, and uses only adjacent-comparison swaps.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Students must be able to *(i) write an ALP for ascending/descending sorting of an array using the 8051 instruction set, (ii) trace register contents after each iteration, and (iii) justify the addressing mode chosen for pointer manipulation.*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Bubble Sort for 8051?**
> 1. Uses only **O(1) auxiliary space** (in-place swaps via `XCH`).
> 2. Naturally maps to **nested DJNZ loops** — the canonical 8051 looping construct.
> 3. Avoids recursion, stack overflow, and pointer-arithmetic pitfalls.
> 4. Demonstrates the use of the **`CJNE` (Compare and Jump if Not Equal)** instruction — a high-frequency KTU exam point.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Classroom Height Line"

Imagine **10 students** of varying heights standing in a random line. The class teacher (the 8051 CPU) wants them arranged shortest-to-tallest (**ascending**) in **N − 1 = 9 passes**:

- In **Pass 1**, the teacher walks from left-to-right. If the *front* student is *taller* than the *next* one, they **swap places**; otherwise they stay. By the end of Pass 1, the **tallest student** has "bubbled up" to the far right end of the line.
- In **Pass 2**, the same comparison-and-swap is performed over the *remaining* 9 positions, so the **second-tallest** settles at position 9.
- This continues. After 9 passes, the line is perfectly sorted.

The 8051's role is precisely this: it is the teacher, the `R0/R1` registers are the teacher's *eyes* (pointers), and the `XCH` instruction is the *swap* action.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Map Convention Used in This Lab:**
> | Address Range | Purpose |
> |---|---|
> | `0x00 – 0x1F` | Register Banks R0–R7 (4 banks) |
> | `0x20 – 0x2F` | Bit-addressable scratch area |
> | `0x30 – 0x3F` | General-purpose user RAM (often used for variables) |
> | `0x40 – 0x4F` | **Array storage (10 bytes for our experiment)** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of Bubble Sort (Formal)

Given an array `A[0] … A[N-1]` stored in on-chip RAM starting at base address `BASE`, the algorithm performs exactly **N − 1 passes**. During *pass i* (0-indexed), it executes **N − 1 − i** adjacent comparisons, swapping whenever the ordering condition is violated.

**Ascending sort** swaps if:

$$A[j] \;>\; A[j+1]$$

**Descending sort** swaps if:

$$A[j] \;<\; A[j+1]$$

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Algorithmic Decomposition

1. **Initialization Phase**
   - Load base pointer: `R0 ← BASE` (e.g., `0x40`).
   - Initialize outer-loop counter: `R2 ← N − 1` (e.g., `0x09` for N = 10).
2. **Outer Loop Control**
   - At the start of each pass, reset the inner pointer: `R1 ← BASE`.
   - Initialize inner-loop counter: `R3 ← N − 1`.
3. **Inner Loop — Comparison & Conditional Swap**
   - Fetch first element: `A ← @R1`.
   - Back up A into B: `B ← A`.
   - Advance pointer: `R1 ← R1 + 1`.
   - Fetch second element: `A ← @R1`.
   - Compare using `CJNE A, B, label` (sets Carry = 1 if A < B, Carry = 0 if A > B, and always jumps if A ≠ B).
   - Decision branch:
     - *Ascending:* if `A < B` (C = 1), perform swap; else skip.
     - *Descending:* if `A > B` (C = 0 and A ≠ B), perform swap; else skip.
4. **Swap Mechanism**
   - Use `XCH A, B` to swap accumulator and B register.
   - Decrement `R1` to point back to the first element, store A, increment R1, store B.
5. **Loop Termination**
   - `DJNZ R3, INNER` — repeats inner loop `N − 1` times.
   - `DJNZ R2, OUTER` — repeats outer loop `N − 1` times.
6. **Halt** — `SJMP $` (infinite loop on self to halt the program).

## 2.3 KTU Formula & Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Number of passes | $P = N - 1$ | dimensionless |
| Total comparisons (worst case) | $C_{max} = \dfrac{N(N-1)}{2}$ | dimensionless |
| Total swaps (worst case) | $S_{max} = \dfrac{N(N-1)}{2}$ | dimensionless |
| Time complexity | $T(N) = O(N^{2})$ | asymptotic |
| Space complexity | $S(N) = O(1)$ | in-place |
| Address of $i^{th}$ element | $Addr_i = BASE + i$ | hex address |
| Pointer increment | $R1_{new} = R1_{old} + 1$ | 8-bit wraparound |
| Instruction cycles per inner iteration | $T_{in} \approx 25\text{ – } 40$ | machine cycles (8051) |
| Total machine cycles (worst) | $T_{tot} = T_{in} \times \dfrac{N(N-1)}{2}$ | cycles |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Sorting routines in embedded MCUs underpin **sensor-data fusion**, **median filtering** in DSP, **lookup-table generation**, and **priority-based task scheduling** in RTOS kernels. The 8051's bubble sort is the conceptual gateway to understanding **radix sort** in modern 32-bit ARM Cortex-M controllers.

## 2.4 Why `CJNE` Instead of `SUBB`?

| Aspect | `SUBB` then `JC/JNC` | `CJNE A, B, rel` |
|---|---|---|
| Affects A | Yes (destructive) | No (preserved) |
| Sets Carry correctly | Yes | Yes |
| Built-in inequality test | No (requires separate check) | **Yes — single instruction** |
| KTU recommendation | Acceptable | **Preferred (idiomatic 8051)** |

The `CJNE` instruction sets `CY = 1` if the unsigned value of `A` is **less than** `B`, and `CY = 0` otherwise — exactly the condition we need for an ascending-order swap decision.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Fully Annotated 8051 Assembly Program (Ascending Sort, N = 10)

> The following program is the **canonical KTU lab answer**. Every instruction is annotated with its purpose and effect on registers/flags.

```assembly
; ================================================================
;  8051 ALP : ASCENDING BUBBLE SORT  (N = 10, Base = 40H)
;  Module   : PCCGL507 - Experiment 2
;  Algorithm: Bubble Sort
;  Crystal  : 11.0592 MHz (one machine cycle = 1.085 us)
; ================================================================

            ORG  0000H          ; Reset vector
            LJMP MAIN           ; Jump to main program

            ORG  0030H          ; Program begins after interrupt vectors

; ------------------- MAIN ROUTINE -------------------------------
MAIN:       MOV  R0, #40H       ; R0 = base pointer to array (unused here, but loaded for clarity)
            MOV  R2, #09H       ; R2 = outer loop counter (N-1 = 9 passes)
            CLR  00H            ; Clear bit-addressable 20H.0 (swap flag, optional)

OUTER:      MOV  R1, #40H       ; R1 = inner pointer, reset to array start
            MOV  R3, #09H       ; R3 = inner counter (N-1 = 9 comparisons/pass)

INNER:      MOV  A,  @R1        ; A = arr[i]   (first operand of compare)
            MOV  B,  A          ; B = arr[i]   (back-up, since CJNE only compares)
            INC  R1             ; R1 points to arr[i+1]
            MOV  A,  @R1        ; A = arr[i+1] (second operand)
            CJNE A,  B, CHECK   ; If A != B, jump to CHECK (CY=1 if A<B, CY=0 if A>B)
            SJMP NOSWAP         ; A == B : no swap

CHECK:      JNC  NOSWAP         ; CY=0 means A >= B : in-order, skip swap
                                ; CY=1 means A <  B : out-of-order, swap
            XCH  A,  B          ; A = arr[i] (larger), B = arr[i+1] (smaller)
            DEC  R1             ; Move R1 back to arr[i]
            MOV  @R1, A         ; arr[i]   <- larger value
            INC  R1             ; R1 back to arr[i+1]
            MOV  @R1, B         ; arr[i+1] <- smaller value
            SETB 00H            ; Set swap flag (bit 20H.0)

NOSWAP:     DJNZ R3, INNER      ; R3-- ; if R3 != 0, repeat inner loop
            DJNZ R2, OUTER      ; R2-- ; if R2 != 0, repeat outer loop
HERE:       SJMP HERE           ; Infinite self-loop : program halts

            END                  ; End of assembly source
```

## 3.2 Register Allocation Matrix (Mandatory in KTU Viva)

| Register | Width | Role | Preserved Across Loop? |
|---|---|---|---|
| `R0` | 8-bit | Auxiliary base pointer (optional) | Yes |
| `R1` | 8-bit | Inner-loop pointer to `A[j]` / `A[j+1]` | **Reset at start of each outer pass** |
| `R2` | 8-bit | Outer-loop counter (`N − 1`) | Decremented by `DJNZ` |
| `R3` | 8-bit | Inner-loop counter (`N − 1`) | Decremented by `DJNZ` |
| `A` (Acc) | 8-bit | Comparator operand 1 / swap medium | Destructively overwritten |
| `B` | 8-bit | Backup of first operand | Overwritten during swap |
| `00H` (bit 20H.0) | 1-bit | Swap-executed flag | Cleared per pass |
| `CY` (PSW.7) | 1-bit | Borrow flag from `CJNE` | Set/cleared by `CJNE` |
| `PSW` | 8-bit | Contains `CY`, `AC`, `RS0`, `RS1`, `OV`, `P`, `F0` | Modified by `DJNZ` branch |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The KTU Examiner's "Must-See" Point:** `DJNZ` does **NOT** affect the Carry flag. Only `CJNE` and `SUBB` modify `CY` in the inner loop. If a student claims "DJNZ sets CY", that is a **mark-losing** misconception.

## 3.3 Sample Execution Trace (N = 5, illustrative)

Let `arr[ ]` = `{ 5, 2, 8, 1, 4 }` stored at `0x40 – 0x44`.

**Pass 1** (R2 = 9 → 8 after `DJNZ`):

| Step | R1 | A | B | Action | Array after |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 40 | 5 | 5 | A=B → no swap | 5 2 8 1 4 |
| 2 | 41 | 2 | 5 | 2 < 5 → swap | **2 5** 8 1 4 |
| 3 | 42 | 8 | 5 | 8 > 5 → no swap | 2 5 8 1 4 |
| 4 | 43 | 1 | 8 | 1 < 8 → swap | 2 5 **1 8** 4 |
| 5 | 44 | 4 | 8 | 4 < 8 → swap | 2 5 1 **4 8** |

After Pass 1, **8** (largest) is correctly at index 4. ✓

**Pass 2** (R2 = 8 → 7):

| Step | A | B | Action | Array |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 5 | 2 | 2 < 5 → swap | **2 5** 1 4 8 |
| 2 | 1 | 5 | 1 < 5 → swap | 2 **1 5** 4 8 |
| 3 | 4 | 5 | 4 < 5 → swap | 2 1 **4 5** 8 |
| 4 | 5 | 8 | 5 < 8 → swap | 2 1 4 **5 8** ← but 8 was already fixed; harmless extra |

After 4 passes, array = `{ 1, 2, 4, 5, 8 }`. ✓

## 3.4 Derivation of the Address-Increment Formula

The 8051's internal RAM is byte-addressable. To traverse the array element-by-element, we use the indirect-addressing mode `@R1`, where `R1` itself is the address.

$$
Addr_{i+1} \;=\; Addr_i \;+\; 1 \;=\; BASE \;+\; (i+1)
$$

Since the 8051 register is 8-bit, this is naturally bounded to the range `0x00 – 0xFF`. For our array in the lower 128 bytes, no overflow occurs. The instruction `INC R1` (opcode `0x09`, 1 byte, 1 cycle) is the most efficient way to advance the pointer — far cheaper than `MOV A, R1 / ADD A, #1 / MOV R1, A`.

## 3.5 Conversion Logic — Ascending to Descending

The only required modification is **inverting the branch condition** at the `CHECK` label:

$$
\text{Ascending:  swap iff } A < B \; \Leftrightarrow \; CY = 1 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{JC SWAP}
$$

$$
\text{Descending: swap iff } A > B \; \Leftrightarrow \; CY = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{JNC SWAP}
$$

Concretely, replace `JNC NOSWAP` with `JC NOSWAP` (or simply re-label the logic). All other instructions remain identical — a frequent KTU viva trick question.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Bubble Sort Algorithm on 8051

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode[START Program] --> initPtr[Load R1 with base address 40H]
    initPtr --> initOuter[Load R2 with N minus 1 = 09H]
    initOuter --> outerLoop{Outer loop R2 equal to 0}
    outerLoop -->|Yes| haltNode[STOP via SJMP self]
    outerLoop -->|No| resetInner[Reset R1 to 40H]
    resetInner --> loadR3[Load R3 with N minus 1 = 09H]
    loadR3 --> innerLoop{Inner loop R3 equal to 0}
    innerLoop -->|Yes| decOuter[DJNZ R2 and repeat outer]
    innerLoop -->|No| loadA[Load A from M R1]
    loadA --> copyB[Copy A to B for backup]
    copyB --> incR1[Increment R1 pointer]
    incR1 --> loadA2[Load A from M R1 second element]
    loadA2 --> cjneCheck{A compared with B using CJNE}
    cjneCheck -->|A equal to B| noswapPath[No swap required]
    cjneCheck -->|A less than B Carry equals 1| swapPath[Execute XCH and store back]
    noswapPath --> decR3[DJNZ R3 and repeat inner]
    swapPath --> decR3
    decOuter --> outerLoop
    decR3 --> innerLoop
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Adaptation:** The 8051 board's physical schematic is generic across all lab experiments; rather than redraw pins, the functional interaction between the *Program Memory (ROM)*, the *CPU's ALU*, the *Internal RAM* array, and the *I/O ports* is captured below.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    romNode[Program Memory ROM 0000H to 0FFFH contains ALP] --> cpuNode
    keysNode[Keypad or DIP Switches provide unsorted input] --> cpuNode
    cpuNode[8051 CPU fetches decodes executes instructions] --> ramNode
    cpuNode --> portsNode
    ramNode[Internal RAM 40H to 49H holds 10 byte array] --> sortedOut
    portsNode[Port P1 and P2 output to LEDs or LCD] --> lcdNode
    lcdNode[16 by 2 LCD displays sorted result] --> userNode[Student verifies ordering]
    sortedOut[Sorted array in RAM 40H to 49H] --> portsNode
    cpuNode --> uartNode
    uartNode[Serial UART TXD pin] --> pcNode[PC terminal for Keil debug print]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Memory-Map Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    bank0[00H to 07H Register Bank 0 default]
    bank1[08H to 0FH Register Bank 1]
    bank2[10H to 17H Register Bank 2]
    bank3[18H to 1FH Register Bank 3]
    bitArea[20H to 2FH Bit Addressable 16 bytes equals 128 bits]
    scratch[30H to 3FH General Purpose Scratch]
    arrayBlock[40H to 49H Unsorted and Sorted Array N equals 10]
    unused[4AH to 7FH Free User RAM]
    bank0 --> bank1 --> bank2 --> bank3 --> bitArea --> scratch --> arrayBlock --> unused
```

## 4.4 Addressing-Mode Decision Matrix (Why `@R1` and not `DPTR`?)

| Mode | Example | Use Case | KTU Preference |
|---|---|---|---|
| Register direct | `MOV A, R1` | Fast data transfer | ✓ |
| Direct | `MOV A, 40H` | Single fixed address | Acceptable for constants |
| Register indirect | `MOV A, @R1` | **Pointer traversal of array** | **✓ Strongly preferred** |
| Data pointer indirect | `MOVX A, @DPTR` | External XRAM access | Not needed here |

`@R1` is chosen because the array resides in **internal RAM**, and `MOV A, @R1` is a **1-byte, 1-cycle** instruction, while `MOVX A, @DPTR` is a **1-byte, 2-cycle** instruction intended for external data memory.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Q1]**
> **Q1.** *Explain the role of the `CJNE` instruction in an 8051 bubble-sort routine. Why is it preferred over `SUBB` followed by `JC`?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
`CJNE A, B, rel` (1 byte, 2 cycles) compares the unsigned values of `A` and `B`, sets `CY = 1` if `A < B`, and `CY = 0` if `A ≥ B`, then jumps to `rel` only if they are *unequal*. In a bubble sort, the swap condition `A < B` (ascending) maps directly to a single `CJNE` plus a `JNC`/`JC` branch (½ mark). Unlike `SUBB` (1 mark), `CJNE` is **non-destructive** to `A` (½ mark) and is the canonical 8051 idiom for comparison-without-arithmetic (½ mark), making the inner loop shorter, faster, and more readable.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Q2]**
> **Q2.** *In an 8051 bubble-sort program, the array is stored from `40H` to `49H`. List the registers used and state the role of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- `R1` — inner-loop pointer to the current element (1 mark).
- `R2` — outer-loop counter (initialised to `N − 1 = 09H`) (1 mark).
- `R3` — inner-loop counter (also initialised to `09H`) (½ mark).
- `A` & `B` — comparator operands and swap medium (½ mark).

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 2]**

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Option 1*

**Q.A.** *(a)* Write an 8051 ALP to sort **10 unsigned bytes** stored in internal RAM from address `0x40` in **ascending order** using the **bubble sort algorithm**. Clearly comment each instruction. (7 marks)
*(b)* Trace the contents of registers `R1`, `R2`, `R3`, `A`, `B` and the array locations `40H – 44H` for the input array `{ 04H, 02H, 05H, 01H, 03H }` during the **first two passes**. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

The complete program with annotation is reproduced from **Section 3.1** of these notes. The marking scheme is:

| Component | Marks |
|---|---|
| Correct initialization (R1, R2, R3) | 1.5 |
| Correct use of `CJNE` for comparison | 1.5 |
| Correct branch logic (JNC NOSWAP) | 1.0 |
| Correct swap mechanism using `XCH` and `MOV @R1` | 1.5 |
| Correct `DJNZ` nesting and termination | 1.0 |
| Neat comments and indentation | 0.5 |

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

For input `{ 04H, 02H, 05H, 01H, 03H }`:

**Pass 1 trace** (R2 = 09H → 08H):

| Iter | R1 (after INC) | A | B | Decision | Array after step |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 41H | 02H | 04H | 02 < 04 → swap | 02 04 05 01 03 |
| 2 | 42H | 05H | 04H | 05 > 04 → no swap | 02 04 05 01 03 |
| 3 | 43H | 01H | 05H | 01 < 05 → swap | 02 04 01 05 03 |
| 4 | 44H | 03H | 05H | 03 < 05 → swap | 02 04 01 03 **05** |

Largest (`05H`) settles at `44H`. R2 = 08H, R3 = 05H after pass.

**Pass 2 trace** (R2 = 08H → 07H):

| Iter | A | B | Decision | Array after step |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 04H | 02H | 02 < 04 → swap | 02 04 01 03 05 |
| 2 | 01H | 04H | 01 < 04 → swap | 02 01 04 03 05 |
| 3 | 03H | 04H | 03 < 04 → swap | 02 01 03 04 05 |

Second-largest (`04H`) settles at `43H`. The final sorted array is `{ 01H, 02H, 03H, 04H, 05H }` after 4 passes (2 marks per pass + 1 mark for final interpretation + 1 mark for register table = 7).

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Option 2*

**Q.B.** *(a)* Modify the ascending bubble-sort program from Part A to perform **descending order sorting**, highlighting the **exact instructions changed** and the **reason** for the change. (7 marks)
*(b)* Calculate, for `N = 10`:
   (i) the **total number of comparisons** in the worst case,
   (ii) the **total number of swaps** in the worst case,
   (iii) the **total machine cycles** consumed by the inner loop, given that one inner iteration takes **30 machine cycles** at 11.0592 MHz crystal. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

Only **one line** changes at the `CHECK` label:

| Ascending | Descending |
|---|---|
| `JNC NOSWAP` | `JC NOSWAP` |

**Reason:** In ascending order, a swap is required when `A < B` ⇒ `CY = 1`, hence we *continue past* swap when `CY = 0` (i.e., `A ≥ B`), which is `JNC NOSWAP`. For descending, a swap is required when `A > B` ⇒ `CY = 0` (and `A ≠ B`), so we *continue past* swap only when `CY = 1` (i.e., `A < B`), which is `JC NOSWAP`. (5 marks for the table & reason + 2 marks for the surrounding logic justification.)

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**(i) Total comparisons (worst case):**

$$
C_{max} \;=\; \dfrac{N(N-1)}{2} \;=\; \dfrac{10 \times 9}{2} \;=\; 45 \;\text{comparisons}
$$

**(ii) Total swaps (worst case):**
The worst case is a strictly reverse-sorted input. Every comparison triggers a swap:

$$
S_{max} \;=\; 45 \;\text{swaps}
$$

**(iii) Total machine cycles:**

$$
T_{tot} \;=\; 45 \times 30 \;=\; 1350 \;\text{machine cycles}
$$

At 11.0592 MHz, one machine cycle = 12 / 11.0592 MHz = **1.0851 µs**, so total time:

$$
t_{sort} \;=\; 1350 \times 1.0851\,\mu s \;\approx\; 1465\,\mu s \;\approx\; 1.47\,ms
$$

(Marking: 2 + 2 + 3 = 7 marks.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting to reset `R1` at the start of each outer pass.** The inner loop must always begin at `BASE`; otherwise, after the first pass `R1` will be pointing to the end of the array, producing **silent wrong output** in the Keil simulator. **−1.5 marks**.
> 2. **Using `SUBB` but forgetting to subtract a zero offset from B** — corrupts the compare. **−1 mark**.
> 3. **Inverting the JNC/JC branch** when switching between ascending and descending. **−1 mark**.
> 4. **Decrementing `R1` *after* `INC R1` but *before* storing both swapped values** — produces an out-of-order write. **−2 marks**.
> 5. **Not declaring `END` directive** — Keil assembler will throw *A27 ERROR: END EXPECTED*. **−0.5 marks**.
> 6. **Hardcoding N as 10 without making the program general** — examiners love asking "modify for N = 20"; failure to do so = **−1.5 marks**.
> 7. **Claiming `DJNZ` sets the Carry flag** — it does not. **−0.5 mark**.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- **Algorithm chosen:** Bubble sort, because it is in-place, non-recursive, and maps cleanly to nested `DJNZ` loops on 8051.
- **Default array size:** `N = 10`, base address `0x40` (Internal RAM).
- **Three key registers:** `R1` (pointer), `R2` (outer counter = N−1), `R3` (inner counter = N−1).
- **Key instruction pair:** `CJNE A, B, CHECK` followed by `JNC NOSWAP` (ascending) or `JC NOSWAP` (descending).
- **Swap idiom:** `XCH A, B` to exchange operands, then two `MOV @R1, …` writes with `DEC R1` / `INC R1` to manage the pointer.
- **Reset invariant:** `R1` must be reloaded with `BASE` at the *start* of every outer pass.
- **Loop termination:** `DJNZ` for counting, `SJMP $` for halting.
- **Complexity:** Time `O(N²)`, Space `O(1)`.
- **No recursion, no stack, no external memory** — entirely on-chip.
- **Subtle flag fact:** `CJNE` sets `CY`; `DJNZ` does *not*; `XCH` does *not*.
- **Examiner's favourite variations:** (a) Trace a small array, (b) Convert asc → desc, (c) Generalize N, (d) Explain why `@R1` over `@DPTR`.
- **Practical extension (beyond syllabus):** Insert a swap-flag bit in `20H.0` and an early-exit check at the end of each pass to short-circuit on a sorted array, reducing best-case time to `O(N)`.
- **Debugging tip:** Always view the *Memory window* at `0x40 – 0x49` in Keil µVision's "Memory 1" tab after every pass during simulation.
- **Pins to remember:** `P1.0 – P1.7` (Port 1, used for LED output of sorted bytes in extended experiments), `XTAL1/XTAL2` (crystal), `RST` (reset).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: LED Blinking Using Arduino
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGL507 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** LED Blinking Using Arduino

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**LED Blinking Using Arduino** is a foundational embedded systems laboratory experiment that demonstrates the basic operation of a microcontroller-based digital output system. In this experiment, a Light Emitting Diode (LED) connected to a digital General Purpose Input/Output (GPIO) pin of an Arduino microcontroller board is made to turn ON and OFF at a pre-defined time interval by executing a user-written program (called a *sketch*) inside the Arduino Integrated Development Environment (IDE).

The experiment primarily validates the following microcontroller concepts as per the **KTU 2024 Scheme PCCGL507 syllabus**:

- **Digital I/O Configuration** of microcontroller ports
- **Bit Manipulation** through register-level programming abstractions
- **Instruction Cycle Timing** and software-based delay generation
- **Hardware-Software Co-design** in embedded systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This experiment is mapped to **CO2 (Apply)** of PCCGL507 and tests the student's ability to *interface basic output peripherals with a microcontroller and write, compile, and upload embedded C/C++ programs using an industry-standard toolchain.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **light switch in your room** connected to an **automatic timer**.

| Real World Object | Arduino Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Electrical switch | Digital GPIO pin (e.g., pin 13) |
| Light bulb | LED (Load) |
| Timer mechanism | `delay()` function call |
| You flipping the switch | `digitalWrite()` function call |
| Set the switch's mode (ON/OFF only) | `pinMode()` function call |

Just as a person flips a switch ON, waits some seconds, flips it OFF, and repeats — the Arduino does the same electrically. The microcontroller is essentially a **tiny, ultra-fast, programmable human** living on a small green circuit board, following your written instructions exactly, millions of times per second.

### 1.3 Key Physical and Electrical Constants

- **Arduino Uno Logic Voltage (Vcc):** **5 V DC**
- **Arduino Uno Input Voltage (recommended):** **7 V – 12 V DC**
- **Maximum DC Current per I/O pin:** **40 mA** (Absolute Maximum)
- **Recommended DC Current per I/O pin:** **20 mA**
- **Standard LED Forward Voltage (Vf):** **2 V** (Red LED typical)
- **Standard LED Forward Current (If):** **15 mA – 20 mA**
- **ATmega328P Clock Frequency:** **16 MHz**
- **Instruction Cycle Time:** **62.5 ns** (since 1 / 16 MHz = 62.5 ns)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 62.5 ns?** The ATmega328P microcontroller executes **one instruction per clock cycle** because it uses a pipelined RISC architecture. One clock period = $1/f_{CLK}$ where $f_{CLK} = 16 \text{ MHz}$.

### 1.4 Hardware Components Used

| Component | Quantity | Specification / Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Arduino Uno Board | 1 | ATmega328P microcontroller, 14 digital I/O pins |
| LED (5mm) | 1 | Red, Green, or Yellow standard indicator |
| Current Limiting Resistor | 1 | **220 Ω or 330 Ω** to limit current |
| Breadboard | 1 | For solderless prototyping |
| Jumper Wires (M-M) | 2 | For connections |
| USB Cable (Type A-B) | 1 | For power + programming interface |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architecture of Arduino Uno

The **Arduino Uno** is an open-source microcontroller development board based on the **Atmel (now Microchip) ATmega328P** 8-bit AVR RISC microcontroller. Understanding its architecture is essential for KTU lab examinations.

**Key Hardware Subsystems:**
- **Microcontroller Unit (MCU):** ATmega328P (DIP-28 package)
- **Operating Voltage:** **5 V**
- **Digital I/O Pins:** 14 (of which 6 provide PWM output)
- **Analog Input Pins:** 6 (10-bit ADC)
- **Flash Memory:** **32 KB** (0.5 KB used by bootloader)
- **SRAM:** **2 KB**
- **EEPROM:** **1 KB**
- **Crystal Oscillator:** **16 MHz**

### 2.2 Working Principle of LED Blinking — Step-by-Step Logic

The experiment works on a simple binary digital switching principle:

1. **Initialization Phase (`setup()` function):** The microcontroller configures the desired digital pin (typically **pin 13**, which has an onboard LED) as an **OUTPUT** direction. This means the pin will drive voltage out, not read it in.

2. **Execution Phase (`loop()` function):** The processor enters an infinite loop where it repeatedly:
   - Writes **HIGH (5V)** to the pin → LED turns ON
   - Waits for a defined duration (e.g., **1000 ms**)
   - Writes **LOW (0V)** to the pin → LED turns OFF
   - Waits again for the same duration
   - Repeats forever

3. **Hardware Current Path:** When pin 13 outputs HIGH → current flows from Arduino pin → through current-limiting resistor → through LED (anode to cathode) → to GND → LED illuminates. When LOW is written, the pin acts as 0V (sink), no current flows, LED is OFF.

### 2.3 Why a Current-Limiting Resistor is Mandatory

An LED is a **non-ohmic semiconductor device**. Without a resistor, the LED would draw excessive current from the Arduino pin and either:
- Burn out the LED instantly
- Damage the ATmega328P's I/O port driver transistor

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

**Formula 1: Series Current-Limiting Resistor Value**

The required resistance to safely drive an LED from a 5V Arduino pin is computed using Ohm's Law:

$$R = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{f}}{I_{f}}$$

Where:
- $V_{CC}$ = Arduino logic HIGH voltage = **5 V**
- $V_{f}$ = LED forward voltage drop (typically **2 V** for red LED)
- $I_{f}$ = Desired LED forward current (typically **15 mA = 0.015 A**)

**Formula 2: Actual Current Through the Circuit**

$$I = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{f}}{R}$$

**Formula 3: Power Dissipated by the Resistor**

$$P_{R} = I^{2} \cdot R$$

**Formula 4: Instruction Execution Time**

$$T_{inst} = \frac{1}{f_{CLK}}$$

For the ATmega328P running at 16 MHz:

$$T_{inst} = \frac{1}{16 \times 10^{6} \text{ Hz}} = 62.5 \text{ ns}$$

**Formula 5: Delay Approximation (Why it isn't exact)**

The `delay()` function uses hardware timers, not instruction counting. The millisecond delay uses **Timer0** overflow interrupts. It is approximate because the interrupt latency adds small overhead.

### 2.5 Worked Numerical Examples

**Example A: Calculate the resistance for a Red LED**

Given: $V_{CC} = 5 \text{ V}$, $V_{f} = 2 \text{ V}$, $I_{f} = 15 \text{ mA}$

$$R = \frac{5 - 2}{0.015} = \frac{3}{0.015} = 200 \text{ }\Omega$$

Since 200 Ω is a non-standard value, the closest standard **E12 series** value is **220 Ω** → widely used in KTU labs.

**Example B: Calculate actual current with 220 Ω resistor**

$$I = \frac{5 - 2}{220} = \frac{3}{220} = 0.01364 \text{ A} = 13.64 \text{ mA}$$

This is safely within the 20 mA recommended limit.

### 2.6 Engineering Utility of LED Blinking

- **Heartbeat Indicators:** Status LEDs on routers, modems, IoT devices
- **Debugging Tool:** "Blink without delay" used to verify microcontroller is alive
- **Foundation for Complex Patterns:** LED matrices, traffic light systems, Morse code transmitters
- **Embedded Training:** Standard first program in every microcontroller curriculum worldwide

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Current Through LED with 330 Ω Resistor

Let us exhaustively derive the circuit current when a **330 Ω** resistor is used (a common alternative choice in KTU labs).

**Step 1: Identify given parameters**

$$V_{CC} = 5 \text{ V}, \quad V_{f} = 2 \text{ V}, \quad R = 330 \text{ }\Omega$$

**Step 2: Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)**

In the series loop (Arduino pin → R → LED → GND), the sum of voltage drops equals source voltage:

$$V_{CC} = V_R + V_{f}$$

**Step 3: Solve for Voltage Across Resistor**

$$V_R = V_{CC} - V_{f} = 5 - 2 = 3 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4: Apply Ohm's Law to find current**

$$I = \frac{V_R}{R} = \frac{3}{330}$$

**Step 5: Numerical Evaluation**

$$I = \frac{3}{330} = 0.009091 \text{ A}$$

Converting to milliamperes:

$$I = 9.091 \text{ mA}$$

**Step 6: Compute power dissipation in resistor**

$$P_R = I^{2} \cdot R = (0.009091)^{2} \cdot 330$$

$$P_R = (8.265 \times 10^{-5}) \cdot 330$$

$$P_R = 0.02727 \text{ W} = 27.27 \text{ mW}$$

A standard **¼ W (250 mW)** resistor is more than sufficient — a common KTU examiner question.

### 3.2 Derivation: Time Period of One Blink Cycle

If the ON delay = $T_{ON}$ and OFF delay = $T_{OFF}$, the total time period of one complete blink cycle is:

$$T_{cycle} = T_{ON} + T_{OFF}$$

The **frequency of blinking** (in Hz) is:

$$f_{blink} = \frac{1}{T_{cycle}}$$

**Example:** If `delay(1000)` is used for both ON and OFF:

$$T_{cycle} = 1000 + 1000 = 2000 \text{ ms} = 2 \text{ s}$$

$$f_{blink} = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5 \text{ Hz}$$

This means the LED blinks **0.5 times per second** — i.e., completes one full cycle every 2 seconds.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Using `delay()` | Using `millis()` (Non-blocking) |
|---|---|---|
| Blocking? | Yes, CPU is halted | No, CPU remains free |
| Use Case | Simple tutorials | Real-time embedded systems |
| Timing Accuracy | Approximate (timer interrupt) | High accuracy |
| Multiple Tasks | Not possible easily | Possible (multitasking) |
| KTU Syllabus Coverage | **Primary method taught** | Mentioned in advanced part |
| Suitable for Beginners? | Yes | No |

### 3.4 Register-Level Insight (Behind the Abstraction)

When you call `digitalWrite(13, HIGH)`, the Arduino IDE actually manipulates hardware registers of the ATmega328P. This is what KTU examiners often test in viva:

- **PORTB** register controls pins 8 to 13 (Arduino digital pins)
- **DDRB** (Data Direction Register B) sets pin direction (1 = OUTPUT, 0 = INPUT)
- Pin 13 corresponds to **PB5** (bit 5 of PORTB)

**Equivalent low-level code:**

```c
DDRB |= (1 << DDB5);   // Set pin 13 (PB5) as OUTPUT
PORTB |= (1 << PORTB5); // Set pin 13 HIGH
```

### 3.5 Truth Table for LED Operation

| Pin Mode (DDRB bit 5) | Pin State (PORTB bit 5) | LED Condition |
|---|---|---|
| 0 (INPUT) | 0 (LOW) | OFF (High Impedance) |
| 0 (INPUT) | 1 (HIGH) | OFF (Internal pull-up enabled) |
| 1 (OUTPUT) | 0 (LOW) | **OFF** (Sinks current to GND) |
| 1 (OUTPUT) | 1 (HIGH) | **ON** (Sources 5V to LED) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Arduino Uno Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    PC[Personal Computer with Arduino IDE]
    USB[USB Type B Cable]
    PWR[Power Supply Section 5V Regulator]
    MCU[ATmega328P Microcontroller 16 MHz Crystal]
    DIG[14 Digital I/O Pins Port B Port C Port D]
    ADC[6 Analog Input Pins 10 bit ADC]
    LED1[External LED with 220 ohm Resistor]
    LED2[Onboard LED Pin 13]
    BOOT[Bootloader Section 0.5 KB Flash]
    MEM[32 KB Flash 2 KB SRAM 1 KB EEPROM]

    PC --> USB
    USB --> PWR
    PWR --> MCU
    MCU --> DIG
    MCU --> ADC
    MCU --> BOOT
    MCU --> MEM
    DIG --> LED1
    MCU --> LED2
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart: Program Execution Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Power On or Reset] --> B[Initialize Variables]
    B --> C[Configure Pin 13 as OUTPUT]
    C --> D{Enter Infinite loop}
    D --> E[Write HIGH to Pin 13]
    E --> F[Delay 1000 ms]
    F --> G[Write LOW to Pin 13]
    G --> H[Delay 1000 ms]
    H --> D
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Schematic: Hardware Connection Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Arduino Pin 13 DIGITAL] -->|Current path| R[220 ohm Resistor]
    R -->|Anode| L[LED Red 5mm]
    L -->|Cathode| G[Arduino GND Pin]

    style A fill:#f9c74f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style R fill:#90be6d,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style L fill:#f94144,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#577590,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Sequence Diagram: Compilation and Upload Process

```mermaid

    participant IDE as Arduino IDE
    participant Compiler as AVR GCC Compiler
    participant Board as Arduino Uno Board
    participant MCU as ATmega328P

    User->>IDE: Write Sketch in C/C++
    User->>IDE: Click Verify and Upload
    IDE->>Compiler: Compile .ino to .hex
    Compiler-->>IDE: Returns Blink.ino.hex
    IDE->>Board: Upload via USB Serial
    Board->>MCU: Reset and Enter Bootloader
    MCU->>MCU: Write Flash Memory
    MCU->>MCU: Jump to User Code Address 0x0000
    MCU-->>User: LED starts Blinking
```
