**List the operating voltage range, temperature range, and humidity range of the DHT11 sensor.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> The DHT11 sensor specifications are:
> - **Operating Voltage:** 3.3 V to 5.5 V DC
> - **Temperature Range:** 0 °C to 50 °C (with ±2 °C accuracy)
> - **Humidity Range:** 20 % to 90 % RH (with ±5 % RH accuracy)
>
> The sensor provides a digital output and uses a single-wire bidirectional protocol. It is widely used in IoT applications such as weather stations, HVAC systems, and agricultural automation.

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]
**What is the purpose of the pull-up resistor in a DHT11 interfacing circuit with a Raspberry Pi?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> The pull-up resistor (typically **4.7 kΩ to 10 kΩ** connected between the DATA line and VCC) serves the following purpose:
> 1. The DHT11's DATA line is **open-drain**, meaning the sensor can only pull the line LOW, not HIGH.
> 2. Without the pull-up, the line would **float** between logic states, causing unreliable readings.
> 3. The pull-up **passively holds the line at logic HIGH** during idle periods and after the sensor releases the line.
> 4. The Raspberry Pi's internal pull-up (~50 kΩ) is **too weak** for reliable DHT11 communication, so an **external pull-up is mandatory**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]
**(a)** Describe the single-wire communication protocol used by the DHT11 sensor to send temperature and humidity data. Include the timing diagram and the significance of the 18 ms start signal. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a complete Python program to read temperature and humidity from a DHT11 sensor connected to GPIO4 of a Raspberry Pi and display the readings every 2 seconds on the terminal. Handle the case of failed sensor reads. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

The DHT11 uses a **single-wire bidirectional, master-slave serial protocol** where the Raspberry Pi (master) initiates every transaction and the sensor (slave) responds.

**Step 1 — Idle State:**
The data line remains HIGH continuously, held by the **external 10 kΩ pull-up resistor**. This is the "bus is free" state.

**Step 2 — Master Start Signal:**
The Pi pulls the data line **LOW for at least 18 ms** (typically 18–20 ms is used in practice). This LOW pulse acts as a "wake-up call" to the sensor. After 18 ms, the Pi releases the line.

**Step 3 — Sensor Response:**
After the Pi releases the line, the DHT11 responds with:
- A **LOW pulse of 80 µs**, followed by
- A **HIGH pulse of 80 µs**.

This two-pulse handshake confirms to the Pi that the sensor is alive and ready to transmit.

**Step 4 — Data Transmission (40 bits = 5 bytes):**
The sensor transmits **40 bits** in LSB-first order:
- **Byte 1:** Humidity integer part
- **Byte 2:** Humidity decimal part
- **Byte 3:** Temperature integer part
- **Byte 4:** Temperature decimal part
- **Byte 5:** Checksum (sum of the first 4 bytes mod 256)

Each bit is encoded as:
- A **50 µs LOW pulse** (always present)
- Followed by either a **~27 µs HIGH** (logical 0) or a **~70 µs HIGH** (logical 1)

**Step 5 — End of Transmission:**
After the 40th bit, the sensor pulls the line LOW for 50 µs and then releases it. The line returns to its idle HIGH state, completing one transaction.

> [!NOTE]
> The 18 ms start signal is critical because it exceeds the sensor's wake-up threshold. A shorter pulse (say, 1 ms) would be ignored by the DHT11, resulting in `None` readings from the Python script.

#### Model Solution for (b):

The complete Python program is:

```
import Adafruit_DHT
import time

# Sensor type and GPIO configuration
SENSOR = Adafruit_DHT.DHT11
DHT_PIN = 4  # BCM pin 4 (physical pin 7)

print("=" * 40)
print(" KTU DHT11 Weather Monitor")
print(" Press CTRL+C to exit")
print("=" * 40)

try:
    while True:
        # Read humidity and temperature
        humidity, temperature = Adafruit_DHT.read(SENSOR, DHT_PIN)

        if humidity is not None and temperature is not None:
            print(f"Temperature: {temperature:0.1f} C  |  "
                  f"Humidity: {humidity:0.1f} %")
        else:
            print("[ERROR] Failed to read sensor. "
                  "Check wiring, pull-up resistor, and power supply.")

        # Respect the 1 Hz minimum sampling interval
        time.sleep(2)

except KeyboardInterrupt:
    print("\nProgram terminated by user.")
```

**Explanation of key components:**

1. **`Adafruit_DHT.read()`** — internally performs the 18 ms start signal, reads 40 bits, and validates the checksum. Returns `(None, None)` on any failure.
2. **The `if humidity is not None`** block — KTU-mandatory error handling. Without it, the script would print `None C` on the screen.
3. **`time.sleep(2)`** — DHT11 cannot be read more than once per second; 2 seconds provides a safe margin.
4. **`try/except KeyboardInterrupt`** — provides a clean exit message when the user presses `CTRL+C`.

### Question B. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]
**(a)** Explain the 40-bit data frame structure of the DHT11 sensor with a suitable example. Describe the checksum verification process in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the interfacing of a DHT11 sensor with a Raspberry Pi. Mention the role of each GPIO pin used and explain why an external pull-up resistor is necessary. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**The 40-Bit Data Frame:**

The DHT11 transmits **40 bits (5 bytes)** of data for every read operation. The structure is:

| Byte | Field | Bit Width | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Humidity Integer | 8 bits | Whole-number part of relative humidity |
| 2 | Humidity Decimal | 8 bits | Decimal part of relative humidity (always 0 for DHT11) |
| 3 | Temperature Integer | 8 bits | Whole-number part of temperature in °C |
| 4 | Temperature Decimal | 8 bits | Decimal part of temperature (always 0 for DHT11) |
| 5 | Checksum | 8 bits | Error-detection value |

**Worked Example:**

Suppose the room condition is 45 % RH and 24 °C. The DHT11 will transmit the following 5 bytes:

| Byte | Decimal Value | Binary Representation |
|---|---|---|
| Byte 1 | 45 | 0010 1101 |
| Byte 2 | 0 | 0000 0000 |
| Byte 3 | 24 | 0001 1000 |
| Byte 4 | 0 | 0000 0000 |
| Byte 5 | 69 | 0100 0101 |

**Checksum Verification Process:**

The Pi receives all 5 bytes and performs the following check:

**Step 1:** Add the first 4 bytes as unsigned 8-bit integers:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Sum} &= \text{Byte}_1 + \text{Byte}_2 + \text{Byte}_3 + \text{Byte}_4 \\
&= 45 + 0 + 24 + 0 \\
&= 69
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Take the sum modulo 256 (keep only the lower 8 bits):

$$
\text{Checksum}_{\text{computed}} = 69 \bmod 256 = 69
$$

**Step 3:** Compare with Byte 5:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Byte}_5 &= 69 \\
\text{Checksum}_{\text{computed}} &= 69 \\
\text{Byte}_5 = \text{Checksum}_{\text{computed}} &\Rightarrow \text{Data is VALID}
\end{aligned}
$$

If the computed checksum does not match Byte 5, the data is **corrupt** (likely due to electrical noise, loose wiring, or timing errors) and the entire reading is **discarded**. The library returns `(None, None)` to signal failure.

> [!NOTE]
> For the **DHT22** (the more precise cousin of DHT11), Byte 2 and Byte 4 can be non-zero, providing 0.1 °C and 0.1 % RH resolution. For DHT11, these are always 0.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Block Diagram of DHT11–Raspberry Pi Interfacing:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PI[Raspberry Pi Board]
        PWR[3.3V Output Pin 1]:::power
        GND[GND Pin 6]:::ground
        GPIO4[GPIO4 Pin 7]:::gpio
    end

    subgraph SENSOR[DHT11 Module]
        VCC[VCC]:::power
        DGND[GND]:::ground
        DATA[DATA OUT]:::gpio
    end

    PWR -->|Red Wire| VCC
    GND -->|Black Wire| DGND
    GPIO4 -->|Yellow Wire| DATA
    PWR -->|10k Resistor| DATA
```

**Role of Each Connection:**

1. **3.3 V (Pin 1) → DHT11 VCC:**
   The Raspberry Pi's 3.3 V rail powers the sensor. Although the DHT11 can accept 5 V, using 3.3 V is **safer** because it matches the Pi's GPIO logic levels and avoids the need for a logic-level converter.

2. **GND (Pin 6) → DHT11 GND:**
   A **common ground reference** is mandatory. Without it, the HIGH/LOW voltage levels on the data line would be undefined (no reference for "0 V").

3. **GPIO4 (Pin 7) → DHT11 DATA:**
   This is the **bidirectional serial data line**. The Pi uses it both as an output (to send the 18 ms start signal) and as an input (to receive the 40-bit response). BCM pin 4 is the conventional choice in the Adafruit library.

4. **10 kΩ Pull-up Resistor (VCC → DATA):**
   This is the **critical component** often forgotten by students.

**Why the External Pull-up is Necessary:**

- The DHT11's data output stage is **open-drain** (also called open-collector). The sensor contains a transistor that can only **pull the line to ground**; it has no internal circuitry to drive the line HIGH.
- During the **idle state** (when neither the Pi nor the sensor is driving the line), the line would otherwise be **electrically floating** — a high-impedance state where the voltage can be influenced by electromagnetic noise, resulting in random "phantom" pulses.
- The pull-up resistor **passively ties the line to 3.3 V** through a 10 kΩ resistor. This ensures that whenever no one is actively pulling it down, the line **deterministically reads as logic HIGH**.
- The Raspberry Pi's internal pull-up resistors are **approximately 50 kΩ**, which is **too weak** to overcome the capacitance of even short jumper wires and breadboard traces. This leads to **rounded rising edges** and **timing errors**, causing checksum mismatches.
- An **external 10 kΩ resistor** provides a stronger pull-up current (I = 3.3 V / 10 kΩ = **330 µA**), giving crisp, fast rising edges that the Pi can time accurately.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Pitfall 1:** Using 5 V instead of 3.3 V for VCC. This is allowed by the DHT11 spec but creates a 5 V data line, which **can damage the Pi's 3.3 V GPIO** if mis-wired. KTU evaluators **deduct 1 mark** for not justifying the 3.3 V choice.
> - **Pitfall 2:** Forgetting the pull-up resistor. The code will work sometimes and fail randomly, but evaluators will see this as a **wiring error** (-1 mark).
> - **Pitfall 3:** Using the physical pin number (7) instead of the BCM pin number (4) in the code. The library expects BCM numbers — using physical pin 7 will read from the wrong GPIO. **(-1 mark)**
> - **Pitfall 4:** Not including the `if humidity is not None` check. This shows poor error handling. **(-1 mark)**
> - **Pitfall 5:** Polling faster than 1 second. The DHT11 will return corrupt data. **(-1 mark)**
> - **Pitfall 6:** In the viva, saying "DHT11 uses I2C" or "uses 1-Wire protocol" — both are **wrong**. It is a **proprietary single-wire protocol**. **(-2 marks)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master These 10 Points Before the Lab Exam:**

1. **DHT11 specs:** 0–50 °C (±2 °C), 20–90 % RH (±5 %), 3.3–5.5 V, 1 Hz max sampling.
2. **Pin connections:** VCC → Pin 1 (3.3V), DATA → GPIO4 (Pin 7), GND → Pin 6.
3. **Pull-up resistor:** **10 kΩ** between VCC and DATA line — **mandatory**, not optional.
4. **Protocol:** Custom **single-wire bidirectional** (NOT I2C, NOT SPI, NOT 1-Wire, NOT UART).
5. **Start signal:** Pi pulls DATA **LOW for ≥ 18 ms** before releasing.
6. **Data frame:** **40 bits** = 8 bits Hum_int + 8 bits Hum_dec + 8 bits Temp_int + 8 bits Temp_dec + 8 bits Checksum.
7. **Checksum:** $\text{Byte}_5 = (\text{Byte}_1 + \text{Byte}_2 + \text{Byte}_3 + \text{Byte}_4) \bmod 256$.
8. **Bit encoding:** 50 µs LOW always, then **27 µs HIGH = 0**, **70 µs HIGH = 1**.
9. **Library:** `Adafruit_DHT` with `Adafruit_DHT.DHT11` and BCM pin number.
10. **Mandatory sleep:** `time.sleep(2)` between reads — DHT11 cannot do sub-second sampling.

> [!TIP]
> **Bonus Viva Points to Impress the Examiner:**
> - The DHT11 contains a **capacitive polymer** for humidity sensing and an **NTC thermistor** for temperature.
> - The "DHT" in DHT11 stands for **Digital Humidity and Temperature**.
> - The sensor's internal ADC digitizes both values, so the **microcontroller does not need its own ADC**.
> - The DHT22 (AM2302) is the higher-precision sibling: -40 to 80 °C, 0–100 % RH, ±0.5 °C accuracy.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reading Analog Data from a Sensor Using an MCP3008 ADC with Raspberry Pi
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCGL507 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Reading Analog Data from a Sensor Using an MCP3008 ADC with Raspberry Pi # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **MCP3008** is a **10-bit, 8-channel, Successive Approximation Register (SAR) Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** manufactured by Microchip Technology. It interfaces with a host controller (Raspberry Pi) through the **Serial Peripheral Interface (SPI)** bus. The experiment involves sampling a continuous analog voltage from a sensor (such as a potentiometer, LM35 temperature sensor, or photoresistor) and converting it into a discrete 10-bit digital word (0–1023) readable by the Raspberry Pi, which inherently lacks a native Analog-to-Digital Converter on its primary GPIO header. ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!IMPORTANT] > **Think of the MCP3008 as a "Digital Translator for the Raspberry Pi"** > > Imagine the **Raspberry Pi** is a strict mathematics professor who only understands integers (digital 0s and 1s). However, the outside world — light, heat, pressure — speaks in a fluid, continuous language (analog voltages). The **MCP3008** acts as an interpreter standing between them. It listens to the smooth, infinite whisper of the sensor, and at a precise moment, shouts out a number on a scale of **0 to 1023** loud enough for the Pi to understand. The sensor says, "It is 2.4 V out of 3.3 V," and the MCP3008 shouts, "**743**!" ## 1.3 Key Specifications of MCP3008 | Parameter | Value | |---|---| | Resolution | **10 bits** (1024 discrete steps) | | Number of Channels | **8 single-ended** or 4 differential inputs | | Interface Protocol | **SPI (Mode 0,0 and Mode 1,1)** | | Supply Voltage (V_DD) | **2.7 V to 5.5 V** | | Sampling Rate | **200 ksps @ 5V**, 75 ksps @ 2.7V | | Reference Voltage (V_REF) | **V_REF ≤ V_DD** | | Package | 16-pin PDIP/SOIC | > [!NOTE] > **Why is an external ADC required for Raspberry Pi?** > Unlike Arduino (which has a built-in 10-bit ADC), the Broadcom BCM283x/BCM2711 SoC of the Raspberry Pi **does NOT expose analog input pins** on its 40-pin GPIO header. The SoC internally has ADCs, but they are routed to dedicated video/display functions. Therefore, to read any analog sensor, an external ADC chip like the **MCP3008** must be interfaced via the SPI bus. ## 1.4 Pin Configuration of MCP3008 (DIP-16) | Pin | Symbol | Function | |---|---|---| | 1 | CH0 | Analog Input Channel 0 | | 2 | CH1 | Analog Input Channel 1 | | 3 | CH2 | Analog Input Channel 2 | | 4 | CH3 | Analog Input Channel 3 | | 5 | CH4 | Analog Input Channel 4 | | 6 | CH5 | Analog Input Channel 5 | | 7 | CH6 | Analog Input Channel 6 | | 8 | CH7 | Analog Input Channel 7 | | 9 | DGND | Digital Ground | | 10 | CS/SHDN | Chip Select / Shutdown (Active LOW) | | 11 | DIN | Serial Data Input (MOSI from Pi) | | 12 | DOUT | Serial Data Output (MISO to Pi) | | 13 | CLK | Serial Clock (SCLK from Pi) | | 14 | AGND | Analog Ground | | 15 | V_REF | Reference Voltage Input | | 16 | V_DD | +V Power Supply | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Successive Approximation Register (SAR) Working Principle The MCP3008 uses **SAR architecture** to perform analog-to-digital conversion in a deterministic, bit-by-bit fashion. This is essential to understand for viva questions. ### Step-by-Step Operational Logic: 1. **Sample-and-Hold:** The internal S/H capacitor charges to the unknown input voltage (V_IN) using an internal MOSFET switch. 2. **Internal DAC Comparison:** A binary value is initially guessed (e.g., 1000000000 for 10-bit half-scale). 3. **Comparator Action:** An internal high-gain comparator checks if V_IN > V_DAC (where V_DAC is the internal Digital-to-Analog conversion of the guess). 4. **Bit Decision:** If V_IN > V_DAC, the most significant bit (MSB) is kept as '1'. Otherwise, it is forced to '0'. 5. **Successive Refinement:** The SAR shifts to the next bit and repeats the comparison. 6. **10 Clock Cycles:** This process repeats for all 10 bits, requiring exactly **10 SCLK pulses** for a single conversion. 7. **Latch Output:** At the 10th clock's falling edge, the 10-bit result is latched onto the DOUT pin. > [!IMPORTANT] > **SAR Trade-off:** It uses only **one comparator and one DAC**, making it cheap and low-power. However, conversion time is fixed at exactly **10 clock cycles** — it cannot be sped up. This is why the MCP3008 is ideal for low-to-medium speed applications (e.g., temperature, light, joystick, potentiometer) but unsuitable for high-frequency audio. ## 2.2 The SPI Communication Protocol The Raspberry Pi acts as the **SPI Master**, and the MCP3008 is the **SPI Slave**. The bus uses 4 wires: | Signal | Master Side (RPi) | Slave Side (MCP3008) | Direction | |---|---|---|---| | SCLK | GPIO 11 (Pin 23) | CLK (Pin 13) | Master → Slave | | MOSI | GPIO 10 (Pin 19) | DIN (Pin 11) | Master → Slave | | MISO | GPIO 9 (Pin 21) | DOUT (Pin 12) | Slave → Master | | CS/CE | GPIO 8 (Pin 24) | CS (Pin 10) | Master → Slave | ### SPI Configuration Parameters: - **Mode:** SPI Mode 0 (CPOL = 0, CPHA = 0) — Clock idles LOW, data sampled on rising edge. - **Clock Speed:** Maximum **1 MHz** for MCP3008 (practical value: 1.35 MHz @ 5V). - **Bit Order:** MSB First. - **Bits per Word:** 8 bits per transfer (requires 2 transfers of 8 bits = 16 clocks total). ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table summarizes **every formula** a KTU student must memorize for the analog reading calculations. | # | Formula | Description | Unit / Symbol | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $V_{in} = \dfrac{ADC_{value}}{1023} \times V_{ref}$ | Convert digital reading to actual voltage | $V_{in}$ in Volts | | 2 | $\text{Resolution} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^{n}}$ | Smallest detectable voltage change (LSB) | Volts per LSB | | 3 | $n = 10, \quad 2^{10} = 1024$ | Number of quantization levels (0 to 1023) | dimensionless | | 4 | $\% \text{ Error} = \dfrac{V_{measured} - V_{theoretical}}{V_{theoretical}} \times 100$ | Quantization error calculation | percentage | | 5 | $V_{in(max)} = V_{ref}$ | Maximum measurable input voltage | Volts | | 6 | $\text{ADC}_{value} = \text{round}\left(\dfrac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \times 1023\right)$ | Reverse: voltage to digital value | dimensionless | | 7 | $\text{LSB} = 1 \text{ in decimal} = 0 \text{ in binary (lowest bit)}$ | Least Significant Bit weight | — | | 8 | $T_{conversion} = \dfrac{10}{f_{SCLK}}$ | Time for one ADC conversion | seconds | | 9 | $f_{SCLK(max)} = 3.6 \text{ MHz @ 5V}$ | Maximum SPI clock | Hz | | 10 | $R_{pot} = \dfrac{ADC_{value}}{1023} \times R_{total}$ | Resistance from potentiometer wiper | Ohms ($\Omega$) | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility In modern embedded systems, the MCP3008 + Raspberry Pi combination is used in: - **IoT Weather Stations** (LM35, DHT11 analog variants) - **Smart Agriculture** (soil moisture sensors with analog output) - **Industrial Process Control** (4-20 mA current loop digitization) - **Robotics** (joystick and encoder position sensing) - **Medical Devices** (ECG/EMG signal acquisition with front-end amplification) - **Renewable Energy** (solar panel current/voltage monitoring) # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: The Core Voltage Conversion Formula The fundamental relationship between the digital output and the analog input is derived from the definition of an n-bit ADC. The MCP3008 divides the input range $[0, V_{ref}]$ into $2^{10} = 1024$ equally spaced quantization levels. ### Logical Foundation: The smallest voltage increment that the ADC can resolve (the **Least Significant Bit** value) is given by: $$ V_{LSB} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^{n}} = \frac{V_{ref}}{1024} $$ However, because digital codes range from **0 to 1023** (a total of 1023 intervals, not 1024), the practical linear mapping used in code is: $$ V_{in} = D \cdot \frac{V_{ref}}{1023} $$ where $D$ is the 10-bit digital output code. ## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example **Problem Statement:** A potentiometer is connected to **CH0** of the MCP3008. The Raspberry Pi reads a digital value of **D = 512**. Given the supply voltage is **3.3 V** (used as V_REF), calculate: 1. The actual analog input voltage. 2. The voltage resolution (LSB size). 3. The percentage of full scale. ### Solution: **Step 1 — Identify the Given Parameters** We are given: - $D = 512$ (10-bit digital output from the ADC) - $V_{ref} = 3.3 \text{ V}$ (reference voltage, typically the Pi's 3.3V rail) - $n = 10$ bits (resolution of MCP3008) **Step 2 — Calculate the Actual Input Voltage** Substitute the known values into the primary conversion formula: $$ V_{in} = \frac{D}{1023} \times V_{ref} $$ $$ V_{in} = \frac{512}{1023} \times 3.3 \text{ V} $$ Now perform the intermediate fraction: $$ \frac{512}{1023} = 0.5005 \quad \text{(approximately half-scale)} $$ Now multiply by the reference voltage: $$ V_{in} = 0.5005 \times 3.3 \text{ V} $$ $$ V_{in} \approx 1.6517 \text{ V} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Observation:** A reading of 512 corresponds to almost exactly half of the full scale, which physically corresponds to the potentiometer wiper being at the **center position**. This is an excellent **sanity check** during lab examinations. **Step 3 — Calculate the Voltage Resolution (LSB Size)** The resolution represents the smallest voltage difference the ADC can detect between two adjacent codes: $$ V_{LSB} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^{n}} = \frac{3.3 \text{ V}}{1024} $$ $$ V_{LSB} = 0.003223 \text{ V} $$ $$ V_{LSB} \approx 3.223 \text{ mV} $$ **Step 4 — Calculate the Percentage of Full Scale** The percentage of full scale tells us how much of the dynamic range is being utilized: $$ \% \text{ FS} = \left(\frac{D}{1023}\right) \times 100 $$ $$ \% \text{ FS} = \left(\frac{512}{1023}\right) \times 100 \approx 50.05\% $$ ### Final Answer Summary: | Quantity | Symbol | Value | |---|---|---| | Digital Code | $D$ | **512** | | Input Voltage | $V_{in}$ | **1.6517 V** | | Resolution (LSB) | $V_{LSB}$ | **3.223 mV** | | Percentage of Full Scale | $\% \text{ FS}$ | **50.05 %** | ## 3.3 Derivative Analytical Matrix — Multiple Worked Cases > [!IMPORTANT] > This matrix provides a **rapid-look reference** for various digital codes with **V_REF = 3.3 V**. The same procedure applies for any V_REF. | Digital Code (D) | Binary (10-bit) | $V_{in}$ (V) | $\% \text{ FS}$ | Sensor Interpretation (e.g., Pot) | |---|---|---|---|---| | **0** | 0000000000 | 0.0000 | 0.00 % | Minimum position | | **128** | 0010000000 | 0.4129 | 12.51 % | Quarter-low | | **256** | 0100000000 | 0.8258 | 25.02 % | Quarter | | **512** | 1000000000 | 1.6517 | 50.05 % | Center | | **768** | 1100000000 | 2.4774 | 75.07 % | Three-quarter | | **1023** | 1111111111 | 3.3000 | 100.00 % | Maximum position | ## 3.4 Time Domain Conversion Analysis The MCP3008 requires exactly **10 SCLK cycles** for one analog-to-digital conversion. Given an SPI clock frequency of $f_{SCLK} = 1 \text{ MHz}$: $$ T_{conversion} = \frac{10}{f_{SCLK}} $$ $$ T_{conversion} = \frac{10}{1 \times 10^{6} \text{ Hz}} = 10 \text{ \mu s} $$ This corresponds to a maximum theoretical sampling rate of: $$ f_{sample(max)} = \frac{1}{T_{conversion}} = 100 \text{ ksps} $$ The MCP3008 datasheet specifies **200 ksps at 5V**, which is achieved with a faster SPI clock. The discrepancy arises from internal settling times. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (MCP3008 ↔ Raspberry Pi) The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the complete signal chain from the physical sensor to the digital read in Python. The diagram adheres strictly to the **Mermaid Compilation Safeguards** (alphanumeric node IDs, no markdown formatting inside labels). ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Physical Sensor
Potentiometer LM35 LDR] --> A2[Analog Voltage
0V to 3.3V] A2 --> A3[MCP3008 ADC
10 bit SAR] A3 -- SPI Bus MOSI MISO SCLK CS --> A4[Raspberry Pi
SPI Master] A4 --> A5[Python Script
spidev library] A5 --> A6[Digital Value
0 to 1023] A6 --> A7[Voltage Conversion
V in equals D by 1023 times V ref] A7 --> A8[Physical Quantity
Temperature Lux Degrees] A8 --> A9[Display or Cloud
Print ThingSpeak MQTT] style A1 fill:#E6F3FF,stroke:#1F77B4 style A3 fill:#FFE6E6,stroke:#D62728 style A4 fill:#E6FFE6,stroke:#2CA02C style A5 fill:#FFF7E6,stroke:#FF7F0E style A9 fill:#F0E6FF,stroke:#9467BD ``` ## 4.2 SPI Communication Sequence Diagram The following diagram shows the **bit-level handshake** during one full 16-clock-cycle transaction between the Pi and the MCP3008. The configuration byte structure is also detailed. ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Pi as Raspberry Pi Master participant ADC as MCP3008 Slave Note over Pi,ADC: CS is pulled LOW to start conversion Pi->>ADC: SCLK 1st pulse (Start bit 1 sent on MOSI) Pi->>ADC: SCLK 2nd pulse (SGL or DIFF bit sent) Pi->>ADC: SCLK 3rd pulse (D2 channel select bit) Pi->>ADC: SCLK 4th pulse (D1 channel select bit) Pi->>ADC: SCLK 5th pulse (D0 channel select bit) Pi->>ADC: SCLK 6th pulse (Null bit 0 sent) Note over ADC: Internal SAR starts conversion ADC-->>Pi: SCLK 7th to 16th pulse (10 bit result on MISO) Pi->>Pi: Combine bits 9 to 0 into 10 bit integer Note over Pi,ADC: CS is pulled HIGH to end transaction ``` ## 4.3 Configuration Byte Bit-Map (KTU High-Yield) The **3 configuration bits** sent during the first byte (along with the start bit) determine **which channel** is read. This is a guaranteed viva question. | Bit Position (7-0) | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | **First Byte Meaning** | Start (1) | SGL/DIFF | D2 | D1 | D0 | X | X | X | | **Read CH0 Example** | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | | **Read CH1 Example** | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | | **Read CH7 Example** | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | | **Read CH0 vs CH1 Differential** | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | > [!NOTE] > **SGL/DIFF bit**: When set to **1** → Single-ended mode (channel read against AGND). When set to **0** → Pseudo-differential mode (channel pairs CH0-CH1, CH2-CH3, etc.). > **D2, D1, D0 bits**: Three-bit channel address in single-ended mode. ## 4.4 Hardware Wiring Architecture (Raspberry Pi ↔ MCP3008) ```mermaid graph TD subgraph PI[RASPBERRY PI GPIO HEADER] GPIO11["GPIO 11 Pin 23
SCLK OUTPUT"] GPIO10["GPIO 10 Pin 19
MOSI OUTPUT"] GPIO9["GPIO 9 Pin 21
MISO INPUT"] GPIO8["GPIO 8 Pin 24
CE0 CHIP SELECT"] P3V3["Pin 1
3.3V POWER"] PGND["Pin 6
GROUND"] end subgraph ADC[MCP3008 16 PIN DIP] P13["Pin 13
CLK"] P11["Pin 11
DIN"] P12["Pin 12
DOUT"] P10["Pin 10
CS"] P15["Pin 15
VREF"] P16["Pin 16
VDD"] P14["Pin 14
AGND"] P9["Pin 9
DGND"] PCH0["Pin 1
CH0 SENSOR INPUT"] end GPIO11 --- P13 GPIO10 --- P11 GPIO9 --- P12 GPIO8 --- P10 P3V3 --- P15 P3V3 --- P16 PGND --- P9 PGND --- P14 PCH0 --- SENSOR["External Sensor
Pot or LM35"] style PI fill:#E6FFE6,stroke:#2CA02C style ADC fill:#FFE6E6,stroke:#D62728 style SENSOR fill:#FFF7E6,stroke:#FF7F0E ``` ## 4.5 Programmatic Read Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD B1[Import spidev and time libraries] --> B2[Open SPI bus 0 device 0] B2 --> B3[Set SPI mode to 0] B3 --> B4[Set max clock speed to 1 MHz] B4 --> B5[Build TX array of 3 bytes] B5 --> B6{Channel
0 to 7} B6 -- Set bit 6 to 1 --> B7[Single ended mode] B6 -- Set bit 6 to 0 --> B8[Differential mode] B7 --> B9[Set channel bits in byte 1] B8 --> B9 B9 --> B10[Pull CS LOW by calling xfer2] B10 --> B11[Receive 3 bytes on MISO] B11 --> B12[Extract 10 bit value from byte 1 and 2] B12 --> B13[Mask and shift bits 9 to 0] B13 --> B14[Return decimal 0 to 1023] B14 --> B15[Convert to voltage using V by 1023 times Vref] B15 --> B16[Print or store to database] style B1 fill:#E6F3FF,stroke:#1F77B4 style B14 fill:#FFE6E6,stroke:#D62728 style B16 fill:#E6FFE6,stroke:#2CA02C ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Model Paper Pattern] **Q: Differentiate between a Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADC and a Flash ADC. Why is the MCP3008 designed as a SAR type?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **Flash ADC** uses $2^n - 1$ comparators for an n-bit conversion, providing extremely high speed (1 GSPS+) but consuming significant power and silicon area. In contrast, a **SAR ADC** uses only **one comparator and one internal DAC**, performing a **binary search** over 10 clock cycles to determine the output code. The MCP3008 is implemented as a SAR ADC because of its **low power consumption (only 5 mW typical @ 5V), small 16-pin package, and adequate 200 ksps speed** — which is more than sufficient for low-bandwidth sensor signals like temperature, light, and potentiometer readings. **(3 marks for clear contrast + justification)** ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Sample Pattern] **Q: A sensor outputs 1.65 V. The MCP3008 is supplied with V_REF = 3.3 V. Calculate the expected digital output code.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Using the conversion formula: $$ D = \text{round}\left(\frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \times 1023\right) $$ $$ D = \text{round}\left(\frac{1.65}{3.3} \times 1023\right) $$ $$ D = \text{round}(511.5) = 512 $$ The expected digital code is **512**, which represents **50% of full scale**. **(1 mark for formula, 1 mark for substitution, 1 mark for answer)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Sub-Parts) ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Module Level] **Q: With a neat block diagram, explain the interfacing of an MCP3008 10-bit ADC with a Raspberry Pi via the SPI bus. How is an analog voltage converted to a digital code? Show the configuration byte for reading CH3 in single-ended mode. (14 marks)** #### Part (a) — Hardware Interfacing and SPI Communication (7 marks) **Model Answer:** The Raspberry Pi (BCM2711 SoC) does not have built-in analog input pins. To read analog sensors, the **MCP3008** 10-bit, 8-channel ADC is connected to the Pi's **SPI bus** using the 40-pin GPIO header. **Interfacing Connections (3 marks):** | Signal | Raspberry Pi Pin | MCP3008 Pin | |---|---|---| | VCC (+3.3V) | Pin 1 | Pin 16 (V_DD), Pin 15 (V_REF) | | GND | Pin 6 | Pin 9 (DGND), Pin 14 (AGND) | | SCLK | GPIO 11 (Pin 23) | Pin 13 (CLK) | | MOSI | GPIO 10 (Pin 19) | Pin 11 (DIN) | | MISO | GPIO 9 (Pin 21) | Pin 12 (DOUT) | | CE0 | GPIO 8 (Pin 24) | Pin 10 (CS) | **SPI Protocol (2 marks):** The Pi acts as the master. For each conversion: 1. CS is pulled **LOW** to activate the chip. 2. Pi sends **3 bits of configuration** (start, SGL/DIFF, D2, D1, D0) on the first byte via MOSI. 3. MCP3008 performs SAR conversion internally during the next 2 SCLK cycles. 4. The 10-bit result is clocked out on MISO over the next 10 SCLK cycles. 5. CS is pulled **HIGH** to end the transaction. **Mode and Speed (2 marks):** SPI Mode 0 (CPOL=0, CPHA=0), MSB first, maximum clock 1 MHz at 3.3V. #### Part (b) — Configuration Byte for CH3 and Voltage Conversion (7 marks) **Model Answer:** For reading **Channel 3 (CH3) in single-ended mode**, the configuration bits are: - **Start bit** = 1 - **SGL/DIFF** = 1 (single-ended) - **D2 D1 D0** = 0 1 1 (binary for channel 3) Therefore, the first transmitted byte is: $$ \text{Byte 1} = 1\,1\,0\,1\,1\,0\,0\,0_2 = 0x68 = 104_{10} $$ The second byte is a "don't care" value (typically 0x00), and the 10-bit result is extracted from the lower 2 bits of the second byte and all 8 bits of the third byte. **Voltage Conversion Formula (3 marks):** $$ V_{in} = \frac{D}{1023} \times V_{ref} $$ If the Pi reads **D = 614** with **V_REF = 3.3 V**: $$ V_{in} = \frac{614}{1023} \times 3.3 = 1.981 \text{ V} $$ This voltage corresponds to the physical quantity sensed by the sensor (e.g., temperature in the case of an LM35, where 1.981 V = 198.1 °C... *which is physically impossible, so the sensor choice must match the application*). --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Module Level — Alternative Choice] **Q: Design and implement a Python program on Raspberry Pi to continuously read an LM35 temperature sensor via the MCP3008 ADC and print the temperature in °C every second. Explain the bit-extraction logic from the SPI response. (14 marks)** #### Part (a) — Python Program with Bit-Extraction Explanation (7 marks) **Model Answer:** ```python import spidev import time # Create SPI object spi = spidev.SpiDev() spi.open(0, 0) # Open bus 0, device 0 (CE0) spi.max_speed_hz = 1000000 # 1 MHz spi.mode = 0 # SPI Mode 0 def read_adc(channel): """Read 10-bit value from MCP3008 channel (0-7).""" assert 0 <= channel <= 7 # Build 3-byte transaction # First byte: 0x01 (start bit) # Second byte: 0x80 | (channel << 4) # Third byte: 0x00 (don't care) tx = [0x01, 0x80 | (channel << 4), 0x00] rx = spi.xfer2(tx, 5000) # 5000 = timeout in microseconds # Bit extraction: # rx[0] is null (start bit consumed) # rx[1] lower 6 bits contain B9..B4 of result # rx[2] all 8 bits contain B3..B0...wait, only B3..B0 (lower 4) result = ((rx[1] & 0x03) << 8) | rx[2] return result while True: raw = read_adc(0) voltage = (raw / 1023.0) * 3.3 temperature = voltage * 100 # LM35: 10 mV per °C print(f"Raw: {raw} | V: {voltage:.3f} V | T: {temperature:.1f} °C") time.sleep(1) ``` **Bit-Extraction Logic Explanation (3 marks):** The MCP3008 returns 3 bytes (24 SCLK cycles total). After the start bit and 3 configuration bits (consumed during the first 7 SCLK pulses), the 10-bit result appears as: - **Bits 9 down to 4** → packed in the **lower 6 bits of rx[1]**. Since we only need the top 2 bits (B9 and B8), we mask with `0x03` and shift left by 8 positions. - **Bits 3 down to 0** → contained in **rx[2]**. We use the full 8 bits because Python handles integers of arbitrary precision, so the lower 4 bits are the actual ADC result, with the upper 4 bits being "don't care" zeros (since the shift is not done in C). #### Part (b) — LM35 Sensor Physics and Error Analysis (7 marks) **Model Answer:** **LM35 Sensor Characteristics (3 marks):** The LM35 is a precision centigrade temperature sensor with a linear scale factor of **+10 mV/°C**. It outputs: - 0 V at 0 °C - 0.25 V at 25 °C - 1.50 V at 150 °C (max rated) Conversion equation: $$ T(^{\circ}C) = V_{out}(\text{in volts}) \times 100 $$ **Complete System Equation (2 marks):** $$ T = \left(\frac{D}{1023} \times 3.3\right) \times 100 $$ **Error Analysis (2 marks):** Sources of error include: 1. **Quantization Error**: $\pm \frac{1}{2} \text{ LSB} = \pm 1.65 \text{ mV}$ → $\pm 0.165 \text{ °C}$. 2. **V_REF Drift**: Pi's 3.3V rail has $\pm 5\%$ tolerance → up to $0.3 \text{ °C}$ error. 3. **Self-Heating of LM35**: 0.08 °C in still air. 4. **SPI Clock Jitter**: Affects settling time of internal S/H capacitor. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks** > > 1. **Wrong Masking**: Students often write `result = (rx[1] & 0x0F) << 8 | rx[2]` instead of `(rx[1] & 0x03) << 8`. The MCP3008 only puts **2 bits** in the second byte and **8 bits** in the third, NOT 4 bits. Losing **2 marks** here. > 2. **Forgetting SPI Activation**: Failing to enable SPI via `raspi-config → Interfacing → SPI` is a guaranteed **1-mark deduction** in code demonstrations. > 3. **Confusing AGND/DGND**: Some students tie AGND to digital ground directly. Best practice uses a single-point ground or ferrite bead. Examiners **may** ask this in viva; be prepared. > 4. **Not Pulling CS High**: Leaving CS floating can cause noise. Always use `spi.xfer2()` which auto-controls CS. > 5. **Using V_REF = 5V with 3.3V Pi Logic**: The Pi GPIO is **NOT 5V tolerant**! Supplying MCP3008 with 5V V_DD while reading DOUT into a 3.3V Pi pin can damage the SoC. **Always use 3.3V supply for both V_DD and V_REF.** Examiners specifically test this understanding. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Memorize Before Exam** - [x] **MCP3008 is 10-bit, 8-channel, SAR-type ADC** with SPI interface. **(Most asked definition)** - [x] **Raspberry Pi has NO built-in ADC** on its GPIO header — external ADC like MCP3008 is mandatory. - [x] **SPI uses 4 wires**: SCLK (Pin 23), MOSI (Pin 19), MISO (Pin 21), CE0 (Pin 24). - [x] **MCP3008 uses SPI Mode 0, MSB First, max 1 MHz** at 3.3V. - [x] **Voltage formula**: $V_{in} = \dfrac{D}{1023} \times V_{ref}$ **(Use 1023, NOT 1024)** - [x] **Resolution formula**: $V_{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^{10}} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{1024}$ - [x] **Configuration byte for single-ended CHx**: `0x80 | (channel << 4)` for second byte, with `0x01` as first byte. - [x] **Bit extraction**: `result = ((rx[1] & 0x03) << 8) | rx[2]` — this is the **most critical line of code**. - [x] **AGND and DGND** must be connected to the same ground potential (single-point star ground preferred). - [x] **V_REF = V_DD = 3.3V** when interfacing with Raspberry Pi to avoid logic-level damage. - [x] **SAR conversion takes 10 SCLK cycles**; with 1 MHz clock, that is **10 µs** per sample. - [x] **LM35 scale factor**: 10 mV/°C → $T = V_{in} \times 100$. - [x] **LM36 scale factor**: 10 mV/°C with +0.5V offset (centigrade). - [x] **Photoresistor (LDR)**: Forms a voltage divider with a fixed resistor; ADC reads the divider midpoint. - [x] **Enable SPI** on Raspberry Pi: `sudo raspi-config` → Interfacing Options → SPI → Enable → Reboot. - [x] **Python library used**: `spidev` (install via `pip install spidev`). - [x] **Alternative library**: `gpiozero` has a built-in `MCP3008` class (`from gpiozero import MCP3008`). - [x] **Differential mode** allows noise-immune reading between two channels: SGL/DIFF bit = 0, e.g., CH0-CH1 pair. - [x] **Differential mode reading CH0 vs CH1**: byte 1 = `0x00` (i.e., start=1, SGL/DIFF=0, D2-D0=000). - [x] **Daisy-chaining**: Multiple MCP3008s can share SPI bus by giving each its own dedicated CS pin. - [x] **Sampling rate** = 200 ksps @ 5V, 75 ksps @ 2.7V (datasheet specification). - [x] **Package pinout**: 16-pin PDIP, with CH0–CH7 on pins 1–8, power on pin 16, V_REF on pin 15. - [x] **Viva question trick**: "Why 1023 and not 1024?" — Because digital codes run 0 to 1023 (1023 intervals), while $2^{10}$ gives 1024 possible codes including zero. > [!NOTE] > **Final KTU Study Tip:** Always draw the **full circuit diagram** before writing the code during the lab examination. The examiner awards **2-3 separate marks** for the circuit diagram even if your code is perfect. Also, demonstrate the experiment **live** with a working sensor (potentiometer or LM35) — this guarantees the **highest internal marks** in continuous evaluation.

topics.title: Controlling a Servo Motor Using the Raspberry Pi
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCGL507 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Controlling a Servo Motor Using the Raspberry Pi # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **Servo Motor** is a rotary actuator that allows for precise control of angular position, velocity, and acceleration. It consists of a DC motor, a position feedback sensor (typically a potentiometer), and a control circuit. In the context of the KTU 2024 *Microprocessors and Microcontrollers Lab* syllabus, servo control via the Raspberry Pi is implemented through **Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)**, a technique in which the width of digital pulses is varied to encode an analog-like control signal. The **Raspberry Pi** is a single-board computer (SBC) based on the Broadcom BCM2837/BCM2711 system-on-chip. It exposes a **General Purpose Input/Output (GPIO)** header through which PWM signals can be generated via software (using libraries like `RPi.GPIO`) or hardware (using the chip's dedicated PWM peripheral). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The standard hobby servo (e.g., SG90, MG996R) used in this experiment accepts a **50 Hz control signal** (period $T = 20\text{ ms}$) and rotates to a specific angle based on the **pulse width**, which typically ranges from **1 ms (0°)** to **2 ms (180°)**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Steering Wheel of an RC Car Imagine the steering wheel of a radio-controlled (RC) car. You do not push it with continuous force; instead, you give short, sharp, **timed nudges** to the left or right. The steering mechanism "remembers" the last nudge and holds the wheel there. A servo motor works exactly the same way: - The Raspberry Pi sends a short **electrical pulse** every **20 ms**. - The **width** of that pulse tells the servo *where* to point. - A **short pulse** (~1 ms) → "turn all the way left" (0°). - A **long pulse** (~2 ms) → "turn all the way right" (180°). - A **medium pulse** (~1.5 ms) → "stay in the middle" (90°). The internal potentiometer acts as the "memory," constantly comparing the actual shaft angle to the commanded angle and correcting any error. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** The servo is **not** driven by voltage level (like a DC motor). It is driven by the **timing** of a digital pulse. This is the foundational concept of PWM-based actuation. ## 1.3 Physical & Electrical Constants | Parameter | Standard Value | Unit | |---|---|---| | Supply Voltage ($V_{CC}$) | **4.8 – 6.0** | V (V) | | PWM Frequency ($f_{PWM}$) | **50** | Hz (Hz) | | PWM Period ($T$) | **20** | milliseconds (ms) | | Pulse Width (Min) | **1.0** | ms (0°) | | Pulse Width (Neutral) | **1.5** | ms (90°) | | Pulse Width (Max) | **2.0** | ms (180°) | | Stall Torque (SG90) | **1.8** | kg·cm | | Operating Temperature | **0 – 55** | °C | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Theory — Why PWM Controls a Servo A hobby servo contains a small **closed-loop feedback system**. The signal path is: 1. The Raspberry Pi GPIO outputs a **3.3 V digital pulse**. 2. The servo's control board measures the **pulse width** ($\tau$). 3. The on-board controller compares $\tau$ with the feedback from an internal **potentiometer** coupled to the output shaft. 4. An **H-bridge driver** powers the DC motor until the error $\Delta\theta = \theta_{commanded} - \theta_{actual}$ becomes zero. 5. The shaft then **holds** its position against external disturbance (this is called *positional stiffness*). ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of PWM Signal Generation on Raspberry Pi The Raspberry Pi BCM2837 chip has two dedicated hardware PWM channels (GPIO 12, 13, 18, 19). For lab purposes, the standard procedure is: 1. **Import** the `RPi.GPIO` library. 2. **Set** the GPIO numbering mode (`BCM` mode is preferred). 3. **Configure** the chosen GPIO pin as an output. 4. **Initialize** PWM on that pin at 50 Hz: `p = GPIO.PWM(pin, 50)`. 5. **Start** PWM with a 0% duty cycle: `p.start(0)`. 6. **Change duty cycle** to move the servo: $$ \text{Duty Cycle (\%)} = \frac{\tau}{T} \times 100 $$ where $\tau$ is the desired pulse width and $T = 20\text{ ms}$. 7. **Cleanup** the GPIO pins when finished. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Formula / Equation | Description | Units | |---|---|---| | $f_{PWM} = \dfrac{1}{T}$ | PWM frequency is the inverse of period | Hz | | $T = 20\text{ ms}$ | Standard servo control period | ms | | $D = \dfrac{\tau}{T} \times 100$ | Duty cycle from pulse width | % | | $\theta = \dfrac{(\tau - 1.0)}{1.0} \times 90$ | Angle (0°–180°) from pulse width (1–2 ms) | degrees | | $\tau = 1.0 + \dfrac{\theta}{90}$ | Inverse mapping (angle → pulse width) | ms | | $D(\theta) = \left(1.0 + \dfrac{\theta}{90}\right) \times 5$ | Direct duty-cycle formula for 0°–180° | % | | $V_{avg} = D \times V_{CC}$ | Average voltage seen by a DC load (not used for servo control) | V | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** This PWM-based servo control is the backbone of **robotics** (robotic arms, humanoid joints), **aerospace** (model aircraft control surfaces), **CNC machines**, **camera gimbals**, and **automatic door locks**. The same principle scales up to industrial servos that handle kilowatt-level loads. ## 2.4 Critical Boundary Conditions - **Pulse width < 1.0 ms** → Some servos interpret this as a *shutdown* or *reset* command. Avoid. - **Pulse width > 2.5 ms** → Mechanical stop is reached; sending longer pulses can **burn out** the motor. - **Frequency deviation** → If $f_{PWM} \neq 50$ Hz, the servo may jitter, overheat, or refuse to respond. - **GPIO voltage** → Raspberry Pi GPIO outputs **3.3 V** logic. A level shifter is **required** for servos that expect 5 V control signals (though most hobby servos work fine with 3.3 V). # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Pulse Width to Angle Mapping **Given:** - Pulse width $\tau_0 = 1.0\text{ ms}$ corresponds to $\theta_0 = 0°$ - Pulse width $\tau_{180} = 2.0\text{ ms}$ corresponds to $\theta_{180} = 180°$ **Step 1: Linear Interpolation Assumption** The servo's internal control circuit treats pulse width as a *linear* function of angle. This is a valid approximation over the operating range: $$ \theta(\tau) = m\tau + c $$ **Step 2: Substitute the Two Boundary Conditions** $$ \begin{aligned} 0° &= m(1.0) + c \quad &\text{(at } \tau = 1.0\text{ ms)} \\ 180° &= m(2.0) + c \quad &\text{(at } \tau = 2.0\text{ ms)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Solve for $c$ from the First Equation** $$ c = -m(1.0) = -m $$ **Step 4: Substitute into the Second Equation** $$ \begin{aligned} 180° &= 2.0m - m \\ 180° &= m \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5: Back-Substitute for $c$** $$ c = -180° $$ **Step 6: Final Linear Mapping** $$ \boxed{\theta(\tau) = 180\tau - 180 \quad \text{[degrees]}} $$ **Step 7: Verification** - At $\tau = 1.5\text{ ms}$: $\theta = 180(1.5) - 180 = 90°$ ✓ (Neutral) - At $\tau = 1.0\text{ ms}$: $\theta = 0°$ ✓ - At $\tau = 2.0\text{ ms}$: $\theta = 180°$ ✓ ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Angle to Duty Cycle Conversion **Given:** $T = 20\text{ ms}$, $\tau = 1.0 + \dfrac{\theta}{90}$ **Step 1: Substitute the Pulse Width Expression into the Duty Cycle Formula** $$ D = \frac{\tau}{T} \times 100 $$ $$ D = \frac{1.0 + \frac{\theta}{90}}{20} \times 100 $$ **Step 2: Simplify the Numerator by Finding a Common Denominator** $$ D = \frac{\frac{90 + \theta}{90}}{20} \times 100 $$ **Step 3: Multiply Numerator and Denominator** $$ D = \frac{90 + \theta}{1800} \times 100 $$ **Step 4: Final Simplified Formula** $$ \boxed{D(\theta) = \frac{90 + \theta}{18} \quad \text{[percent]}} $$ **Step 5: Numerical Evaluation Table** | Target Angle $\theta$ | Pulse Width $\tau$ (ms) | Duty Cycle $D$ (%) | |---|---|---| | 0° | 1.0 | 5.0 | | 45° | 1.5 | 7.5 | | 90° | 1.5 | 7.5 | | 135° | 1.5 | 8.33... wait — recompute | **Correction Table (re-derived):** | $\theta$ | $\tau = 1 + \theta/90$ (ms) | $D = (\tau/20) \times 100$ (%) | |---|---|---| | 0° | 1.000 | 5.00 | | 45° | 1.500 | 7.50 | | 90° | 2.000 | 10.00 | | 135° | 2.500 | 12.50 | | 180° | 3.000 | 15.00 | > [!NOTE] > **Correction Note for Students:** A common mistake in the KTU 2024 lab manual is to assume the *total* pulse is 1–2 ms. In reality, the pulse is the **ON portion** of a 20 ms period, so a 1.5 ms pulse is 1.5/20 = **7.5% duty cycle**, not 1.5%. This is the single most common error in viva questions. ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Power Dissipation in Servo (Optional Advanced) The average current drawn by an SG90 under no load is approximately $I_0 = 100\text{ mA}$, and under stall $I_{stall} = 650\text{ mA}$. At $V_{CC} = 5\text{ V}$: $$ P_{idle} = V_{CC} \times I_0 = 5 \times 0.1 = 0.5\text{ W} $$ $$ P_{stall} = V_{CC} \times I_{stall} = 5 \times 0.65 = 3.25\text{ W} $$ This is why a **separate 5 V supply** is recommended when controlling servos from a Raspberry Pi — the Pi's 5 V rail can supply only ~1.2 A total, which is shared with USB and other peripherals. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Hardware Connection Block Diagram (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Raspberry Pi
BCM2837 SOC] -->|GPIO 18 Pin 12
3.3V PWM Signal| B[Servo Motor
SG90 Signal Wire Orange] A -->|Ground Pin 6| C[Common GND Bus] D[External 5V Supply
Recommended 2A] -->|Red Wire| B D -->|Ground| C B -->|Internal Feedback| E[Potentiometer
Position Sensor] E -->|Compare Error| F[Servo Control IC] F -->|H-Bridge Drive| G[DC Motor] G -->|Gear Train| H[Output Shaft 0 to 180 deg] H -->|Mechanical Coupling| E styleA fill:e1f5ff,stroke:01579b styleB fill:fff3e0,stroke:e65100 styleD fill:ffebee,stroke:b71c1c ``` ## 4.2 Software Control Flow Diagram (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TD start([Program Start]) --> import[Import RPi.GPIO and time modules] import --> mode[Set GPIO mode to BCM] mode --> warn[Disable GPIO warnings] warn --> pin[Set GPIO 18 as OUTPUT] pin --> pwm[Initialize PWM at 50 Hz on Pin 18] pwm --> start[Start PWM with 0% duty cycle] start --> loop{Loop through angles} loop --> angle[Compute pulse width tau for target angle] angle --> duty[Compute duty cycle D] duty --> change[Change duty cycle to new value] change --> delay[Wait 1 second for servo to reach position] delay --> loop loop -->|Sequence complete| stop[Stop PWM] stop --> clean[Cleanup GPIO pins] clean --> finish([Program End]) styleStart fill:c8e6c9 styleFinish fill:c8e6c9 stylePwm fill:fff59d styleChange fill:ffcc80 ``` ## 4.3 Pinout Reference Table (Raspberry Pi GPIO Header) | Physical Pin | BCM Number | Function in this Experiment | |---|---|---| | Pin 6 | GND | Common ground | | Pin 12 | GPIO 18 | PWM output (Hardware PWM Channel 0) | | Pin 2 or 4 | 5V Power | Optional (only for low-current servos) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Tip:** GPIO 18 is the **only** GPIO pin on the Raspberry Pi that supports **hardware PWM** by default on Channel 0. GPIO 12 and 13 support Channel 1. All other pins require **software PWM** (which is less precise and can introduce jitter). Always mention this distinction in viva. ## 4.4 Signal Timing Diagram (ASCII Schematic) ``` Time ───────────────────────────────────────────► ┌──┐ ┌──┐ ┌──┐ ──┘ └──────────────────┘ └──────────────────┘ └── |τ₁| T - τ₁ |τ₂| T - τ₂ For 0° : τ₁ = 1.0 ms (D = 5%) For 90° : τ₂ = 1.5 ms (D = 7.5%) For 180° : τ₃ = 2.0 ms (D = 10%) T = 20 ms (constant) ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper] **Q: What is Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)? Why is it used to control a servo motor instead of a varying DC voltage?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Pulse Width Modulation (PWM) is a digital signaling technique in which the width of a rectangular pulse is varied while keeping the frequency (and hence the period) constant. The ratio of the ON time to the total period is called the **duty cycle**. $$D = \frac{\tau}{T} \times 100\%$$ PWM is used to control servo motors because the servo's internal control circuit is designed to interpret **timing information**, not voltage levels. The control board measures the duration of the HIGH pulse using an internal timer and compares it with feedback from a potentiometer. Using PWM also provides **noise immunity** — even if the supply voltage fluctuates, the timing of the pulse remains accurate, ensuring reliable position control over a range of $0°$ to $180°$. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Expected] **Q: A servo motor is controlled by a 50 Hz PWM signal. Calculate the duty cycle required to position the servo shaft at 60°.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Step 1:** Period of the signal: $$T = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{50} = 0.02\text{ s} = 20\text{ ms}$$ **Step 2:** Pulse width for 60° using $\tau = 1.0 + \dfrac{\theta}{90}$: $$\tau = 1.0 + \frac{60}{90} = 1.0 + 0.667 = 1.667\text{ ms}$$ **Step 3:** Duty cycle: $$D = \frac{\tau}{T} \times 100 = \frac{1.667}{20} \times 100 = 8.33\%$$ **Answer: 8.33 %** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Q(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the internal architecture of a hobby servo motor and describe how the closed-loop feedback maintains shaft position. (7 marks) **Q(b)** Design and explain a Raspberry Pi-based circuit to control a servo motor through 5 discrete angles: 0°, 45°, 90°, 135°, and 180°. Include the hardware connections, the duty cycle calculations, and the control logic. (7 marks) --- #### Model Solution for Q(a) [7 marks] **Block Diagram (1 mark):** ```mermaid flowchart LR pwm[PWM Input Signal] --> ctrl[Control IC] ctrl -->|Compare| error[Error Signal] fb[Potentiometer Feedback] --> error error -->|Direction + Magnitude| hb[H-Bridge Driver] hb --> motor[DC Motor] motor --> gear[Gear Reduction] gear --> shaft[Output Shaft] shaft -.->|Mechanical Coupling| fb ``` **Explanation (6 marks):** 1. **PWM Input Stage (1 mark):** The control IC receives a 50 Hz PWM signal and extracts the pulse width $\tau$ using an internal timer. 2. **Reference Generation (1 mark):** The pulse width is converted internally to a reference voltage $V_{ref}$ that is proportional to the desired angle. For an SG90, $V_{ref}$ varies from 0 V (at $\tau = 1\text{ ms}$) to $V_{ref,max}$ (at $\tau = 2\text{ ms}$). 3. **Feedback Stage (1 mark):** A potentiometer is mechanically coupled to the output shaft. Its wiper voltage $V_{fb}$ represents the actual shaft angle. When the shaft is at 0°, $V_{fb} = 0$ V; at 180°, $V_{fb} = V_{ref,max}$. 4. **Error Detection (1 mark):** The error amplifier computes: $$\Delta V = V_{ref} - V_{fb}$$ This error signal drives the H-bridge. 5. **H-Bridge Motor Drive (1 mark):** Based on the sign of $\Delta V$, current flows through the motor in one of two directions, rotating the shaft to reduce the error. When $\Delta V = 0$, the motor stops and the shaft holds position. 6. **Closed-Loop Stability (1 mark):** This continuous feedback loop provides *positional stiffness* — the servo resists external disturbances up to its stall torque. The steady-state error is ideally zero. --- #### Model Solution for Q(b) [7 marks] **Hardware Connections (2 marks):** | Servo Wire | Connects To | Pin Type | |---|---|---| | Orange (Signal) | GPIO 18 (Pin 12) | PWM output | | Red (V+) | External 5V, ≥2A supply | Power | | Brown (GND) | Common GND (Pi Pin 6 + Supply GND) | Ground | **Duty Cycle Calculations (2 marks):** Using $D(\theta) = \dfrac{90 + \theta}{18}$: | Angle | Pulse Width (ms) | Duty Cycle (%) | |---|---|---| | 0° | 1.000 | 5.00 | | 45° | 1.500 | 7.50 | | 90° | 2.000 | 10.00 | | 135° | 2.500 | 12.50 | | 180° | 3.000 | 15.00 | **Control Logic (3 marks):** 1. Import `RPi.GPIO` and `time` modules. 2. Set `GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BCM)` and disable warnings. 3. Configure GPIO 18 as output. 4. Create PWM instance: `p = GPIO.PWM(18, 50)`. 5. Start PWM: `p.start(0)`. 6. For each angle $\theta$ in `[0, 45, 90, 135, 180]`: - Compute $D = (90 + \theta)/18$ - Apply `p.ChangeDutyCycle(D)` - Wait 1 second for the shaft to settle 7. After loop ends: `p.stop()` and `GPIO.cleanup()`. The 1-second delay ensures the servo has time to physically reach the target before the next command is issued, preventing jitter and mechanical stress. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice - KTU 2024 Model Paper] **Q(a)** Derive the linear relationship between servo pulse width and shaft angle for a standard hobby servo operating on a 50 Hz PWM signal. Show that the duty cycle for 90° is 7.5%. (7 marks) **Q(b)** Explain the difference between *hardware PWM* and *software PWM* on the Raspberry Pi. Which one is preferred for servo control, and why? (7 marks) --- #### Model Solution for Q(a) [7 marks] **Given:** $f = 50\text{ Hz} \Rightarrow T = 20\text{ ms}$ **Boundary conditions:** $\tau = 1.0\text{ ms}$ at $\theta = 0°$; $\tau = 2.0\text{ ms}$ at $\theta = 180°$ **Step 1 — Assume linear relationship (1 mark):** $$\theta(\tau) = m\tau + c$$ **Step 2 — Apply boundary conditions (2 marks):** $$0 = m(1.0) + c \quad \text{...(i)}$$ $$180 = m(2.0) + c \quad \text{...(ii)}$$ **Step 3 — Solve the system (2 marks):** From (i): $c = -m$ Substituting into (ii): $180 = 2m - m \Rightarrow m = 180$, so $c = -180$. **Step 4 — Final equation (1 mark):** $$\boxed{\theta(\tau) = 180\tau - 180}$$ **Step 5 — Verify 90° gives 7.5% duty cycle (1 mark):** At $\theta = 90°$: $\tau = 1.0 + 90/90 = 1.5\text{ ms}$ $$D = \frac{1.5}{20} \times 100 = 7.5\% \quad \blacksquare$$ --- #### Model Solution for Q(b) [7 marks] **Hardware PWM (3.5 marks):** - Generated by the **BCM2837's dedicated PWM peripheral** (built into the silicon). - Uses an internal **counter and comparator** to toggle the GPIO pin with high timing accuracy. - Available **only on specific pins**: GPIO 12, 13, 18, 19. - **Does not consume CPU cycles** once initialized — the hardware toggles the pin autonomously. - **Jitter-free** at 50 Hz, ideal for servo control. - Configured via the `/sys/class/pwm` interface or libraries like `RPi.GPIO` / `pigpio`. **Software PWM (3.5 marks):** - Generated by the **CPU** using a high-frequency loop that toggles the pin. - Available on **any GPIO pin**. - **Susceptible to jitter** caused by OS scheduling, interrupts, and process preemption (especially under multi-tasking Linux). - **Consumes CPU time** proportional to frequency and number of active channels. - Unsuitable for precision servo control under heavy system load. **Conclusion:** **Hardware PWM is preferred** because servo control is timing-critical; even microsecond-level jitter can cause visible jitter, audible buzzing, or premature wear on the motor. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:** > 1. Writing "$T = 1.5\text{ ms}$ for 90°" as the *period* — it is the **pulse width**, not the period. The period is always 20 ms. > 2. Confusing the **signal wire color** (orange for SG90) — it varies across manufacturers. > 3. Forgetting the **cleanup function** in the program — examiners actively look for `GPIO.cleanup()`. > 4. Powering the servo **only from the Pi's 5V pin** — this can cause a brownout and crash the Pi. > 5. Calculating duty cycle as $\tau \times 100$ instead of $(\tau/T) \times 100$ — instant 0 marks for that step. > 6. Saying "servo runs on analog voltage" — it does **not**. The control is **digital PWM timing**. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ **Servo control is about timing, not voltage.** Pulse width encodes the desired angle. - ✅ The **standard period is 20 ms** (50 Hz). Pulse width varies from **1 ms (0°)** to **2 ms (180°)**. - ✅ **Duty cycle** $D = (\tau / T) \times 100$. For 90°, $D = 7.5\%$, not $1.5\%$. - ✅ **GPIO 18 (Pin 12)** is the preferred hardware PWM pin on the Raspberry Pi. - ✅ Always use an **external 5V supply** for the servo to avoid overloading the Pi. - ✅ **Hardware PWM** is jitter-free; **software PWM** is CPU-intensive and imprecise. - ✅ Always call `GPIO.cleanup()` at the end of the program. - ✅ The formula $\theta(\tau) = 180\tau - 180$ is the **master key** for all angle–pulse conversions. - ✅ The servo's **potentiometer** provides the feedback that makes the control loop **closed-loop** and self-correcting. - ✅ A typical lab program sweeps through 0° → 90° → 180° → 90° → 0° in a loop with 1-second delays.

topics.title: Displaying Text on an OLED Display Using I2C Communication with NodeMCU
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGL507 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Displaying Text on an OLED Display Using I2C Communication with NodeMCU

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**OLED (Organic Light Emitting Diode) Display** is a self-emissive flat-panel display technology in which an organic compound layer emits light in response to an electric current. In embedded systems, the most common variant is the **0.96-inch monochrome SSD1306-based OLED** with a resolution of **128 × 64 pixels**, interfaced via the **I2C (Inter-Integrated Circuit) protocol** as defined by NXP Semiconductors (formerly Philips Semiconductor).

**NodeMCU** is an open-source IoT platform based on the **ESP8266 Wi-Fi SoC (System on Chip)** manufactured by Espressif Systems. It operates at a core voltage of **3.3 V**, integrates a 32-bit **Tensilica L106 Diamond Standard 106Micro** RISC processor running at **80 MHz (default) to 160 MHz**, and provides hardware I2C peripheral on pins **D1 (GPIO5, SCL)** and **D2 (GPIO4, SDA)**.

**I2C Communication** is a synchronous, multi-master, multi-slave, packet-switched, single-ended, serial communication bus invented by Philips Semiconductor in 1982. It uses only **two bidirectional open-drain lines**: **SDA (Serial Data Line)** and **SCL (Serial Clock Line)**, with pull-up resistors connected to VCC.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping (CO Alignment):**
> This experiment maps directly to **CO2: Implement communication protocols with microcontrollers** and **CO3: Interface peripherals using standard protocols**, both under Bloom's cognitive levels **Apply (L3)** and **Analyze (L4)**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine a **two-lane tunnel between two cities**:
- **City A** = Your NodeMCU (the Master / Boss)
- **City B** = The OLED Display (the Slave / Worker)
- **Lane 1 (SDA)** = The road carrying the actual *mail* (data bits)
- **Lane 2 (SCL)** = A metronome that ticks — every tick means "send the next bit of mail now"

The tunnel has a single receptionist at each end. When the boss (NodeMCU) wants to send a message, it first **rings a doorbell** (Start Condition), announces **the worker's house number** (7-bit I2C slave address, e.g., `0x3C`), says **"write mode"** (R/W bit = 0), the worker says **"ready!"** (ACK bit), and then the actual letter (data byte) is delivered. Finally, the boss rings the **"we're done" bell** (Stop Condition).

For text display, each character is a **5×8 dot pattern** (font glyph) stored in the SSD1306's internal **GDDRAM (Graphics Display Data RAM)**. The controller converts ASCII codes into pixel patterns and lights up the corresponding dots on the OLED panel.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Conceptual Takeaway:** I2C is a *question-and-answer* protocol, not a *stream*. Every byte the master sends is acknowledged (ACK) or not-acknowledged (NACK) by the slave. This makes it robust but slightly slower than SPI for high-bandwidth tasks.

---

## 1.3 Critical Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **OLED Logic Voltage (VCC)** | **3.3 V – 5 V** | Must match NodeMCU's 3.3 V logic; never connect a 5 V OLED signal to 3.3 V MCU without level shifting |
| **SSD1306 I2C Address** | **0x3C** (default) or **0x3D** | 7-bit address; bit-shifted to `0x78` (write) or `0x79` (read) on the wire |
| **Standard I2C Clock Frequency** | **100 kHz** | Defined by Philips/NXP I2C Specification v2.1 |
| **Fast Mode I2C Clock** | **400 kHz** | Optional; SSD1306 supports up to **400 kHz** |
| **GDDRAM Size** | **1024 bytes** (128 × 64 bits ÷ 8) | Total pixel memory |
| **Display Pages** | **8 pages** (Page 0 to Page 7) | Each page is 8 pixels tall |
| **NodeMCU Operating Frequency** | **80 MHz** (default) | Configurable to 160 MHz |
| **Pull-up Resistor Value** | **4.7 kΩ** (typical) | Required on both SDA and SCL lines |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Tip:** The pull-up resistor value of **4.7 kΩ** is the *de-facto* industry standard for 3.3 V I2C buses operating below 400 kHz. Values higher than **10 kΩ** may cause signal integrity issues at higher clock rates.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The I2C Protocol — Layered Operational Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Physical Layer (Layer 1)

I2C uses two **open-drain** lines pulled HIGH by external resistors. This is fundamentally different from push-pull (used in SPI). The open-drain configuration allows **wire-AND** logic — any device can pull the line LOW, but none can drive it HIGH. This enables bus arbitration and clock synchronization between multiple masters.

### 2.1.2 Data Link Layer — Frame Structure

A complete I2C transaction follows this structure:

**Start Condition → Slave Address (7 bits) → R/W bit (1 bit) → ACK → Data Byte (8 bits) → ACK → ... → Stop Condition**

- **Start (S)**: SDA transitions from HIGH to LOW **while SCL is HIGH**
- **Stop (P)**: SDA transitions from LOW to HIGH **while SCL is HIGH**
- **ACK**: Receiver pulls SDA LOW during the 9th clock pulse
- **NACK**: Receiver leaves SDA HIGH during the 9th clock pulse

### 2.1.3 Step-by-Step "Why & How" Logic

1. **Why use a 7-bit address?** — To allow up to **127 devices** on a single bus (addresses `0x00` and `0x7F` are reserved).
2. **Why R/W bit after address?** — To inform the slave whether the master will *write to* or *read from* the slave's register.
3. **Why ACK bit?** — Confirms the slave received the byte successfully. If NACK, the master retries or aborts.
4. **Why pull-up resistors?** — Open-drain outputs cannot drive HIGH by themselves; they can only pull LOW. Resistors do the "pulling HIGH."

---

## 2.2 SSD1306 OLED Controller — Internal Architecture

The SSD1306 is a **single-chip CMOS OLED/PLED driver** with these subsystems:

- **GDDRAM (1024 bytes)** — Stores the 128×64 pixel bit-pattern
- **Command Decoder** — Interprets control bytes (Co = 0)
- **Page Register** — Tracks which of the 8 pages is currently selected
- **Segment Driver** — Drives columns 0 to 127
- **Common Driver** — Drives rows (pages) 0 to 7
- **Oscillator & Timing Generator** — Generates internal display refresh signals

> [!NOTE]
> **Communication Stream Distinction:** The SSD1306 uses a clever "stream of bytes" interpretation. If the *Control byte* (sent first after the address) has its **Co bit = 0** and **D/C# bit = 0**, then the next bytes are interpreted as **commands** (e.g., Set Page Address). If **D/C# bit = 1**, the next bytes are **display data** (pixel data).

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (I2C + OLED)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No vertical pipes `|` allowed inside this table — using `\vert` and `\mid` for absolute values per protocol.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Value | Unit / Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | I2C Bit Period (Standard) | $T_{bit} = \dfrac{1}{f_{SCL}} = \dfrac{1}{100\,\text{kHz}} = 10\,\mu s$ | seconds |
| 2 | I2C Bit Period (Fast) | $T_{bit} = \dfrac{1}{f_{SCL}} = \dfrac{1}{400\,\text{kHz}} = 2.5\,\mu s$ | seconds |
| 3 | Bytes per I2C Transaction | $N_{bytes} = 1 + \dfrac{D_{payload}}{8}$ | bytes (1 addr byte + data bytes) |
| 4 | GDDRAM Capacity | $M_{GDDRAM} = 128 \times 64 \div 8 = 1024$ | bytes |
| 5 | Number of Display Pages | $P = \dfrac{H_{pixels}}{8} = \dfrac{64}{8} = 8$ | pages |
| 6 | Page Width in Bytes | $W_{page} = 128$ | bytes/page |
| 7 | Characters per Line (5×8 font) | $C = \dfrac{128}{6} \approx 21$ (with 1 px gap) | chars |
| 8 | Display Lines (8 px font + 2 px gap) | $L = \dfrac{64}{10} = 6$ (typical layout) | lines |
| 9 | SCL HIGH Time (Standard) | $t_{HIGH} \geq 4.0\,\mu s$ | required by spec |
| 10 | SCL LOW Time (Standard) | $t_{LOW} \geq 4.7\,\mu s$ | required by spec |
| 11 | Setup Time (SDA to SCL) | $t_{SU;DAT} \geq 250\,ns$ | required by spec |
| 12 | Rise Time of SDA/SCL | $t_{r} \leq 1000\,ns$ (Standard) | required by spec |
| 13 | Bus Pull-up Resistance | $R_{p} = \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_{OL}}{I_{OL}} \approx 4.7\,k\Omega$ | typical |
| 14 | I2C Address (SSD1306) | $A_{7} = 0x3C$ (binary `0111100`) | 7-bit address |
| 15 | Wire Format Address | $A_{wire} = (A_{7} \ll 1) \;\vert\; R/W$ | 8-bit on wire |
| 16 | Total Display Refresh Rate | $f_{refresh} \approx 100\,Hz$ | frames per second |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

I2C-OLED combinations are the **de-facto standard user interface** for:

- **IoT sensor readouts** (temperature, humidity on DHT11 / DHT22 boards)
- **Wearable devices** (smart watches, fitness bands — low power OLED)
- **Industrial HMI panels** (machine status displays)
- **Medical devices** (portable glucometers, pulse oximeters)
- **Smart home controllers** (Alexa, Google Nest displays)
- **Aerospace instrumentation** (secondary cockpit displays)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why OLED over LCD?** OLED pixels emit their own light — no backlight needed. This results in **true blacks**, infinite contrast ratio (**> 10,000:1**), and **40–60% lower power consumption** when displaying dark content. The KTU lab uses OLED to teach both *bus protocols* and *modern display technology*.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: I2C Address Wire-Format Conversion

When the SSD1306 datasheet says the address is **0x3C** (7-bit), it does **not** appear on the wire as `0x3C`. The 7-bit address must be **left-shifted by 1 bit** and the LSB is the **R/W flag**. Let us derive this step by step.

**Given:**
- 7-bit I2C address: $A_7 = 0x3C$
- Binary: $A_7 = 0111100$
- Operation: WRITE

**Step 1: Convert 7-bit address to binary representation.**

$$A_7 = 0x3C = 0 \times 128 + 0 \times 64 + 1 \times 32 + 1 \times 16 + 1 \times 8 + 1 \times 4 + 0 \times 2 + 0 \times 1$$

$$A_7 = 0\,1100\,1100 \;(\text{as 8-bit for visualization: } 0x3C)$$

**Step 2: Left-shift by 1 bit to make room for R/W flag.**

$$A_{shifted} = A_7 \ll 1 = 01111000_2 = 0x78$$

**Step 3: Append R/W bit (0 for write, 1 for read).**

$$A_{wire,write} = (A_7 \ll 1) \;\vert\; 0 = 01111000_2 = 0x78$$

$$A_{wire,read} = (A_7 \ll 1) \;\vert\; 1 = 01111001_2 = 0x79$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Hint:** The `Wire.beginTransmission(0x3C)` function in Arduino **automatically** handles the left-shift. Students should know that on the physical bus, the *first* byte transmitted is **`0x78`** for write operations, not `0x3C`. This distinction is a **favourite KTU viva question**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: GDDRAM Page Organization

The SSD1306 divides its 128×64 pixel GDDRAM into **8 horizontal pages** (Page 0 to Page 7). Each page is **8 pixels tall** and **128 pixels wide** (one byte per column).

**Step 1: Compute total pixel count.**

$$N_{pixels} = W \times H = 128 \times 64 = 8192\;\text{pixels}$$

**Step 2: Compute total bytes (1 bit per pixel).**

$$M_{GDDRAM} = \dfrac{N_{pixels}}{8} = \dfrac{8192}{8} = 1024\;\text{bytes}$$

**Step 3: Compute number of pages.**

$$P = \dfrac{H_{pixels}}{8} = \dfrac{64}{8} = 8\;\text{pages}$$

**Step 4: Compute bytes per page.**

$$W_{page} = W_{pixels} = 128\;\text{bytes per page}$$

**Step 5: Verify consistency.**

$$M_{GDDRAM} = P \times W_{page} = 8 \times 128 = 1024\;\text{bytes} \;\checkmark$$

**Resulting Page Layout Table:**

| Page Number | Pixel Rows | Column Range | GDDRAM Address Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Page 0 | Rows 0–7 | 0–127 | 0x00 – 0x7F |
| Page 1 | Rows 8–15 | 0–127 | 0x80 – 0xFF |
| Page 2 | Rows 16–23 | 0–127 | 0x100 – 0x17F |
| Page 3 | Rows 24–31 | 0–127 | 0x180 – 0x1FF |
| Page 4 | Rows 32–39 | 0–127 | 0x200 – 0x27F |
| Page 5 | Rows 40–47 | 0–127 | 0x280 – 0x2FF |
| Page 6 | Rows 48–55 | 0–127 | 0x300 – 0x37F |
| Page 7 | Rows 56–63 | 0–127 | 0x380 – 0x3FF |

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Maximum Text Capacity on the Display

Using the standard 5×8 font with 1-pixel horizontal and 1-pixel vertical spacing:

**Step 1: Character cell width.**

$$W_{cell} = 5_{glyph} + 1_{spacing} = 6\;\text{pixels}$$

**Step 2: Character cell height.**

$$H_{cell} = 8_{glyph} + 1_{spacing} = 9\;\text{pixels (approx. 10 with line break)}$$

**Step 3: Characters per line.**

$$C_{line} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{W_{display}}{W_{cell}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \dfrac{128}{6} \right\rfloor = 21\;\text{characters}$$

**Step 4: Number of text lines (using 8-pixel line spacing).**

$$L_{lines} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{H_{display}}{H_{cell}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \dfrac{64}{10} \right\rfloor = 6\;\text{lines}$$

**Step 5: Total character capacity.**

$$C_{total} = C_{line} \times L_{lines} = 21 \times 6 = 126\;\text{characters}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Limitation:** The above is theoretical. In practice, line height is usually 8 pixels (one page) for 5×8 font, giving 8 lines × 21 characters = **168 chars max**. The `Adafruit_SSD1306` library defaults to 6×8 spacing.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Pin Mapping & Connection

| NodeMCU Pin | GPIO | I2C Function | OLED Pin | Description |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **3V3** | Power | VCC | **VCC** | Power supply (3.3 V) |
| **GND** | Power | Ground | **GND** | Common ground |
| **D1** | GPIO5 | **SCL** | **SCL** | Serial Clock Line |
| **D2** | GPIO4 | **SDA** | **SDA** | Serial Data Line |
| — | — | — | **RES** (optional) | Reset (tie to VCC if unused) |
| — | — | — | **DC** (optional) | Data/Command (only for SPI mode) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Wiring Mistake:** Some SSD1306 modules label the I2C pins as `SCL`/`SDA` but the **physical pin order on the PCB** is sometimes reversed. Always verify with a multimeter's continuity test — the **SCL pin** is connected to the OLED controller's pin **10** and **SDA to pin 9** per the SSD1306 datasheet.

---

## 3.5 Reference Implementation — Annotated Walkthrough

The following Arduino sketch is the **canonical KTU lab program** for this experiment.

```cpp
// ============================================================
//  KTU Experiment 2: OLED Text Display via I2C with NodeMCU
//  Controller: ESP8266 (NodeMCU v1.0)
//  Display:    0.96" SSD1306 OLED (128x64), I2C address 0x3C
// ============================================================
#include <Wire.h>                  // I2C library (built-in)
#include <Adafruit_GFX.h>          // Graphics primitives library
#include <Adafruit_SSD1306.h>      // SSD1306-specific driver

// ---- Pin Configuration ----
#define SDA_PIN  D2                // GPIO4  -- OLED SDA
#define SCL_PIN  D1                // GPIO5  -- OLED SCL
#define OLED_ADDR 0x3C             // 7-bit I2C address
#define SCREEN_W  128
#define SCREEN_H  64

// ---- OLED Object Construction ----
// -4 means no external RESET pin (we tie OLED RES to VCC)
Adafruit_SSD1306 display(SCREEN_W, SCREEN_H, &Wire, -4);

void setup() {
  Serial.begin(115200);
  delay(1000);

  // (1) Initialise I2C bus on custom pins for NodeMCU
  Wire.begin(SDA_PIN, SCL_PIN);

  // (2) Initialise OLED with the resolved I2C address
  if (!display.begin(SSD1306_SWITCHCAPVCC, OLED_ADDR)) {
    Serial.println(F("SSD1306 allocation failed"));
    for (;;);                     // Halt execution
  }

  // (3) Clear the display buffer (GDDRAM = 0x00)
  display.clearDisplay();
  display.setTextSize(1);         // 6x8 pixel font
  display.setTextColor(SSD1306_WHITE);
  display.setCursor(0, 0);        // Top-left origin
  display.println(F("KTU 2024 SCHEME"));
  display.println(F("OLED + I2C Lab"));
  display.println(F("NodeMCU ESP8266"));
  display.display();              // Push buffer to GDDRAM
}

void loop() {
  // Optional: blink a pixel, scroll text, or update sensor readings
}
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Library Installation Note:** Students must install **`Adafruit SSD1306`**, **`Adafruit GFX Library`**, and **`Adafruit BusIO`** via Arduino IDE Library Manager. The `Wire.h` is built-in. **No external library download is permitted during the lab exam.**

---

## 3.6 Initialization Command Sequence (What the Library Does Internally)

| Step | Command (Hex) | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `0xAE` | **Display OFF** — prevent garbage during init |
| 2 | `0xD5 0x80` | **Set Display Clock Divide Ratio** to default |
| 3 | `0xA8 0x3F` | **Set Multiplex Ratio** to 64 (for 128×64) |
| 4 | `0xD3 0x00` | **Set Display Offset** to 0 |
| 5 | `0x40` | **Set Start Line** to 0 |
| 6 | `0x8D 0x14` | **Enable Charge Pump** (essential for 3.3 V) |
| 7 | `0x20 0x00` | **Set Memory Addressing Mode** to Horizontal |
| 8 | `0xA1` | **Set Segment Re-map** (mirror horizontally) |
| 9 | `0xC8` | **Set COM Output Scan Direction** (mirror vertically) |
| 10 | `0xDA 0x12` | **Set COM Pins** for 128×64 |
| 11 | `0x81 0xCF` | **Set Contrast** to mid-high (0xCF ≈ 80%) |
| 12 | `0xD9 0xF1` | **Set Pre-charge Period** |
| 13 | `0xDB 0x40` | **Set VCOMH Deselect Level** |
| 14 | `0xA4` | **Display from RAM** (not all-on) |
| 15 | `0xA6` | **Normal Display** (not inverted) |
| 16 | `0x2E` | **Deactivate Scroll** |
| 17 | `0xAF` | **Display ON** — final step |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System-Level Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MCU["NodeMCU ESP8266"]
        CPU["Tensilica L106\nCPU Core 80MHz"]
        I2C_PER["Hardware I2C\nPeripheral"]
        GPIO["GPIO4 = D2\nGPIO5 = D1"]
    end

    subgraph BUS["I2C Bus"]
        SDA["SDA Line\n4.7k pull-up"]
        SCL["SCL Line\n4.7k pull-up"]
    end

    subgraph OLED["SSD1306 OLED Module"]
        CTRL["Command\nDecoder"]
        GDDRAM["GDDRAM\n1024 bytes"]
        DRIVER["Segment +\nCommon Driver"]
        PANEL["128x64\nOLED Panel"]
    end

    subgraph APP["Application Layer"]
        SKETCH["Arduino Sketch\nAdafruit_SSD1306 lib"]
    end

    APP -->|compiles to| MCU
    CPU -->|controls| I2C_PER
    I2C_PER --> GPIO
    GPIO --> SDA
    GPIO --> SCL
    SDA --> CTRL
    SCL --> CTRL
    CTRL -->|writes pixels| GDDRAM
    GDDRAM --> DRIVER
    DRIVER --> PANEL
```

> [!NOTE]
> This block diagram maps the **data flow path** from the user's Arduino code, through the ESP8266's I2C peripheral, onto the physical bus, into the SSD1306's command decoder, and finally to the OLED panel. Each block corresponds to a **distinct hardware/software layer** that a KTU examiner may ask about.

---

## 4.2 I2C Transaction Flow — Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant M as Master (NodeMCU)
    participant S as Slave (SSD1306)

    M->>S: Start Condition (SDA falls while SCL high)
    Note over M,S: Step 1: Address Phase
    M->>S: 7-bit Address 0x3C << 1 = 0x78 (Write)
    S-->>M: ACK (pulls SDA LOW)
    Note over M,S: Step 2: Control Byte Phase
    M->>S: Control Byte: 0x00 (Co=0, D/C#=0, Command Stream)
    S-->>M: ACK
    Note over M,S: Step 3: Command Payload
    M->>S: Command Byte: 0xAE (Display OFF)
    S-->>M: ACK
    M->>S: Command Byte: 0xD5 0x80 (Set Clock)
    S-->>M: ACK
    M->>S: Command Byte: 0xAF (Display ON)
    S-->>M: ACK
    Note over M,S: Step 4: Data Phase (after another Start)
    M->>S: Start Condition
    M->>S: Address 0x78 (Write)
    S-->>M: ACK
    M->>S: Control Byte: 0x40 (Co=0, D/C#=1, Data Stream)
    S-->>M: ACK
    M->>S: Display Data Byte 1 (e.g., 'K' glyph column 0)
    S-->>M: ACK
    M->>S: Display Data Byte 2 (e.g., 'K' glyph column 1)
    S-->>M: ACK
    M->>S: ... more glyph columns ...
    M->>S: Stop Condition (SDA rises while SCL high)
```

---

## 4.3 I2C Bit-Level Timing Diagram

```mermaid
gantt
    title I2C Bit Timing (Standard Mode, f_SCL = 100 kHz)
    dateFormat X
    axisFormat %s
    section SCL
    SCL High (tHIGH >= 4.0us)   :a1, 0, 5
    SCL Low (tLOW >= 4.7us)     :a2, 5, 10
    section SDA
    SDA Valid (tSU;DAT >= 250ns):b1, 0, 4
    SDA Hold (tHD;DAT >= 0ns)   :b2, 4, 9
    section ACK
    Slave ACK slot (9th clock)  :c1, 9, 10
```

> [!NOTE]
