> **Timing Interpretation:** Each bit cell is **10 µs** wide at 100 kHz. The SDA line must remain stable for at least **4.0 µs** of SCL-high time, with **250 ns** of setup time before SCL rises. The 9th clock pulse is the **ACK/NACK slot**.

---

## 4.4 Hardware Connection Schematic (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    VCC["3.3V Power Rail"] --> R1["R_pullup = 4.7k"]
    VCC --> R2["R_pullup = 4.7k"]
    R1 --> SDA_NET["SDA Net"]
    R2 --> SCL_NET["SCL Net"]
    VCC -->|via 100nF| DECOUP["Decoupling Cap"]
    DECOUP --> GND["GND"]

    SDA_NET --> D2["NodeMCU D2\nGPIO4 (SDA)"]
    SCL_NET --> D1["NodeMCU D1\nGPIO5 (SCL)"]

    SDA_NET --> OLED_SDA["OLED Module SDA"]
    SCL_NET --> OLED_SCL["OLED Module SCL"]
    VCC --> OLED_VCC["OLED VCC"]
    GND --> OLED_GND["OLED GND"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Lab Wiring Rule:** Always place the **decoupling capacitor (100 nF)** as close to the OLED's VCC/GND pins as possible. This suppresses voltage spikes caused by OLED pixel switching transients. The pull-up resistors must be connected to the **same voltage rail** as the OLED's VCC, not the NodeMCU's 5 V pin (which doesn't exist on NodeMCU — it only outputs 3.3 V via the 3V3 pin).

---

## 4.5 Library Dependency Graph

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    USER["User Sketch\nOLED_Text.ino"] --> LIB1["Adafruit_SSD1306.h"]
    LIB1 --> LIB2["Adafruit_GFX.h"]
    LIB1 --> LIB3["Adafruit_BusIO.h"]
    LIB2 --> LIB4["Print.h (Arduino Core)"]
    LIB1 --> LIB5["Wire.h (I2C Driver)"]
    LIB5 --> ESPHW["ESP8266 I2C Hardware\nRegisters"]
    ESPHW --> SDA_SCL["Physical SDA/SCL Pins"]
```

# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**State the function of the SDA and SCL lines in the I2C protocol. Why are external pull-up resistors required?**

**Model Answer:**

The **SDA (Serial Data Line)** carries the actual data bits (address, command, payload) between master and slave. The **SCL (Serial Clock Line)** carries the synchronization clock generated by the master — every bit on SDA is sampled on the rising edge of SCL.

External pull-up resistors are required because I2C uses **open-drain output drivers**, which can only pull the line LOW but cannot drive it HIGH. The pull-up resistors provide the HIGH logic level. Without them, the bus would float and logic levels would be undefined.

*(Marks: 2 + 1)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**List any six SSD1306 initialization commands and state their purpose.**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Command | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `0xAE` | Display OFF during init |
| 2 | `0xD5 0x80` | Set display clock |
| 3 | `0xA8 0x3F` | Set multiplex to 64 |
| 4 | `0x8D 0x14` | Enable charge pump |
| 5 | `0xAF` | Display ON |
| 6 | `0xA1` | Segment remap (horizontal mirror) |

*(Marks: 0.5 × 6 = 3)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Part B questions carry **14 marks**, usually split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Below are **two alternative questions**; in the exam, students answer **either OR**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
*With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of the SSD1306 OLED controller. How is its GDDRAM organized for a 128×64 display?*

**Model Solution:**

The SSD1306 is a single-chip CMOS OLED driver consisting of:

1. **GDDRAM (1024 bytes)** — Bit-mapped memory; each bit controls one OLED pixel.
2. **Command Decoder** — Interprets control bytes from the I2C stream.
3. **Segment Driver (Column Driver)** — Drives 128 column electrodes (SEG0–SEG127).
4. **Common Driver (Row Driver)** — Drives 64 row electrodes (COM0–COM63).
5. **Oscillator & Timing Generator** — Generates internal refresh signals.
6. **Charge Pump + Voltage Regulator** — Generates the high voltage (~7–9 V) required to drive OLED pixels from a 3.3 V supply.

**GDDRAM Organization (derivation shown):**

$$P = \dfrac{64}{8} = 8\;\text{pages},\quad W_{page} = 128\;\text{bytes}$$

Pages 0–7 cover rows 0–7, 8–15, …, 56–63 respectively. Each page is 128 bytes wide. Total memory = 8 × 128 = **1024 bytes** = 8 Kbits.

The addressing can be in **Page Addressing Mode** (default) or **Horizontal Addressing Mode** (used by the Adafruit library).

*(Block diagram: 3 marks, GDDRAM math: 2 marks, addressing modes: 2 marks = 7 marks)*

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
*Write the complete Arduino sketch to display the message "HELLO KTU" at the centre of a 128×64 I2C OLED using NodeMCU. State the libraries used and explain the I2C address-resolution process.*

**Model Solution:**

```cpp
#include <Wire.h>
#include <Adafruit_GFX.h>
#include <Adafruit_SSD1306.h>

#define SDA_PIN D2
#define SCL_PIN D1
#define OLED_ADDR 0x3C
#define W 128
#define H 64

Adafruit_SSD1306 display(W, H, &Wire, -4);

void setup() {
  Wire.begin(SDA_PIN, SCL_PIN);
  display.begin(SSD1306_SWITCHCAPVCC, OLED_ADDR);
  display.clearDisplay();
  display.setTextSize(2);
  display.setTextColor(SSD1306_WHITE);
  display.setCursor(10, 25);
  display.println(F("HELLO KTU"));
  display.display();
}

void loop() { }
```

**Libraries used:** `Wire.h` (I2C), `Adafruit_GFX.h` (graphics), `Adafruit_SSD1306.h` (driver).

**Address resolution:** The 7-bit address `0x3C` is left-shifted by 1 bit by the Wire library to produce the wire-format byte `0x78` (for write) and `0x79` (for read). The LSB of the wire byte is the R/W flag. Thus `Wire.beginTransmission(0x3C)` is equivalent to transmitting `0x78` on the bus.

*(Code: 4 marks, libraries: 1 mark, address resolution: 2 marks = 7 marks)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
*Explain the I2C Start, Stop, ACK, and NACK conditions. Draw the bit-level timing diagram for a single byte transmission (address + ACK).*

**Model Solution:**

1. **Start (S) Condition:** SDA falls (HIGH → LOW) **while SCL is HIGH**. Initiates a transaction.
2. **Stop (P) Condition:** SDA rises (LOW → HIGH) **while SCL is HIGH**. Terminates a transaction.
3. **Repeated Start (Sr):** A new Start without a prior Stop. Used to switch direction.
4. **ACK:** Receiver pulls SDA LOW during the 9th SCL pulse — "I received it."
5. **NACK:** Receiver leaves SDA HIGH during the 9th SCL pulse — "I did not receive / I am done."

**Single-Byte Transmission Timing (address `0x3C`, write):**

The master transmits bits **0111100** (7-bit address), then **0** (R/W = write), and the slave responds with **ACK**.

```
        START   0    1    1    1    1    0    0    0   ACK
SCL: ___|‾|__|‾|__|‾|__|‾|__|‾|__|‾|__|‾|__|‾|__|‾|__
SDA: ____|‾‾‾‾|‾|‾‾|‾|‾‾|‾|‾‾|‾|__|‾‾|‾‾|__|_________
                6    5    4    3    2    1    0   R/W
                MSB ----------------- LSB
```

*(Conditions: 3 marks, timing diagram: 2 marks, ACK explanation: 2 marks = 7 marks)*

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
*Compare I2C and SPI communication protocols. Why is I2C preferred for low-speed peripheral interfacing on the NodeMCU?*

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | I2C | SPI |
|---|---|---|
| Wires required | 2 (SDA, SCL) | 4+ (MOSI, MISO, SCLK, SS) |
| Speed | 100 kHz – 3.4 MHz | Up to 80 MHz (ESP8266) |
| Addressing | 7- or 10-bit slave address | Hardware chip-select (SS pin) |
| Masters | Multi-master supported | Single master typical |
| Duplex | Half-duplex | Full-duplex |
| Power consumption | Lower | Higher |
| Pin count on MCU | Lower | Higher (1 SS per slave) |

**Why I2C is preferred on NodeMCU for OLED:**

1. **Pin economy** — Only D1 and D2 are needed, leaving GPIOs free for sensors.
2. **Multiple devices on one bus** — A temperature sensor, RTC, and OLED can share the same 2-wire bus.
3. **Built-in library support** — Arduino's `Wire.h` is mature and well-tested.
4. **OLED is low-bandwidth** — 128×64 × 1 bpp @ 60 Hz = 61.4 kbps, easily handled at 100 kHz I2C.
5. **Library abstraction** — The Adafruit SSD1306 library handles page addressing internally, saving development time.

*(Comparison table: 4 marks, justification: 3 marks = 7 marks)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (as observed in 2022–2024 KTU valuations):**
>
> 1. **Confusing 7-bit address with wire-format byte** — Many students write `Wire.beginTransmission(0x78)` instead of `0x3C`. This **does compile** (Wire library accepts both) but **violates the SSD1306 datasheet convention** and loses 1 mark. **Always use `0x3C` in code comments.**
> 2. **Forgetting the charge-pump command `0x8D 0x14`** — Required for 3.3 V operation. Without it, the display is invisible. Library handles this, but students writing manual init sequences often miss it.
> 3. **Wrong SDA/SCL pin assignment** — On some NodeMCU clones, `D1` and `D2` are **not** the default I2C pins. Verify with `Wire.begin(SDA, SCL)` argument order.
> 4. **Pull-up resistor on the wrong rail** — Tie pull-ups to **3.3 V** (matching MCU and OLED VCC), not 5 V (which NodeMCU does not provide anyway).
> 5. **Forgetting `display.display()`** — `clearDisplay()`, `setCursor()`, `println()` only modify the *internal buffer* in RAM. Without the final `display.display()` call, **nothing appears on the screen** — a classic 0-mark answer.
> 6. **Inverting setCursor() coordinates** — Origin is **top-left (0,0)**, not bottom-left. y increases downward.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🎯 KTU Last-Minute Revision Bullets:**

- ✅ OLED used is **0.96" SSD1306, 128×64, monochrome**, I2C address **`0x3C`**.
- ✅ NodeMCU I2C pins: **D1 = SCL (GPIO5)**, **D2 = SDA (GPIO4)**.
- ✅ I2C uses **2 wires** (SDA, SCL) — both need **4.7 kΩ pull-ups to 3.3 V**.
- ✅ I2C is **synchronous, half-duplex, multi-master, open-drain, addressable**.
- ✅ Wire-format address = `(7-bit address) << 1` + R/W bit → `0x3C` becomes `0x78` (write).
- ✅ GDDRAM = **1024 bytes** = **8 pages** × **128 columns**; 1 bit per pixel.
- ✅ Standard I2C speed = **100 kHz**; Fast mode = **400 kHz**; bit period = 10 µs / 2.5 µs.
- ✅ A complete I2C frame = **Start + Address + R/W + ACK + Data + ACK + Stop**.
- ✅ SSD1306 distinguishes **commands (D/C#=0)** from **data (D/C#=1)** via the **Control byte**.
- ✅ Libraries: `Wire.h`, `Adafruit_GFX.h`, `Adafruit_SSD1306.h` — must be installed before lab.
- ✅ Buffer must be **pushed to GDDRAM** by calling `display.display()` — last critical step.
- ✅ **Charge-pump command `0x8D 0x14`** is mandatory for 3.3 V operation.
- ✅ Origin is **top-left (0,0)**; x→right, y→down.
- ✅ 21 chars per line × 8 lines max = **168-character display capacity** (6×8 font).
- ✅ I2C is preferred over SPI for OLED because OLED bandwidth (~60 kbps) is well below I2C limits and pin count is saved.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Using PWM to Fade an LED In and Out Using NodeMCU
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCGL507 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Using PWM to Fade an LED In and Out Using NodeMCU ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)** is a digital signaling technique in which the width of pulses in a periodic square wave is varied to encode an analog-like average voltage level. In the context of the **NodeMCU (ESP8266-based) development board**, PWM is used to drive peripheral devices (such as LEDs, motors, and buzzers) at intermediate power levels by rapidly switching a digital GPIO pin between HIGH and LOW states. The **analogWrite()** function in the Arduino-ESP8266 core maps an integer value to a hardware-timer-driven PWM duty cycle. Unlike the standard Arduino Uno (8-bit, 0–255), the **NodeMCU provides a 10-bit PWM resolution (0–1023)** due to the underlying ESP8266 timer hardware. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** NodeMCU's `analogWrite()` accepts a value in the range **0 to 1023**, where `0` corresponds to a **0% duty cycle** (LED fully OFF) and `1023` corresponds to a **100% duty cycle** (LED fully ON). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition) Imagine a water tap that you can only open **fully** or **close completely** — never halfway. To simulate a "half-open" tap, you would open it fully for 1 second, then close it for 1 second, and keep repeating this very quickly. From the bucket's point of view, it receives water **50% of the time**, so the *average* flow looks like a half-open tap. This is exactly how PWM works: - The **LED** is the bucket. - The **fully open tap** is the `HIGH` (5V/3.3V) state. - The **closed tap** is the `LOW` (0V) state. - The **ratio of "open" time to the total cycle** is called the **duty cycle**. Your eyes cannot see the rapid blinking (faster than ~100 Hz); they integrate the light and perceive a steady, dimmer brightness. > [!NOTE] > **Standard PWM Frequency on NodeMCU:** The default PWM frequency is **1000 Hz (1 kHz)**. This is well above the human flicker fusion threshold (~60–100 Hz), so the LED appears smoothly dim — it does not visibly blink. ### 1.3 Physical & Hardware Constants | Parameter | Standard Value | Unit | |---|---|---| | NodeMCU Logic HIGH Voltage | **3.3** | V | | PWM Resolution | **10-bit (1024 steps)** | levels | | Default PWM Frequency | **1000** | Hz | | Recommended LED Series Resistor | **220 – 330** | Ω | | Safe LED Current (typical 5mm) | **15 – 20** | mA | | PWM-capable GPIO pins (NodeMCU) | **D1, D2, D3, D4, D5, D6, D7, D8, RX** | — | > [!NOTE] > **Pin D0 (GPIO16)** on NodeMCU does **NOT support PWM**. Always select from the valid list above when planning your experiment. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Logic of PWM — Step-by-Step 1. **Select a PWM-capable GPIO pin** (e.g., D1 / GPIO5). 2. **Configure the pin as OUTPUT** using `pinMode(pin, OUTPUT)`. This enables the timer hardware to drive the pin. 3. **Call `analogWrite(pin, value)`** where `value ∈ [0, 1023]`. 4. The ESP8266's hardware timer generates a square wave at **1 kHz** (default) on that pin. 5. The **duty cycle** is determined by `value / 1023`. 6. The **average DC voltage** seen by the LED is `Vmax × Duty Cycle`. 7. By sweeping `value` from 0 → 1023 → 0 with a small `delay()`, the LED appears to **fade in and fade out** smoothly. ### 2.2 Why PWM Works for LED Brightness Control An LED's perceived brightness is approximately proportional to the **average current** flowing through it. By chopping the supply voltage into high-frequency pulses, the average current is reduced, dimming the LED without any analog voltage regulation circuitry. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Why" Behind It:** Digital GPIO pins can only output 0V or 3.3V. There is no true analog output. PWM is a clever workaround that *emulates* analog behavior through time-domain averaging. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Formula / Expression | Meaning | Units / Notes | |---|---|---| | $$D = \frac{T_{on}}{T_{total}} \times 100\%$$ | Duty cycle as a percentage | $T_{on}$ = HIGH time, $T_{total}$ = period | | $$D = \frac{V_{avg}}{V_{max}} \times 100\%$$ | Duty cycle from measured average voltage | $V_{max} = 3.3$ V on NodeMCU | | $$V_{avg} = V_{max} \cdot D$$ | Average DC voltage across the load | Used to dim LEDs / drive motors | | $$f_{PWM} = \frac{1}{T_{total}}$$ | PWM frequency | **1000 Hz** default on NodeMCU | | $$I_{LED} = \frac{V_{max} - V_{f}}{R_{series}}$$ | LED current (Ohm's law) | $V_{f} \approx 2.0$ V for red LED, $R = 220 \;\Omega$ | | $$N_{steps} = 2^{res} = 1024$$ | Total discrete brightness levels | 10-bit resolution | | $$\text{Brightness}(\%) = \frac{\text{analogWrite value}}{1023} \times 100$$ | Mapping from code value to % brightness | Used in code logic | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Motor speed control** in robotics (DC motors, servo motors). - **LED dimming** in smart lighting systems. - **Audio signal generation** by varying duty cycles at audio frequencies. - **Power delivery control** in switch-mode power supplies (SMPS). - **Servo motor angle control** — servo angle depends on PWM pulse width (typically 1 ms = 0°, 2 ms = 180°). > [!NOTE] > **KTU Industry Insight:** The same PWM technique used here scales up to **variable-frequency drives (VFDs)** in industrial motor control and **PWM-based class-D audio amplifiers** in consumer electronics. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Mapping `analogWrite()` Value to Duty Cycle **Given:** - NodeMCU PWM resolution $n = 10$ bits. - `analogWrite(pin, value)` with `value ∈ [0, 1023]`. **Step 1:** The total number of discrete duty cycle levels is $$N = 2^{n} = 2^{10} = 1024 \text{ levels}$$ **Step 2:** Each step corresponds to a duty cycle increment of $$\Delta D = \frac{100\%}{N - 1} = \frac{100\%}{1023} \approx 0.09775\%$$ **Step 3:** For any input value `value`, the corresponding duty cycle is $$D(\text{value}) = \frac{\text{value}}{1023} \times 100\%$$ **Step 4:** Worked example: If `value = 512` (mid-range), $$D(512) = \frac{512}{1023} \times 100\% \approx 50.05\%$$ **Step 5:** The average LED voltage becomes $$V_{avg} = V_{max} \cdot \frac{D}{100} = 3.3 \cdot 0.5005 \approx 1.652 \text{ V}$$ --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Required Series Resistor for LED Safety **Given:** - $V_{max} = 3.3$ V (NodeMCU GPIO HIGH). - $V_{f} = 2.0$ V (typical red LED forward voltage). - $I_{safe} = 15$ mA (desired LED current). **Step 1:** Voltage that must be dropped across the resistor is $$V_{R} = V_{max} - V_{f} = 3.3 - 2.0 = 1.3 \text{ V}$$ **Step 2:** Apply Ohm's Law to compute the resistance: $$R = \frac{V_{R}}{I_{safe}} = \frac{1.3 \text{ V}}{15 \times 10^{-3} \text{ A}} \approx 86.67 \;\Omega$$ **Step 3:** Choose the **nearest standard E12 resistor value** greater than or equal to this: $$R_{chosen} = 220 \;\Omega \; \text{(safest standard value)}$$ **Step 4:** Verify the actual current with the chosen resistor: $$I_{actual} = \frac{V_{R}}{R_{chosen}} = \frac{1.3}{220} \approx 5.91 \text{ mA}$$ This is well within the safe operating range, so the LED is protected. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Practical Tip:** Always use a **220 Ω or 330 Ω resistor** in your lab — even if the calculation suggests a smaller value. A slightly dimmer LED is far better than a burnt-out one during a viva! --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Code-to-Hardware Mapping | Code Construct | Hardware Effect | Underlying Timer Action | |---|---|---| | `pinMode(D1, OUTPUT)` | Enables GPIO5 driver | Configures multiplex to PWM mode | | `analogWrite(D1, 0)` | LED OFF (0% duty cycle) | Timer compare register = 0 | | `analogWrite(D1, 512)` | LED ~50% brightness | Compare register ≈ half of period | | `analogWrite(D1, 1023)` | LED fully ON (100%) | Compare register = period | | `delay(30)` | Visual fade step duration | Software pause, not PWM | | `for(int i=0; i<=1023; i++)` | Linear brightness ramp | Iterative `analogWrite` calls | ### 3.4 Complete Arduino Sketch (NodeMCU-Compatible) ```cpp // Experiment: Fading an LED using PWM on NodeMCU (ESP8266) // Course: PCCGL507 - Microprocessors and Microcontrollers Lab int ledPin = D1; // PWM-capable pin (GPIO5) void setup() { pinMode(ledPin, OUTPUT); // Configure D1 as output } void loop() { // ---- Fade IN: 0 -> 1023 ---- for (int duty = 0; duty <= 1023; duty++) { analogWrite(ledPin, duty); delay(3); // Small delay for smooth visual transition } // ---- Fade OUT: 1023 -> 0 ---- for (int duty = 1023; duty >= 0; duty--) { analogWrite(ledPin, duty); delay(3); } } ``` > [!NOTE] > The `delay(3)` is chosen empirically. Values between **2 ms and 10 ms** yield a perceptually smooth fade. Going below 2 ms may cause flicker; above 10 ms may produce visible stepping. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Hardware Connection Diagram (Block-Level) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[NodeMCU Board
ESP8266] -->|GPIO5 / D1| B[220 Ohm Resistor] B --> C[LED Anode +] C --> D[LED Cathode -] D --> E[GND Pin] A -->|3.3V Supply| F[USB Power Input] style A fill:#f9c74f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style B fill:#90be6d,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style C fill:#f8961e,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style D fill:#577590,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:1px style E fill:#43aa8b,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:1px style F fill:#277da1,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:1px ``` **Connection Sequence (Breadboard Wiring):** 1. Connect **NodeMCU D1** → one leg of **220 Ω resistor**. 2. Other leg of resistor → **LED anode (longer leg, +)**. 3. **LED cathode (shorter leg, −)** → **GND** pin on NodeMCU. 4. Power the NodeMCU via the **USB cable** connected to your laptop. --- ### 4.2 Program Flow Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start setup] --> B[pinMode D1 OUTPUT] B --> C[Enter loop] C --> D[for duty = 0 to 1023] D --> E[analogWrite D1 duty] E --> F[delay 3 ms] F -->|duty less than 1023| D F -->|duty equals 1023| G[for duty = 1023 to 0] G --> H[analogWrite D1 duty] H --> I[delay 3 ms] I -->|duty greater than 0| G I -->|duty equals 0| C style A fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style C fill:#118ab2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style D fill:#ffd166,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style G fill:#ef476f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px ``` --- ### 4.3 PWM Signal Behavior Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[analogWrite 0
Duty 0 percent] --> Q1[Always LOW
LED OFF] P2[analogWrite 256
Duty 25 percent] --> Q2[HIGH 1/4 of cycle
LED Dim] P3[analogWrite 512
Duty 50 percent] --> Q3[HIGH 1/2 of cycle
LED Medium] P4[analogWrite 1023
Duty 100 percent] --> Q4[Always HIGH
LED Full Bright] style P1 fill:#e63946,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style P2 fill:#f4a261,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style P3 fill:#e9c46a,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style P4 fill:#2a9d8f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style Q1 fill:#264653,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:1px style Q2 fill:#264653,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:1px style Q3 fill:#264653,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:1px style Q4 fill:#264653,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:1px ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Note on PWM Frequency:** NodeMCU's default PWM frequency is **1 kHz**. If you need a different frequency (e.g., to drive a servo), use `analogWriteFreq(newFrequency)` in the `setup()` function. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question 2024]** *What is Pulse Width Modulation? Define duty cycle and state the PWM resolution of the NodeMCU.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** Pulse Width Modulation (PWM) is a technique used to generate an analog-like output from a digital pin by varying the width of pulses in a square wave signal. The **duty cycle** is defined as the ratio of the ON time ($T_{on}$) to the total period ($T_{total}$) of the signal, expressed as a percentage: $$D = \frac{T_{on}}{T_{total}} \times 100\%$$ The **NodeMCU (ESP8266)** has a **10-bit PWM resolution**, meaning `analogWrite()` accepts values from **0 to 1023**, giving 1024 discrete brightness levels. The default PWM frequency is **1000 Hz**. *(Award: 1 mark for PWM definition, 1 mark for duty cycle + formula, 1 mark for resolution + value range.)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question 2024]** *Mention any two differences between the `analogWrite()` function of Arduino Uno and NodeMCU.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | Arduino Uno | NodeMCU (ESP8266) | |---|---|---| | PWM Resolution | **8-bit** | **10-bit** | | Value Range | 0 to **255** | 0 to **1023** | | Logic HIGH Voltage | 5 V | 3.3 V | | Default PWM Frequency | ~490 Hz / 980 Hz | **1000 Hz** | *(Award: 1.5 marks for any two correct differences with values, 1.5 marks for the remaining two.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Code + Hardware Approach **Q3. [KTU University Exam – Model Question 2024]** **(a)** Draw the circuit diagram to connect an LED to the **D1** pin of the NodeMCU through a 220 Ω resistor and explain the function of the series resistor. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Write an Arduino IDE sketch to **fade an LED in and out** continuously using PWM on the NodeMCU. Explain each line of the code. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Circuit Diagram (7 marks):** The circuit consists of: - **NodeMCU D1 (GPIO5)** connected to one terminal of a **220 Ω resistor**. - The other terminal of the resistor is connected to the **anode (+) of the LED** (longer leg). - The **cathode (−) of the LED** (shorter leg) is connected to the **GND** pin of the NodeMCU. - The NodeMCU is powered via the **USB cable** from the PC. **Function of the 220 Ω series resistor:** 1. **Current limiting:** Limits the current flowing through the LED to a safe value (≈5.9 mA) using Ohm's Law: $$I = \frac{V_{max} - V_{f}}{R} = \frac{3.3 - 2.0}{220} \approx 5.91 \text{ mA}$$ 2. **Protection of the GPIO pin and LED:** Prevents the LED from drawing excessive current that could damage either the microcontroller's GPIO or the LED itself. *(Award: 3 marks for diagram with all labels, 2 marks for series resistor function, 2 marks for Ohm's law calculation.)* **(b) Code (7 marks):** ```cpp int ledPin = D1; void setup() { pinMode(ledPin, OUTPUT); } void loop() { // Fade IN for (int duty = 0; duty <= 1023; duty++) { analogWrite(ledPin, duty); delay(3); } // Fade OUT for (int duty = 1023; duty >= 0; duty--) { analogWrite(ledPin, duty); delay(3); } } ``` **Explanation (line by line):** - `int ledPin = D1;` — Maps the GPIO pin D1 (GPIO5) to a variable for readability. - `pinMode(ledPin, OUTPUT);` — Configures D1 as an output pin in `setup()`. - `for (int duty = 0; duty <= 1023; duty++)` — Loop increases the duty cycle value from 0 to 1023, causing the LED to brighten gradually. - `analogWrite(ledPin, duty);` — Writes the PWM duty cycle to the pin. - `delay(3);` — 3 ms delay between steps for smooth visual fading. - The second `for` loop decreases the duty cycle from 1023 to 0, fading the LED out. - The `loop()` function repeats this fade-in/fade-out cycle continuously. *(Award: 3 marks for correct code, 2 marks for line-by-line explanation, 2 marks for inclusion of both fade-in and fade-out loops.)* --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Theoretical + Analytical Approach **Q4. [KTU University Exam – Model Question 2024]** **(a)** Explain the concept of **PWM duty cycle** with a neat waveform diagram. Derive the relationship between duty cycle and average output voltage. **(7 marks)** **(b)** For a NodeMCU driving an LED with a forward voltage of **2.0 V** and a **330 Ω** series resistor: - (i) Calculate the **peak LED current** when the duty cycle is **75%**. **(3 marks)** - (ii) Calculate the **average LED current** over one PWM cycle. **(2 marks)** - (iii) If the PWM frequency is **1 kHz**, determine the **ON time and OFF time** of each pulse. **(2 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) PWM Duty Cycle (7 marks):** PWM is a digital signal that switches between HIGH and LOW states. The **duty cycle** is the fraction of one period during which the signal is HIGH. $$D = \frac{T_{on}}{T_{on} + T_{off}} = \frac{T_{on}}{T_{total}} \times 100\%$$ **Waveform:** ``` ___ ___ ___ | | | | | | ___| |______| |______| |___ T_on T_off T_on T_off |<-- T_total -->| ``` **Relationship between duty cycle and average voltage:** The average DC voltage seen by the load is the area under the curve over one period: $$V_{avg} = \frac{1}{T_{total}} \int_{0}^{T_{total}} v(t)\, dt = V_{max} \cdot \frac{T_{on}}{T_{total}} = V_{max} \cdot D$$ For NodeMCU with $V_{max} = 3.3$ V: $$V_{avg} = 3.3 \cdot D$$ *(Award: 2 marks for definition, 2 marks for waveform, 3 marks for derivation.)* **(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):** **Given:** $V_{f} = 2.0$ V, $R = 330 \;\Omega$, $D = 75\% = 0.75$, $f_{PWM} = 1$ kHz. **(i) Peak LED current (when signal is HIGH):** $$I_{peak} = \frac{V_{max} - V_{f}}{R} = \frac{3.3 - 2.0}{330} = \frac{1.3}{330} \approx 3.94 \text{ mA}$$ **(ii) Average LED current:** $$I_{avg} = I_{peak} \times D = 3.94 \text{ mA} \times 0.75 \approx 2.95 \text{ mA}$$ **(iii) ON time and OFF time at 1 kHz:** Period: $$T = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{1000} = 1 \text{ ms}$$ ON time: $$T_{on} = D \times T = 0.75 \times 1 \text{ ms} = 0.75 \text{ ms}$$ OFF time: $$T_{off} = T - T_{on} = 1 \text{ ms} - 0.75 \text{ ms} = 0.25 \text{ ms}$$ *(Award: 3 marks for (i), 2 marks for (ii), 2 marks for (iii).)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:** > 1. **Wrong PWM range:** Writing `analogWrite(D1, 255)` (Arduino Uno syntax) on NodeMCU will still compile but the LED will only reach 25% brightness. **Always use 0–1023.** > 2. **Using D0:** D0 (GPIO16) does NOT support PWM. Using it gives no output — guaranteed zero marks. > 3. **No series resistor:** Connecting LED without a resistor can fry the GPIO or the LED. > 4. **Forgetting `pinMode()`:** Without `pinMode(pin, OUTPUT)`, the PWM hardware may not initialize correctly. > 5. **Confusing voltage and current:** Remember that PWM modulates **average voltage**; LED brightness is a function of **average current**, not peak current. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember - ✅ **PWM = Pulse Width Modulation** — emulates analog output using digital pulses. - ✅ NodeMCU has **10-bit PWM resolution** → `analogWrite(pin, 0 to 1023)`. - ✅ Default PWM frequency = **1000 Hz** (1 kHz) on NodeMCU. - ✅ Logic HIGH voltage on NodeMCU = **3.3 V** (NOT 5V like Arduino Uno). - ✅ **Always use a series resistor** (220 Ω or 330 Ω) with LEDs. - ✅ **Valid PWM pins:** D1, D2, D3, D4, D5, D6, D7, D8, RX. - ✅ **D0 (GPIO16) does NOT support PWM.** - ✅ **Duty cycle** $D = \frac{T_{on}}{T_{total}} \times 100\%$. - ✅ **Average voltage** $V_{avg} = V_{max} \cdot D$. - ✅ **Average current** $I_{avg} = I_{peak} \cdot D$. - ✅ Use `analogWriteFreq(newFreq)` to change PWM frequency if needed. - ✅ Use `delay(2–10 ms)` between brightness steps for smooth fading. - ✅ The fade effect is achieved by a **for-loop** sweeping the duty cycle up and down. - ✅ Real-world applications: **LED dimming, motor speed control, servo positioning, audio generation, SMPS**. > [!NOTE] > **Viva-Voce Quick Tip:** If the examiner asks *"Why does your LED not flicker even though PWM is switching it ON/OFF?"* — answer: *"Because the PWM frequency (1 kHz) is well above the human flicker fusion threshold (~60–100 Hz), the human eye integrates the pulses and perceives a steady brightness."*

topics.title: Reading the Value from an Analog Sensor Like an LDR and Printing It to the Serial Monitor Using NodeMCU
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCGL507 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Reading the Value from an Analog Sensor Like an LDR and Printing It to the Serial Monitor Using NodeMCU ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme PCCGL507 Lab syllabus**, **Module Experiment 2** deals with interfacing an **analog transducer (LDR — Light Dependent Resistor)** with the **NodeMCU (ESP8266-based Wi-Fi enabled microcontroller development board)** and observing the digitized sensor value through the **Arduino IDE Serial Monitor** running over a **UART (Universal Asynchronous Receiver-Transmitter)** virtual COM port. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus-Highlight Definition (KTU 2024):** > "Analog Sensor Interfacing" refers to the process of reading a continuously variable physical quantity (such as light intensity) using a transducer whose electrical parameter (resistance/voltage) changes proportionally, converting it into a digital count using the on-chip **SAR (Successive Approximation Register) ADC**, and transmitting the value to a host PC via **serial (UART) communication** at a standard **baud rate of 115200 bps**. ### 1.2 NodeMCU — Hardware Definition The **NodeMCU** is an open-source firmware and development kit built around the **Espressif ESP8266 Wi-Fi SoC (System on Chip)**. It exposes: - **1 × 10-bit SAR ADC** on pin **`A0`** (input range: **0 V – 3.3 V**) - Operating voltage: **3.3 V** (NOT 5 V tolerant on ADC pin) - Clock speed: **80 MHz** (default) / **160 MHz** (boost mode) - USB-to-Serial bridge: **CP2102** or **CH340** IC - 11 GPIO pins, 1 ADC channel ### 1.3 LDR — Sensor Definition An **LDR (Light Dependent Resistor)**, also called a **photoresistor**, is a passive analog transducer whose resistance **decreases** as the **incident light intensity (illuminance, in lux)** increases, following a **negative photoconductivity** behavior. | Parameter | Typical Value | |---|---| | Dark resistance (0 lux) | **1 MΩ – 10 MΩ** | | Bright light resistance (1000 lux) | **100 Ω – 1 kΩ** | | Response time | **20 ms – 30 ms** | > [!NOTE] > **Why LDR cannot be connected directly to a microcontroller?** > A microcontroller ADC can ONLY read **voltage**, not resistance. Hence, the LDR must be placed in a **voltage divider network** with a fixed reference resistor (typically **10 kΩ**) to convert the resistance change into a proportional voltage change. ### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — "The Dimmer Switch Translator" Imagine you are standing in a room and want to tell a friend in another room how bright the light is, but your friend can **only understand numbers from 0 to 1023**. - Your **eyes** perceive light intensity (continuous, analog). - The **LDR** is like a special meter whose "needle" position depends on light. - The **voltage divider circuit** acts like a translator that converts the needle position into a voltage (0 V – 3.3 V). - The **NodeMCU's ADC** is the calculator that converts the voltage into a number between **0 and 1023**. - The **Serial Monitor** is the loudspeaker that announces that number to you on the PC. So the entire chain is: **Light → LDR Resistance → Voltage → ADC Count → UART → Serial Monitor Text**. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Working Principle — Bullet-Wise Operational Logic **Step 1: Photon Absorption in LDR** - The LDR is fabricated using a **cadmium sulfide (CdS)** semiconductor lattice. - When photons strike the CdS surface, electrons in the **valence band** gain energy and jump to the **conduction band** (photoelectric effect). - More free electrons → **lower resistance**. **Step 2: Voltage Divider Formation** - The LDR is connected in series with a fixed **pull-down resistor R = 10 kΩ**. - The junction point between LDR and R is fed to the **A0 pin**. - As light increases → R_LDR decreases → V_A0 increases. **Step 3: ADC Sampling by NodeMCU** - The ESP8266's on-chip **SAR ADC** samples V_A0. - The ADC has **10-bit resolution**, producing **2¹⁰ = 1024 discrete levels**. - Sampling is performed by the `analogRead(A0)` Arduino function. **Step 4: UART Transmission** - The digital count is converted to **ASCII text** by `Serial.println()`. - Data is transmitted via **UART TX pin** at **115200 baud** to the PC's virtual COM port. - The **Arduino IDE Serial Monitor** decodes and displays the text. ### 2.2 Core 'Why' and 'How' Explained | Step | Why It Is Needed | How It Is Implemented | |---|---|---| | Voltage Divider | ADC reads voltage, not resistance | Series combination of LDR + 10 kΩ | | ADC Conversion | Microcontroller works digitally | 10-bit SAR ADC inside ESP8266 | | Serial Output | Human needs visible value | `Serial.begin(115200)` + `Serial.println()` | | Baud Rate Matching | TX-RX clocks must synchronize | Both ends configured at **115200 bps** | ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > All formulas below are **mathematically verifiable** from basic Ohm's Law and ADC quantization theory. | # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Variable Meaning | Unit | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Voltage Divider (LDR Branch) | $V_{A0} = V_{CC} \cdot \dfrac{R}{R + R_{LDR}}$ | V_CC = 3.3 V, R = 10 kΩ, R_LDR = LDR resistance | Volts (V) | | 2 | ADC Digital Count | $D = \text{round}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{A0}}{V_{REF}} \cdot (2^n - 1)\right)$ | V_REF = 3.3 V, n = 10 | Unitless (0–1023) | | 3 | ADC Quantization Step (LSB) | $\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{REF}}{2^n} = \dfrac{3.3}{1024}$ | Resolution per bit | Volts ≈ 3.22 mV | | 4 | Reverse Conversion (Digital → Voltage) | $V_{A0} = D \cdot \Delta V$ | D = digital count from Serial Monitor | Volts | | 5 | Reverse Conversion (Voltage → Resistance) | $R_{LDR} = R \cdot \left(\dfrac{V_{CC}}{V_{A0}} - 1\right)$ | After computing V_A0 | Ohms (Ω) | | 6 | UART Bit Period | $T_{bit} = \dfrac{1}{\text{Baud Rate}}$ | At 115200 baud, T_bit ≈ 8.68 µs | Seconds | | 7 | Total Bits Per UART Frame | $N_{frame} = 1_{start} + 8_{data} + 1_{stop}$ | Standard 8N1 configuration | 10 bits | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Pitfall (Exam Tip):** Many students incorrectly write the formula as $V_{A0} = V_{CC} \cdot \dfrac{R_{LDR}}{R + R_{LDR}}$. This is wrong because R_LDR is the **pull-up element connected to V_CC**, and R (10 kΩ) is the **pull-down to GND**, so the junction voltage is across R, not across R_LDR. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility This experiment is the **foundational building block** of: - **Automatic Street Light Control Systems** (smart city IoT) - **Smartphone Auto-Brightness Algorithms** - **Greenhouse Monitoring Systems** (light intensity logging) - **Solar Tracker Positioning** (sunlight detection for PV panel alignment) - **Museum / Gallery Preservation** (UV + visible light logging for artwork protection) --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Voltage Divider Output Voltage **Statement:** Derive the expression for the voltage appearing at the NodeMCU's **A0 pin** when an LDR (resistance R_LDR) is connected in series with a fixed resistor R = 10 kΩ across a 3.3 V supply. **Step 1 — Identify the Circuit Topology** The LDR is connected between **V_CC (3.3 V)** and the **A0 node**, while the fixed resistor R is connected between the **A0 node** and **GND (0 V)**. The two elements form a closed loop. **Step 2 — Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)** The sum of voltage drops around the loop equals V_CC: $$ \begin{aligned} V_{CC} &= V_{R_{LDR}} + V_{R} \\ V_{CC} &= I \cdot R_{LDR} + I \cdot R \\ V_{CC} &= I \cdot (R_{LDR} + R) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Solve for Loop Current I** Using Ohm's Law rearrangement: $$ \begin{aligned} I &= \dfrac{V_{CC}}{R_{LDR} + R} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Express V_A0 as the Voltage Across R** Since V_A0 is the potential at the junction w.r.t. GND, and R is the pull-down resistor, V_A0 = I · R: $$ \begin{aligned} V_{A0} &= I \cdot R = \dfrac{V_{CC} \cdot R}{R_{LDR} + R} \end{aligned} $$ This is the **final voltage-divider expression** showing that V_A0 depends inversely on R_LDR. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — ADC Digital Count from Analog Voltage **Statement:** Derive the formula for converting the analog voltage V_A0 (0 V – 3.3 V) into a 10-bit digital count. **Step 1 — Define ADC Resolution** For an **n-bit ADC**, the total number of quantization levels is: $$ \begin{aligned} L &= 2^n \\ L &= 2^{10} = 1024 \text{ levels} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Define Quantization Step (LSB Voltage)** Each step corresponds to a voltage increment of: $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta V &= \dfrac{V_{REF}}{L} = \dfrac{V_{REF}}{2^n} \\ \Delta V &= \dfrac{3.3}{1024} \approx 3.2227 \text{ mV} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Compute Digital Count** The digital output D is the integer number of LSBs that fit into V_A0: $$ \begin{aligned} D &= \text{round}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{A0}}{\Delta V}\right) \\ D &= \text{round}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{A0} \cdot 1024}{3.3}\right) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Numerical Sanity Check** For V_A0 = 0 V → D = 0. For V_A0 = 3.3 V → D = 1023. Valid. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Reverse Mapping: Digital Count → LDR Resistance This reverse formula is **exam-critical** for KTU viva questions. **Step 1 — Start from Voltage Divider Equation** $$ \begin{aligned} V_{A0} &= V_{CC} \cdot \dfrac{R}{R + R_{LDR}} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Solve for R_LDR** Divide both sides by V_A0 and rearrange: $$ \begin{aligned} \dfrac{V_{A0}}{V_{CC}} &= \dfrac{R}{R + R_{LDR}} \\ R + R_{LDR} &= \dfrac{R \cdot V_{CC}}{V_{A0}} \\ R_{LDR} &= \dfrac{R \cdot V_{CC}}{V_{A0}} - R \\ R_{LDR} &= R \cdot \left(\dfrac{V_{CC}}{V_{A0}} - 1\right) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Substitute Numerical Values** Assume the Serial Monitor shows D = 512 (mid-range). Then V_A0 = 512 × 3.2227 mV ≈ 1.65 V: $$ \begin{aligned} R_{LDR} &= 10000 \cdot \left(\dfrac{3.3}{1.65} - 1\right) \\ R_{LDR} &= 10000 \cdot (2 - 1) \\ R_{LDR} &= 10000 \; \Omega = 10 \; \text{k}\Omega \end{aligned} $$ This is consistent with the LDR's mid-range operating point. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Full Pin & Function Reference | Component | Pin / Label on NodeMCU | Direction | Function | Voltage Level | |---|---|---|---|---| | LDR Terminal 1 | **3V3 pin** | Power Input | Pull-up side | 3.3 V | | LDR Terminal 2 | **A0 pin** | Analog Output | ADC input | 0 V – 3.3 V | | 10 kΩ Resistor | A0 → **GND** | Pull-down | Forms voltage divider | 0 V – 3.3 V | | USB Data+ | D+ | Bidirectional | UART / Programming | 3.3 V logic | | USB Data− | D− | Bidirectional | UART / Programming | 3.3 V logic | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 System-Level Block Architecture (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Light Source
Sun or Lamp] --> B[LDR Sensor
CdS Photoresistor] B --> C[Voltage Divider
LDR and 10k Resistor] C --> D[NodeMCU ADC
A0 Pin 10 Bit SAR] D --> E[ESP8266 CPU
80 MHz Processor] E --> F[UART TX Module
115200 Baud] F --> G[USB to Serial Bridge
CP2102 or CH340] G --> H[PC Virtual COM Port
Arduino IDE] H --> I[Serial Monitor
Human Readable Display] style A fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900 style B fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366 style C fill:#e6ccff,stroke:#330099 style D fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600 style E fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000 style F fill:#ffe0cc,stroke:#993300 style G fill:#ccffff,stroke:#006666 style H fill:#ffccff,stroke:#660066 style I fill:#ffffcc,stroke:#666600 ``` ### 4.2 Voltage Divider Circuit Schematic (Mermaid) ```mermaid graph TD VCC[3V3 Supply
3.3 Volt DC] --> LDR1[LDR Top Terminal] LDR1 --> LDR2[LDR Bottom Terminal] LDR2 --> A0NODE[Analog Node A0] A0NODE --> R10K[Fixed Resistor 10k Ohm] R10K --> GND[Ground 0 Volt] A0NODE --> ADCPIN[NodeMCU A0 Pin
10 Bit ADC] ADCPIN --> MCU[ESP8266 SoC] style VCC fill:#ff9999,stroke:#990000 style LDR1 fill:#ffcc99,stroke:#cc6600 style LDR2 fill:#ffcc99,stroke:#cc6600 style A0NODE fill:#ffff99,stroke:#999900 style R10K fill:#99ccff,stroke:#003399 style GND fill:#cccccc,stroke:#333333 style ADCPIN fill:#ccff99,stroke:#336600 style MCU fill:#ff99cc,stroke:#990066 ``` ### 4.3 Software Execution Flow (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[Program Start] --> P2[Setup Function runs once] P2 --> P3[Serial Begin at 115200] P3 --> P4[Initialize ADC pin A0] P4 --> L1[Loop Function starts] L1 --> L2[Analog Read from A0] L2 --> L3[Store value in variable sensorValue] L3 --> L4[Serial Print sensorValue] L4 --> L5[Delay 500 milliseconds] L5 --> L1 style P1 fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#0066cc style P2 fill:#fff0e6,stroke:#cc6600 style P3 fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#006600 style P4 fill:#ffe6f3,stroke:#990066 style L1 fill:#f3e6ff,stroke:#660099 style L2 fill:#e6e6ff,stroke:#333399 style L3 fill:#ffffcc,stroke:#999900 style L4 fill:#ccffff,stroke:#009999 style L5 fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000 ``` ### 4.4 Photon-to-Digital Conversion Pipeline ```mermaid graph LR S1[Photon Energy E = h nu] --> S2[Electron Excitation in CdS] S2 --> S3[Resistance Drops in LDR] S3 --> S4[Voltage Divider Output V_A0] S4 --> S5[Sample and Hold in ADC] S5 --> S6[SAR Comparison Logic] S6 --> S7[10 Bit Digital Word] S7 --> S8[ASCII Conversion by Serial Print] S8 --> S9[UART Frame Transmission] S9 --> S10[Serial Monitor Display] style S1 fill:#ffe6cc style S2 fill:#ffd9b3 style S3 fill:#ffcc99 style S4 fill:#ffbf80 style S5 fill:#ffb366 style S6 fill:#ffa64d style S7 fill:#ff9933 style S8 fill:#ff8c1a style S9 fill:#ff8000 style S10 fill:#e67300,stroke:#333 ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** *What is an LDR? Explain its working principle in 3 marks.* **Model Answer:** An **LDR (Light Dependent Resistor)** is a passive analog transducer whose resistance varies inversely with incident light intensity. It is made of **cadmium sulfide (CdS)**. When photons strike the CdS surface, electrons in the valence band jump to the conduction band, increasing conductivity and **decreasing resistance**. In darkness, resistance is in the **MΩ range**; in bright light, it drops to a few hundred ohms. It is widely used in automatic streetlights and camera exposure meters. **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]** *Why is a voltage divider required to interface an LDR with a microcontroller?* **Model Answer:** A microcontroller's ADC can only measure **voltage (0 V – 3.3 V)** and cannot directly sense resistance. The voltage divider converts the LDR's resistance change into a proportional voltage change. The LDR and a fixed **10 kΩ resistor** are connected in series across **3.3 V**, and the midpoint is connected to the **A0 pin**. As light changes, V_A0 changes between 0 V and 3.3 V, which the ADC can digitize into a value between 0 and 1023. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) #### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper]** **(a)** Draw the interfacing circuit diagram to connect an LDR with a NodeMCU. Explain the role of each component. **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** The circuit consists of three main elements: 1. **LDR** — connected between **3V3** pin and **A0** pin of NodeMCU. 2. **10 kΩ fixed resistor** — connected between **A0** pin and **GND**. 3. **USB cable** — connects NodeMCU to PC for serial data and power. **Role of Each Component:** - **LDR:** Acts as the light-sensing element. Its resistance decreases with increasing light. - **10 kΩ Resistor:** Forms a voltage divider with the LDR to produce an analog voltage proportional to light intensity. - **NodeMCU A0 Pin:** Internally connected to a **10-bit SAR ADC** that converts the analog voltage into a digital count. - **USB-UART Bridge (CP2102/CH340):** Converts TTL UART signals from ESP8266 to USB signals for the PC. - **Arduino IDE Serial Monitor:** Displays the digital count at **115200 baud**. **(b)** Write the complete Arduino sketch to read the LDR value and print it to the Serial Monitor every 500 ms. Explain each function used. **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** The Arduino sketch is written in the C/C++ language and follows the standard `setup()` + `loop()` structure. ```cpp int sensorPin = A0; // LDR connected to analog pin A0 int sensorValue = 0; // Variable to store ADC reading void setup() { Serial.begin(115200); // Initialize UART at 115200 baud delay(1000); // Wait for Serial Monitor to open } void loop() { sensorValue = analogRead(sensorPin); // Read 0-1023 from ADC Serial.print("LDR Value: "); // Print label Serial.println(sensorValue); // Print value + newline delay(500); // Wait 500 ms } ``` **Function Explanations:** - **`Serial.begin(115200)`** — Configures the UART baud rate to 115200 bps; must match Serial Monitor setting. - **`analogRead(A0)`** — Triggers ADC conversion and returns a 10-bit integer (0–1023). - **`Serial.print()`** — Sends ASCII text without a newline. - **`Serial.println()`** — Sends ASCII text followed by carriage return + line feed (`\r\n`). - **`delay(500)`** — Pauses execution for 500 ms to prevent data flooding. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]** **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADC** architecture used inside the NodeMCU's ESP8266. **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** The **SAR ADC** is the type of ADC embedded inside the ESP8266 SoC. It uses a **binary search algorithm** to converge on the input voltage in **n clock cycles** (where n = resolution bits). **Architecture Diagram (Conceptual):** ``` Input V_A0 → Sample & Hold → Comparator (+) ← (−) DAC Output ↓ SAR Logic ↓ 10-bit Digital Output ``` **Working:** 1. The input voltage V_A0 is sampled and held constant. 2. The SAR register starts by setting the **MSB (bit 9) = 1** and all other bits = 0. 3. This digital code is fed to an internal **DAC**, producing a voltage V_DAC. 4. The comparator checks: **If V_A0 > V_DAC** → keep bit; **else** → reset bit. 5. The process repeats for bit 8, bit 7, ..., bit 0. 6. After 10 clock cycles, the SAR holds the final 10-bit digital word. **Advantages:** Low power, deterministic conversion time (10 cycles for 10-bit), small silicon area — ideal for IoT devices. **(b)** A student observes an LDR value of **780** on the Serial Monitor. Calculate the corresponding analog voltage and the LDR's resistance. **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Step 1 — Convert Digital Count to Voltage** $$ \begin{aligned} V_{A0} &= D \cdot \dfrac{V_{REF}}{1024} \\ V_{A0} &= 780 \cdot \dfrac{3.3}{1024} \\ V_{A0} &= 780 \cdot 0.0032227 \\ V_{A0} &\approx 2.514 \; \text{V} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Apply Voltage Divider Reverse Formula** $$ \begin{aligned} R_{LDR} &= R \cdot \left(\dfrac{V_{CC}}{V_{A0}} - 1\right) \\ R_{LDR} &= 10000 \cdot \left(\dfrac{3.3}{2.514} - 1\right) \\ R_{LDR} &= 10000 \cdot (1.3126 - 1) \\ R_{LDR} &\approx 3126 \; \Omega \approx 3.13 \; \text{k}\Omega \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Interpret the Result** A resistance of **~3.13 kΩ** corresponds to a **moderately bright ambient light** condition (typical indoor lighting produces LDR resistance in the 1 kΩ – 5 kΩ range). --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks (KTU 2024 Valuation Pattern):** > > 1. **Wrong voltage-divider formula direction** — Students frequently write $V_{A0} = V_{CC} \cdot \dfrac{R_{LDR}}{R + R_{LDR}}$, which is the voltage across the LDR, not the ADC node. (-2 marks) > > 2. **Confusing baud rate** — Setting `Serial.begin(9600)` in code but Serial Monitor at 115200 (or vice versa) → garbled output. Always **match both ends**. (-1 mark) > > 3. **Missing `delay()` in loop** — Causes Serial Monitor to be flooded with thousands of readings per second, making observation impossible. (-1 mark) > > 4. **Connecting LDR to 5 V** — ESP8266 ADC is **NOT 5 V tolerant**; applying 5 V permanently damages the chip. Always use the **3V3 pin**. (-2 marks) > > 5. **Forgetting to select the correct board and COM port** in Arduino IDE → upload fails. (-1 mark) > > 6. **Not declaring pin as input** — Unlike digital pins, `analogRead()` does NOT require `pinMode()`, but students often waste time adding it. (No marks lost, but time wasted.) > > 7. **Confusing NodeMCU pin labels** — `A0` on NodeMCU board silkscreen maps to **ADC channel 0**, NOT GPIO0. (-1 mark) --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Pin this above your lab bench):** - **NodeMCU = ESP8266 + CP2102/CH340 + Voltage Regulator + Breakout Board.** - **Only ONE ADC pin (A0)** is exposed; resolution = **10 bits → 0 to 1023**. - **ADC reference voltage (V_REF) = 3.3 V** (NOT 5 V, NOT configurable on basic NodeMCU). - **LDR must be used with a voltage divider**; a 10 kΩ pull-down resistor is standard. - **LDR resistance INVERSELY proportional** to light intensity (CdS photoconductivity). - **Formula:** $V_{A0} = 3.3 \cdot \dfrac{10k}{10k + R_{LDR}}$ - **Reverse Formula:** $R_{LDR} = 10k \cdot \left(\dfrac{3.3}{V_{A0}} - 1\right)$ - **Baud rate for Serial Monitor = 115200** (ESP8266 default). - **`analogRead()` does not require `pinMode()` declaration.** - **UART frame format = 8N1** (8 data bits, No parity, 1 stop bit). - **ADC type used = SAR (Successive Approximation Register)**. - **LSB size = 3.3 V / 1024 ≈ 3.22 mV.** - **Forbidden: Applying > 3.3 V to A0 pin** (damages ESP8266 permanently). - **Required Arduino IDE setup:** Board = "NodeMCU 1.0 (ESP-12E Module)", CPU = 80 MHz, Flash = 4M (1M SPIFFS), Port = correct COM port. - **Applications:** Automatic streetlights, IoT weather stations, museum lighting, solar trackers, smartphone auto-brightness. - **Code skeleton:** `setup()` runs ONCE → initialize Serial; `loop()` runs FOREVER → read ADC → print → delay.

topics.title: Displaying Numbers 0-9 on a 7-Segment Display Connected to Port 2 Using 8051
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGL507 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Displaying Numbers 0-9 on a 7-Segment Display Connected to Port 2 Using 8051

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **7-Segment Display** is an electronic display device composed of **seven Light Emitting Diodes (LEDs)** arranged in the shape of the digit **"8"**, supplemented by an **eighth LED** for the **decimal point (DP)**. By selectively energizing specific combinations of these segments, the decimal digits **0 through 9** and certain alphabetic characters (A, b, C, d, E, F) can be rendered.

In the context of the **Intel 8051 Microcontroller**, this experiment interfaces the 7-segment display with **Port 2 (P2.0 – P2.7)**, which is one of the four **bidirectional I/O ports** of the 8051. The microcontroller sends an **8-bit byte** to Port 2, where each bit drives one segment of the display. The mapping between the byte sent and the lit segments is governed by a **segment encoding lookup table**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This experiment falls under **Module 2 – I/O Interfacing and Programming**. The primary learning outcome is to demonstrate **memory-mapped I/O**, **lookup table indexing using `MOVC A,@A+DPTR`**, and **software-generated delays for persistence of vision**.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Light Switch Wall"

Imagine a **wall with 8 light switches** lined up in a row. Each switch controls a separate bulb arranged in the shape of a number. You cannot independently type the number "5" — you must manually flip the right combination of switches to make the bulbs **collectively** form the shape of "5". 

The 8051 microcontroller is **you**, flipping all 8 switches (the bits) **simultaneously** with one decisive hand movement (writing one byte to Port 2). The **8 light bulbs** are the **7 LED segments plus the decimal point**.

- If you flip switches **dp, g, f, e, d, c, b, a** = `0011 1111` → You light segments **a, b, c, d, e, f** → The display shows **"0"** (the middle segment g stays dark).
- If you flip switches `0000 0110` → Only segments **b** and **c** light up → The display shows **"1"**.

This "switch pattern" for each digit is **pre-calculated** and stored in memory. The 8051 simply **fetches the pattern** corresponding to the desired digit and pushes it onto Port 2 — this is the essence of **lookup-table-driven display**.

---

## 1.3 Physical Configuration Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Display Pinout (Standard 14-pin DIP package):**
> - **Pin 3** & **Pin 8**: Common Cathode (CC) / Common Anode (CA) — shared terminal.
> - **Pins 7, 6, 4, 2, 1, 9, 10, 5**: Segments **a, b, c, d, e, f, g, dp** respectively.

**Two Display Variants:**

| Parameter | Common Cathode (CC) | Common Anode (CA) |
|---|---|---|
| Common Terminal | Grounded (0 V) | Connected to +Vcc (5 V) |
| Segment ON Condition | Logic HIGH (1) on segment pin | Logic LOW (0) on segment pin |
| Preferred for 8051 | ✅ Yes (direct drive) | Requires inverter (ULN2003) |
| Current Flow | From segment pin → GND | From +Vcc → segment pin |

---

> [!TIP]
> **Why Persistence of Vision matters:** The human eye retains an image for approximately **1/16th of a second (≈ 62.5 ms)**. If we rapidly cycle through digits 0–9 with delays shorter than this threshold, the eye perceives all digits as *displayed simultaneously* — but for this static experiment, we display one digit at a time and hold it via a software delay.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Block Architecture

The 8051-to-7-Segment interface operates through four well-defined layers:

### Layer 1 — The Microcontroller Side
The 8051's **Port 2** is an **8-bit quasi-bidirectional I/O port**. When configured as output (by writing `1`s to all bits of P2, a common practice in 8051), each pin can **sink** or **source** current depending on the written logic level.

- Writing `0` to a bit → Pin pulls to **GND (0 V)**.
- Writing `1` to a bit → Pin weakly pulls up to **+Vcc (5 V)** through internal pull-ups.

### Layer 2 — Current Limiting Resistors
Each segment LED requires a **current-limiting resistor** (typically **220 Ω to 330 Ω**) in series. Without it, the LED draws excessive current and burns out.

### Layer 3 — The 7-Segment Display
The Common Cathode (CC) variant is preferred for direct 8051 interfacing. Sending **HIGH (1)** to a segment pin forward-biases that LED segment relative to the grounded common terminal, illuminating it.

### Layer 4 — Software Layer
The assembly program uses a **lookup table** stored in **Program Memory (ROM)**. The instruction **`MOVC A, @A+DPTR`** fetches the segment code corresponding to a digit (0–9) and outputs it to Port 2.

---

## 2.2 Segment Encoding Truth Table (Common Cathode)

The following encoding is **universally adopted** in KTU lab curricula:

| Digit | g | f | e | d | c | b | a | Hex Code (P2) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **0** | 0 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | `0x3F` |
| **1** | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | `0x06` |
| **2** | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 | `0x5B` |
| **3** | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | `0x7F` |
| **4** | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | `0x66` |
| **5** | 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 | `0x6D` |
| **6** | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 | `0x7D` |
| **7** | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 1 | `0x07` |
| **8** | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | `0x7F` |
| **9** | 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | `0x6F` |

> [!NOTE]
> **Bit Assignment Convention:** The MSB of the byte sent to P2 corresponds to segment **g**, and the LSB corresponds to segment **a**. The decimal point (dp) bit is **unused** (always 0) in this experiment.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas and constants — they appear directly in KTU viva and written exams.**

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **8051 Oscillator Frequency** | $f_{osc}$ | **MHz** (typically 11.0592 MHz) | Clock source for machine cycle |
| **Machine Cycle Period** | $T_{mc} = \dfrac{12}{f_{osc}}$ | **µs** | Time for 1 instruction cycle |
| **Software Delay Formula (Single Loop)** | $T_{delay} = (N \times 12) / f_{osc}$ | **seconds** | Where N = loop count |
| **Nested Loop Delay** | $T_{delay} = (M \times (N \times 12) / f_{osc})$ | **seconds** | M = outer loop count |
| **For 11.0592 MHz Crystal** | $T_{mc} = 12 / 11.0592 \times 10^{6}$ = **1.085 µs** | µs | Standard 8051 timing |
| **Persistence of Vision Threshold** | $\approx 16$ ms (1/60 sec) | milliseconds | Min delay between digit switches |
| **Current through LED segment** | $I_{LED} = \dfrac{V_{cc} - V_f}{R_s}$ | mA | Typical: $\dfrac{5 - 2}{220} = 13.6$ mA |
| **Decimal-to-7Seg Lookup Index** | `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` | — | DPTR = table base address |
| **Port Latch Register** | `P2` at SFR address **0xA0** | — | Writing `0xFF` configures as input |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The 7-segment display, though older, remains ubiquitous in:

- **Digital Clocks and Watches** — Hours/minutes/seconds display.
- **Calculators** — Result display.
- **Microwave Ovens & Washing Machines** — Countdown timers.
- **Industrial PLC Panels** — Process variable readouts.
- **Automotive Instrument Clusters** — Speedometer, odometer.
- **Vending Machines** — Price and product code displays.

> [!TIP]
> **In modern systems**, 7-segment displays are often replaced by **LCD/OLED modules** or **dot-matrix displays**, but the underlying principle of **character encoding via lookup tables** remains identical — making this lab exercise foundational for **display driver design**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Segment Code for Digit "5" (Common Cathode)

Let us exhaustively derive the hex code for displaying the digit **"5"** on a CC display.

### Step 1: Identify Lit Segments for "5"
The seven segments that visually compose "5" are:
- **a** (top horizontal) → ON
- **b** (top-right vertical) → OFF
- **c** (bottom-right vertical) → ON
- **d** (bottom horizontal) → ON
- **e** (bottom-left vertical) → OFF
- **f** (top-left vertical) → ON
- **g** (middle horizontal) → ON

### Step 2: Build the Bit Pattern
Map each segment to its corresponding bit position (a = bit 0, b = bit 1, ..., g = bit 6):

$$
\text{Segments ON} = \{a, c, d, f, g\}
$$

$$
\text{Bit Pattern} = (g, f, e, d, c, b, a) = (1, 1, 0, 1, 1, 0, 1)
$$

### Step 3: Compute the Binary Value

$$
\begin{aligned}
P2 &= 1 \cdot 2^{6} + 1 \cdot 2^{5} + 0 \cdot 2^{4} + 1 \cdot 2^{3} + 1 \cdot 2^{2} + 0 \cdot 2^{1} + 1 \cdot 2^{0} \\
P2 &= 64 + 32 + 0 + 8 + 4 + 0 + 1 \\
P2 &= 109 \text{ (decimal)} \\
P2 &= \texttt{0x6D (hexadecimal)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Cross-Verification
Checking against the lookup table → Digit "5" → `0x6D` ✅ **Match confirmed.**

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Software Delay for ~500 ms (Two-Nested Loop)

For displaying digits with a perceptible pause, we need approximately **500 milliseconds** of delay. Using a **two-nested loop** with an 8051 running at **11.0592 MHz**:

### Step 1: Machine Cycle Period

$$
T_{mc} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^{6}} = 1.085 \, \mu s
$$

### Step 2: Inner Loop Iteration Time

The inner loop contains:
- `MOV R1, #N` → **1 machine cycle**
- `DJNZ R1, HERE` → **2 machine cycles per iteration**
- The label `HERE:` refers to the `DJNZ` instruction itself.

For N iterations: Total cycles = $1 + 2 \times N$.

$$
T_{inner} = (1 + 2N) \times 1.085 \, \mu s
$$

### Step 3: Outer Loop Iteration Time

The outer loop wraps the inner loop:
- `MOV R0, #M` → 1 cycle
- Inner loop executes M times
- `DJNZ R0, OUTER` → 2 cycles per iteration

$$
T_{total} = [1 + M \times (1 + 2N) + 2] \times T_{mc} = [3 + M(1 + 2N)] \times 1.085 \, \mu s
$$

### Step 4: Solve for M and N

We desire $T_{total} \approx 500,000 \, \mu s$ (500 ms).

Assuming N = 255 (max register value):

$$
500000 = [3 + M(1 + 510)] \times 1.085 = [3 + 511M] \times 1.085
$$

$$
[3 + 511M] = \frac{500000}{1.085} \approx 460829
$$

$$
511M \approx 460826 \quad \Rightarrow \quad M \approx 902
$$

Since M must fit in an 8-bit register (max 255), we **cannot** achieve 500 ms with a single nested loop directly. We use a **triple-nested loop** OR set N = 200 to fit M within range:

$$
\text{Choosing } N = 200: \quad 511M \approx 460826 \Rightarrow M \approx 902 \text{ (still > 255)}
$$

### Step 5: Practical Approach (Triple-Nested Loop or Shorter Delay)

A more practical choice for KTU labs is **~250 ms delay**, achievable with M = 250, N = 220:

$$
T_{total} = [3 + 250 \times (1 + 440)] \times 1.085 = [3 + 250 \times 441] \times 1.085
$$

$$
T_{total} = [3 + 110250] \times 1.085 = 110253 \times 1.085 = 119,624 \, \mu s \approx 120 \, ms
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **In KTU lab exams**, students typically use a **two-nested loop with M = FF (255) and N = FF (255)** giving ~158 ms delay — which is sufficient for clearly visible digit transitions.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Algorithm Walkthrough

### Step 1: Initialize Pointer to Lookup Table

```asm
        MOV  DPTR, #LOOKUP    ; DPTR points to start of segment table
        MOV  R0, #00H         ; R0 = digit counter (0 to 9)
```

### Step 2: Main Loop — Display Digit

```asm
BACK:   MOV  A, R0            ; A = current digit value
        MOVC A, @A+DPTR       ; A = segment code from ROM table
        MOV  P2, A            ; Output to Port 2 → segments light up
        ACALL DELAY           ; ~150 ms delay for visibility
        INC  R0               ; Next digit
        CJNE R0, #0AH, BACK   ; Loop until R0 = 10, then restart
        SJMP START            ; Restart from 0
```

### Step 3: Delay Subroutine

```asm
DELAY:  MOV  R1, #0FFH        ; Inner loop counter = 255
LOOP2:  MOV  R2, #0FFH        ; Outer loop counter = 255
LOOP1:  DJNZ R2, LOOP1        ; Inner decrement (2 cycles)
        DJNZ R1, LOOP2        ; Outer decrement (2 cycles)
        RET                   ; Return to main
```

### Step 4: Lookup Table Definition

```asm
        ORG  0300H             ; Place table at Program Memory location 0300H
LOOKUP: DB   3FH, 06H, 5BH, 4FH, 66H, 6DH, 7DH, 07H, 7FH, 6FH
        END
```

---

## 3.4 Complete Analytical Matrix

| Step | Operation | Register/Variable Affected | Effect on Hardware |
|:---:|---|---|---|
| 1 | `MOV DPTR, #LOOKUP` | DPTR = base of table | Pointer ready for indexed fetch |
| 2 | `MOV R0, #00H` | R0 = 0 | Start with digit "0" |
| 3 | `MOV A, R0` | A = 0 | Prepare index for lookup |
| 4 | `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` | A ← ROM[DPTR + 0] | Fetch `0x3F` from code memory |
| 5 | `MOV P2, A` | P2 = 0x3F | Segments a,b,c,d,e,f light → "0" |
| 6 | `ACALL DELAY` | PC → DELAY subroutine | Display held for ~150 ms |
| 7 | `INC R0` | R0 = 1 | Advance to next digit |
| 8 | `CJNE R0, #0AH, BACK` | R0 ≠ 10 → jump back | Continue loop |
| 9 | After digit 9 → `SJMP START` | R0 resets to 0 | Cyclic display |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid diagram captures the **end-to-end data flow** from the 8051's program memory through Port 2 to the 7-segment display hardware:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Initialize R0 = 0] --> B[Set DPTR = LOOKUP Table Base]
    B --> C[Load Digit Index: MOV A, R0]
    C --> D[Fetch Segment Code via MOVC A, @A+DPTR]
    D --> E[Write Segment Code to P2 Register]
    E --> F[Segment Code Travels Through Port 2 Pins P2.0 to P2.6]
    F --> G[Current Limiting Resistors 220 ohm Each Line]
    G --> H[7 Segment LED Array Segments a to g]
    H --> I[Common Cathode Tied to Ground]
    I --> J[Segments Illuminate to Form Digit]
    J --> K[Call DELAY Subroutine approx 150 ms]
    K --> L[Increment R0 for Next Digit]
    L --> M{Is R0 equal to 10?}
    M -->|No| C
    M -->|Yes| N[Reset R0 to 0]
    N --> B
```
