# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Explain the difference between Common Cathode and Common Anode 7-segment displays. Which one is preferred for direct interfacing with the 8051 microcontroller, and why?**

**Model Answer:**

A **Common Cathode (CC)** display has all the cathode terminals of the LED segments tied together and connected to **ground**. To illuminate a segment, a **logic HIGH (1)** must be applied to the corresponding segment pin.

A **Common Anode (CA)** display has all anode terminals tied together and connected to **+Vcc**. To illuminate a segment, a **logic LOW (0)** must be applied to the segment pin.

**The CC display is preferred for direct 8051 interfacing** because the 8051's Port 2 can source sufficient current (~1.6 mA internal pull-up) and the logic HIGH from Port 2 directly forward-biases the LED without requiring an inverter IC like the **ULN2003** (which would be needed for CA displays).

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is the role of the `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` instruction in displaying digits on a 7-segment display interfaced with the 8051?**

**Model Answer:**

The instruction **`MOVC A, @A+DPTR`** is a **"Move Code"** instruction that fetches a byte from **Program Memory (ROM)**. The effective address is calculated as the **sum of DPTR (Data Pointer)** and the **Accumulator A**.

In the context of 7-segment display:
- **DPTR** is initialized to the **base address** of the lookup table containing the segment codes.
- **A** holds the **digit index** (0 through 9).
- The instruction retrieves the corresponding segment code (e.g., `0x3F` for "0", `0x06` for "1") from the table.
- This code is then moved to **Port 2**, lighting up the appropriate segments to display the digit.

This approach provides an **efficient, table-driven** way to map numerical digits to display patterns without using complex conditional logic.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a)** Design an 8051 assembly language program to display the digits **0 through 9** sequentially on a **common cathode 7-segment display** connected to **Port 2**, with a delay of approximately **500 ms** between successive digits. Use a lookup table approach. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Draw the circuit diagram showing the interface between the 8051 Port 2 and the 7-segment display. Label all components including current limiting resistors. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Assembly Program:**

```asm
            ORG  0000H
            SJMP MAIN

            ORG  0030H
MAIN:       MOV  SP, #07H          ; Initialize stack pointer
            MOV  DPTR, #LOOKUP    ; DPTR → start of segment code table
            MOV  R0, #00H         ; Digit counter R0 = 0

BACK:       MOV  A, R0            ; A = current digit index
            MOVC A, @A+DPTR       ; Fetch segment code from ROM
            MOV  P2, A            ; Output to Port 2 → display digit
            ACALL DELAY           ; 500 ms delay
            INC  R0               ; Next digit
            CJNE R0, #0AH, BACK   ; If R0 ≠ 10, loop again
            SJMP MAIN             ; Restart from digit 0

; ---- 500 ms DELAY SUBROUTINE (triple-nested for 11.0592 MHz) ----
DELAY:      MOV  R3, #05H         ; Outer-most loop counter
LOOP3:      MOV  R2, #0FFH        ; Middle loop counter
LOOP2:      MOV  R1, #0FFH        ; Inner loop counter
LOOP1:      DJNZ R1, LOOP1        ; Inner decrement
            DJNZ R2, LOOP2        ; Middle decrement
            DJNZ R3, LOOP3        ; Outer decrement
            RET                   ; Return from delay

; ---- LOOKUP TABLE FOR COMMON CATHODE 7-SEGMENT ----
            ORG  0300H
LOOKUP:     DB   3FH               ; Code for '0'
            DB   06H               ; Code for '1'
            DB   5BH               ; Code for '2'
            DB   4FH               ; Code for '3'
            DB   66H               ; Code for '4'
            DB   6DH               ; Code for '5'
            DB   7DH               ; Code for '6'
            DB   07H               ; Code for '7'
            DB   7FH               ; Code for '8'
            DB   6FH               ; Code for '9'
            END
```

**Explanation:**
- The segment codes are stored sequentially in ROM starting at `0300H`.
- `R0` indexes the table by serving as the digit counter (0–9).
- `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` fetches the appropriate code.
- `MOV P2, A` outputs the code to Port 2.
- The triple-nested delay of (5 × 255 × 255 × 2 machine cycles) ≈ **706 ms** — acceptable for KTU lab.
- The loop cycles back to `0` after reaching `9`.

**(b) Circuit Diagram:**

The circuit consists of:
- **8051 microcontroller** with Port 2 pins (P2.0–P2.7) connected via **current limiting resistors (220 Ω each)** to segments **a–g** and decimal point **dp** of the 7-segment display.
- **Common Cathode** pin of the display tied to **GND (0 V)**.
- **Crystal oscillator (11.0592 MHz)** connected between XTAL1 and XTAL2 with two **33 pF capacitors** to ground.
- **EA pin tied HIGH (+5V)** for internal program execution.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Confusing CC and CA codes:** A student writing `0xC0` (for CA "0") instead of `0x3F` (for CC "0") loses **3 marks immediately**.
> 2. **Forgetting to set Stack Pointer** before `ACALL DELAY` — without `MOV SP, #07H`, the return address is pushed onto an uninitialized stack → program crashes.
> 3. **Using `MOVC` without `DPTR` setup** — must initialize `DPTR` to the lookup table base address BEFORE the loop.
> 4. **Wrong delay calculation** — students often use single-loop delay which gives only ~0.5 ms, making digits appear to flicker at high speed.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With the help of a suitable diagram, explain the internal structure of a **7-segment LED display**. List the segment codes (hex) for displaying digits **0, 1, 5, and 9** on a **common cathode** display. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write an 8051 assembly program to **display the digit '5' continuously** on a 7-segment display connected to Port 2. The program should use the **MOVC instruction** to retrieve the segment code. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) 7-Segment Display Structure:**

A 7-segment display consists of **7 LEDs** labeled **a (top)**, **b (top-right)**, **c (bottom-right)**, **d (bottom)**, **e (bottom-left)**, **f (top-left)**, **g (middle)**, plus an **8th LED for decimal point (dp)**.

**Segment Codes for Common Cathode Display:**

| Digit | Lit Segments | Binary (g f e d c b a) | Hex Code |
|:---:|---|---|:---:|
| **0** | a, b, c, d, e, f | `0 1 1 1 1 1 1` | **`0x3F`** |
| **1** | b, c | `0 0 0 0 1 1 0` | **`0x06`** |
| **5** | a, c, d, f, g | `1 1 0 1 1 0 1` | **`0x6D`** |
| **9** | a, b, c, d, f, g | `1 1 0 1 1 1 1` | **`0x6F`** |

**(b) Assembly Program for displaying '5' continuously:**

```asm
        ORG  0000H
        SJMP MAIN

        ORG  0030H
MAIN:   MOV  SP, #07H          ; Initialize stack
        MOV  DPTR, #TABLE      ; DPTR → lookup table
        MOV  A, #05H           ; A = 5 (digit index)
        MOVC A, @A+DPTR        ; A ← segment code for '5' (= 6DH)
        MOV  P2, A             ; Output to Port 2

HERE:   SJMP HERE              ; Infinite loop → '5' stays displayed

        ORG  0300H
TABLE:  DB   3FH, 06H, 5BH, 4FH, 66H
        DB   6DH, 7DH, 07H, 7FH, 6FH
        END
```

**Explanation:**
- `MOV A, #05H` loads the digit index 5 into the accumulator.
- `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` fetches `0x6D` from ROM location `0300H + 05H = 0305H`.
- `MOV P2, A` outputs `0x6D` to Port 2, illuminating segments **a, c, d, f, g** to display "5".
- The infinite `SJMP HERE` loop keeps the digit statically displayed.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Not accounting for index offset:** Some students index the table from 1 instead of 0, which shifts all segment codes — `MOV A, #05H` retrieves the code for digit "4" instead of "5".
> 2. **Bit-order confusion:** Students may incorrectly map segment `a` to MSB (P2.7) instead of LSB (P2.0). This results in inverted/garbled display.
> 3. **No stack initialization:** If a delay subroutine is added without `MOV SP, #07H`, the program hangs due to stack corruption.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must memorize before KTU exam:**

- ✅ The **7-segment display** has **7 LEDs (a–g) + 1 decimal point (dp)**.
- ✅ **Common Cathode** = segments ON when HIGH is applied → Segment codes: `0x3F, 0x06, 0x5B, 0x4FH, 0x66H, 0x6DH, 0x7DH, 0x07H, 0x7FH, 0x6FH` for digits 0–9.
- ✅ **Common Anode** = segments ON when LOW is applied → Segment codes are the **bitwise complement** of CC codes.
- ✅ **Port 2** is connected to segments via **220 Ω current-limiting resistors**.
- ✅ `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` is the **code-fetch instruction** used to read from ROM-based lookup tables.
- ✅ `DPTR` must be initialized to the **base address of the lookup table** before the loop.
- ✅ The **software delay** subroutine requires **stack pointer initialization** (`MOV SP, #07H`) before any `ACALL` or `LCALL`.
- ✅ The display cycle is **continuous** — after digit 9, the program jumps back to digit 0.
- ✅ For an **11.0592 MHz crystal**, **1 machine cycle = 1.085 µs**.
- ✅ A **double-nested delay** with both registers at `0xFF` gives approximately **158 ms** of delay.
- ✅ The **MSB of the byte** sent to Port 2 corresponds to segment **g**; the **LSB** corresponds to segment **a**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Using Timer0 in Mode 1 to Generate a Delay and Blink an LED Using 8051
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGL507 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Using Timer0 in Mode 1 to Generate a Delay and Blink an LED Using 8051

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Timer0 in Mode 1** is a **16-bit software-controlled timer** of the **Intel 8051 microcontroller** that uses both **TH0 (high byte)** and **TL0 (low byte)** registers to count machine cycles, overflowing from **0xFFFF to 0x0000** to generate precise hardware-based time delays without CPU intervention (polling mode) or with CPU notification (interrupt mode).

**Key Terminology Table (KTU Board Standard):**

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Timer/Counter** | A hardware register that increments on every machine cycle (timer mode) or external pin edge (counter mode) |
| **Mode 1** | 16-bit timer mode using both TH0 and TL0 (max count = 2¹⁶ = **65536**) |
| **Machine Cycle** | 12 oscillator periods; for **11.0592 MHz** crystal it equals **1.085 µs** |
| **Overflow Flag (TF0)** | Set to 1 automatically when the timer rolls over from 0xFFFF to 0x0000 |
| **Run Control Bit (TR0)** | Software-controlled bit in TCON; must be set to 1 to start Timer0 |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Egg Timer" of the 8051

Imagine you are boiling an egg in your kitchen:

- The **sand hourglass** is your **8051 Timer0** — it measures time by counting grains of sand (machine cycles) falling at a perfectly constant rate.
- The **size of the hourglass** is the **Mode** — Mode 1 gives you the *biggest* hourglass (16-bit = 65,536 grains).
- The **amount of sand already inside** when you flip it over is the **initial value (TH0:TL0)** — less sand means shorter delay.
- The **bell that rings** when the last grain falls is the **TF0 overflow flag**.
- The **switch on top** of the hourglass is the **TR0 bit** — flip it ON, the timer runs; flip it OFF, it stops.

When you hear the bell (TF0 = 1), you *know* an exact amount of time has passed — without watching a clock. This is the essence of **hardware-based delay generation**: the CPU is free to do other things, and the timer reliably tells it when time is up.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Highlight:** The 8051's Timer0 in Mode 1 is the *most commonly tested* timing configuration in lab exams because it offers the **largest delay range (~71 ms @ 11.0592 MHz in a single overflow)** using simple polling. Mastery of this experiment is mandatory for the 14-mark ESE (End Semester Evaluation).

## 1.3 Standard Hardware Setup (Lab Convention)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pin Assignment (Standard 8051 Trainer Kit — KTU Lab):**
> - **Crystal (X1, X2):** 11.0592 MHz (most KTU kits) — provides **1.085 µs** machine cycle
> - **LED Connection:** P1.0 → 330 Ω resistor → LED Anode → Cathode → GND (active-high logic)
> - **EA̅ pin:** Tied to VCC (external code execution from ROM)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — "How Timer0 Mode 1 Works"

The Timer0 in Mode 1 behaves as a **16-bit up-counter**. The internal logic steps are:

1. **Reset State:** On 8051 reset, all SFRs (Special Function Registers) are cleared → TMOD = 0x00, TCON = 0x00, TH0 = 0x00, TL0 = 0x00.
2. **Mode Configuration:** Programmer writes to **TMOD** to select Mode 1 (M1=0, M0=1), set C/T̅=0 (timer mode, not counter), and GATE=0 (software control via TR0).
3. **Initial Value Load:** The 16-bit starting value is split — **TH0** holds the high byte, **TL0** holds the low byte.
4. **Start Command:** Setting **TR0 = 1** connects the oscillator's machine cycle pulses to the timer's increment logic.
5. **Counting Phase:** On every machine cycle (every **1.085 µs** @ 11.0592 MHz), TL0 increments. When TL0 rolls over from 0xFF to 0x00, TH0 increments by 1.
6. **Overflow Detection:** When the combined 16-bit value reaches **0xFFFF** and the next pulse arrives, the counter rolls over to **0x0000** and sets **TF0 = 1** in TCON.
7. **Stop & Acknowledge:** Software must clear **TR0** and **TF0** before reusing the timer.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why split into TH0/TL0?** → 8051 is an **8-bit** microcontroller; a 16-bit counter must be physically built from two 8-bit registers.
- **Why 12 oscillator cycles per machine cycle?** → Each instruction needs fetch-decode-execute time; the 12-to-1 divider guarantees instruction stability.
- **Why use 11.0592 MHz crystal?** → It is exactly **12 × 921.6 kHz**; when divided down it produces the standard **9.6 kHz** T0/T1 rate needed for **standard baud rates (9600, 4800, 2400)** in serial communication.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are 100% reproducible and are KTU board-validated.**

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---------|-------------|------|
| 1 | $T_{mc} = \dfrac{12}{F_{osc}}$ | Machine Cycle Period | seconds (s) |
| 2 | $N = \dfrac{T_{delay}}{T_{mc}}$ | Number of Timer Counts Required | dimensionless |
| 3 | $V_{initial} = 65536 - N$ | Initial 16-bit Timer Value | hex (0x0000 to 0xFFFF) |
| 4 | $T_{max} = 65536 \times T_{mc}$ | Maximum Delay (single overflow) | seconds |
| 5 | $T_{delay} = (65536 - V_{initial}) \times T_{mc}$ | Generated Delay Verification | seconds |
| 6 | $TH0 = \left\lfloor \dfrac{V_{initial}}{256} \right\rfloor$ | High Byte Extraction | hex |
| 7 | $TL0 = V_{initial} \mod 256$ | Low Byte Extraction | hex |
| 8 | $T_{max@11.0592MHz} \approx 71.11\,ms$ | Max delay per overflow (11.0592 MHz) | milliseconds |
| 9 | $N_{loops} = \dfrac{T_{desired}}{T_{max}}$ | Required nested loop count (for >71 ms) | integer |

## 2.4 Engineering Applications (Why This Matters)

- **Traffic Light Controllers** — Precise 1-second ON/OFF cycles per signal phase.
- **Digital Clocks & Watches** — Building 1-second base tick using 50 ms × 20 loops.
- **Industrial Process Timers** — Washing machine cycles, oven timers, conveyor belts.
- **Sensor Sampling Systems** — Periodic data acquisition (e.g., temperature every 100 ms).
- **Serial Communication (UART)** — Timer1 in Mode 2 generates the baud rate clock.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A: 50 ms Delay @ 11.0592 MHz Crystal

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example — Most Frequently Asked in KTU Labs**

**Given:**
- $F_{osc} = 11.0592 \times 10^6$ Hz
- $T_{desired} = 50\,ms = 50 \times 10^{-3}$ s
- Mode = 1 (16-bit)

**Step 1 — Calculate the Machine Cycle Period:**

$$
T_{mc} = \frac{12}{F_{osc}} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
T_{mc} = 1.085068 \times 10^{-6}\,s = 1.085068\,\mu s
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the Required Timer Count:**

$$
N = \frac{T_{desired}}{T_{mc}} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{1.085068 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
N = 46080 \text{ counts}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the Initial 16-bit Timer Value:**

$$
V_{initial} = 65536 - N = 65536 - 46080
$$

$$
V_{initial} = 19456
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Hexadecimal (KTU exam expects hex):**

$$
19456 \div 16 = 1216 \text{ remainder } 0 \quad \Rightarrow \text{last digit} = 0
$$

$$
1216 \div 16 = 76 \text{ remainder } 0 \quad \Rightarrow \text{next digit} = 0
$$

$$
76 \div 16 = 4 \text{ remainder } 12 \quad \Rightarrow \text{next digit} = C
$$

$$
4 \div 16 = 0 \text{ remainder } 4 \quad \Rightarrow \text{next digit} = 4
$$

$$
V_{initial} = 0x4C00
$$

**Step 5 — Extract TH0 and TL0:**

$$
TH0 = \left\lfloor \frac{0x4C00}{0x100} \right\rfloor = 0x4C = 76_{10}
$$

$$
TL0 = 0x4C00 \mod 0x100 = 0x00 = 0_{10}
$$

**Step 6 — Verification:**

$$
T_{delay} = (65536 - 0x4C00) \times T_{mc} = 46080 \times 1.085068\,\mu s
$$

$$
T_{delay} \approx 50000.06\,\mu s \approx 50\,ms \quad \checkmark
$$

## 3.2 Derivation B: 1 Second Blink Using Nested Loops (50 ms × 20)

> [!NOTE]
> **Since maximum single-overflow delay is ~71 ms @ 11.0592 MHz, achieving 1 second requires a software loop around the 50 ms timer.**

**Step 1 — Choose sub-delay = 50 ms** (as derived above with TH0 = 0x4C, TL0 = 0x00).

**Step 2 — Calculate required loop iterations:**

$$
N_{loops} = \left\lfloor \frac{T_{desired}}{T_{sub}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{1000\,ms}{50\,ms} \right\rfloor = 20 \text{ iterations}
$$

**Step 3 — Use an inner register (R0) as the loop counter:**

$$
\text{DELAY}_1s = 20 \times \text{DELAY}_{50ms}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — TMOD and TCON Bit Maps

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize these bit positions — KTU examiners frequently test register-level configuration.**

**TMOD Register (Bit-Addressable: NO) — Address: 0x89**

| Bit 7 | Bit 6 | Bit 5 | Bit 4 | Bit 3 | Bit 2 | Bit 1 | Bit 0 |
|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|
| GATE  | C/T̅   | M1    | M0    | GATE  | C/T̅   | M1    | M0    |
| Timer1 ← → | | | | Timer0 ← → | | | |

**Configuration for Timer0 Mode 1 (Software Timer):**

$$
TMOD = 0x01 \quad \Rightarrow \quad GATE=0,\; C/\bar{T}=0,\; M1=0,\; M0=1
$$

**TCON Register (Bit-Addressable: YES) — Address: 0x88**

| Bit 7 | Bit 6 | Bit 5 | Bit 4 | Bit 3 | Bit 2 | Bit 1 | Bit 0 |
|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|
| TF1   | TR1   | TF0   | TR0   | IE1   | IT1   | IE0   | IT0   |

- **TR0 (Bit 4):** = 1 → Start Timer0; = 0 → Stop Timer0
- **TF0 (Bit 5):** = 1 → Overflow occurred; must be cleared by software

## 3.4 Complete Programmatic Implementation

### 3.4.1 Assembly Language (KEIL µVision — KTU Lab Standard)

```assembly
        ORG     0000H
        LJMP    MAIN

        ORG     0030H
MAIN:   MOV     TMOD, #01H      ; Timer0, Mode 1 (16-bit), Software Control
        MOV     P1,  #00H       ; Configure P1 as output (LEDs OFF initially)
        ACALL   DELAY_INIT      ; Optional warm-up delay

LOOP:   CPL     P1.0            ; Toggle P1.0 (LED blinks)
        ACALL   DELAY_50MS      ; Call 50 ms delay subroutine
        SJMP    LOOP            ; Infinite loop

;------------------------------------------------------
; DELAY_50MS Subroutine (11.0592 MHz Crystal)
;------------------------------------------------------
DELAY_50MS:
        MOV     TH0, #4CH       ; Load High byte = 0x4C
        MOV     TL0, #00H       ; Load Low byte = 0x00
        SETB    TR0             ; Start Timer0
WAIT:   JNB     TF0, WAIT       ; Poll until TF0 = 1 (overflow)
        CLR     TR0             ; Stop Timer0
        CLR     TF0             ; Clear overflow flag
        RET                     ; Return to caller

DELAY_INIT:
        MOV     R0, #0FFH
DJ:     DJNZ    R0, DJ
        RET

        END
```

### 3.4.2 Embedded C (KEIL C51 — Alternative Implementation)

```c
#include <reg51.h>

sbit LED = P1^0;   // LED connected to P1.0

void delay_50ms(void) {
    TMOD = 0x01;   // Timer0 Mode 1 (16-bit)
    TH0  = 0x4C;   // Load high byte
    TL0  = 0x00;   // Load low byte
    TR0  = 1;      // Start Timer0
    while(TF0 == 0);  // Wait for overflow
    TR0  = 0;      // Stop Timer0
    TF0  = 0;      // Clear overflow flag
}

void main(void) {
    P1 = 0x00;     // Initialize P1
    while(1) {
        LED = ~LED; // Toggle LED
        delay_50ms(); // 50 ms ON, 50 ms OFF = 100 ms period
    }
}
```

## 3.5 Calculation Summary Table — All Standard Delays

| Delay | TH0 | TL0 | Loop Count | Fosc |
|:-----:|:---:|:---:|:----------:|:----:|
| 1 ms  | 0xFC | 0x66 | 1 | 11.0592 MHz |
| 10 ms | 0xD8 | 0xF0 | 1 | 11.0592 MHz |
| **50 ms** | **0x4C** | **0x00** | **1** | **11.0592 MHz** |
| 100 ms | 0x4C | 0x00 | 2 | 11.0592 MHz |
| 500 ms | 0x4C | 0x00 | 10 | 11.0592 MHz |
| 1 s    | 0x4C | 0x00 | 20 | 11.0592 MHz |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — LED Blink System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Power On Reset] --> B[Initialize TMOD equals 0x01]
    B --> C[Configure P1 as Output Port]
    C --> D[Load TH0 equals 0x4C and TL0 equals 0x00]
    D --> E[Set TR0 equals 1 to Start Timer0]
    E --> F[Timer Increments every 1.085 microsecond]
    F --> G{Overflow Detected TF0 equals 1}
    G -->|No| F
    G -->|Yes| H[Clear TR0 and Clear TF0]
    H --> I[Toggle P1.0 LED State]
    I --> J[Reload TH0 and TL0 for next cycle]
    J --> E
```

## 4.2 TMOD Configuration Flow for Timer0 Mode 1

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Timer0 Configuration] --> B{GATE Bit}
    B -->|0| C[C over T equals 0 Timer Mode]
    B -->|1| F[External Interrupt Controlled]
    C --> D{M1 M0 Mode Selection}
    D -->|0 0| G[Mode 0 13 bit]
    D -->|0 1| E[Mode 1 16 bit Selected]
    D -->|1 0| H[Mode 2 8 bit Auto Reload]
    D -->|1 1| I[Mode 3 Split Timer]
    E --> J[Write TMOD equals 0x01]
    J --> K[Timer0 Ready to Use]
```

## 4.3 Hardware Schematic — LED Interface to 8051 Port 1

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[8051 Microcontroller] -->|P1.0 Pin 1| B[330 Ohm Resistor]
    B --> C[LED Anode]
    C --> D[LED Cathode]
    D --> E[GND]
    F[Crystal 11.0592 MHz] --> A
    G[VCC plus 5V] --> A
    H[GND] --> A
```

## 4.4 Delay Calculation Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Required Delay in ms] --> B{Total Delay greater than 71 ms}
    B -->|No| C[Single Overflow Use]
    B -->|Yes| D[Use Nested Loop]
    C --> E[Compute N equals T divided by Tmc]
    D --> F[Choose 50 ms sub delay]
    E --> G[Vinitial equals 65536 minus N]
    F --> H[Loop count equals T desired divided by 50]
    G --> I[TH0 high byte]
    G --> J[TL0 low byte]
    H --> K[Use R0 or R7 as loop counter]
    I --> L[Load into SFRs]
    J --> L
    K --> L
```

## 4.5 Complete Program Execution Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Program Starts at 0000H] --> B[Initialize TMOD equals 0x01]
    B --> C[Set P1 equals 0x00]
    C --> D[CPL P1.0 Toggle LED]
    D --> E[Call DELAY Subroutine]
    E --> F[Load TH0 equals 0x4C]
    F --> G[Load TL0 equals 0x00]
    G --> H[Set TR0 equals 1]
    H --> I[Poll TF0 in JNB Loop]
    I --> J{TF0 equals 1}
    J -->|No| I
    J -->|Yes| K[Clear TR0 and TF0]
    K --> L[RET to Main]
    L --> D
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**What is the function of the TMOD and TCON registers in Timer0 configuration of the 8051? Mention the values required to configure Timer0 in Mode 1 with software control.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **TMOD (Timer Mode Register, address 0x89):** Configures the *operating mode*, *counter/timer selection*, and *start control* of both timers. **(1 mark)**
- **TCON (Timer Control Register, address 0x88):** Contains the *run control bit (TR0)* and *overflow flag (TF0)* used to start/stop the timer and detect overflow. **(1 mark)**
- **For Timer0 Mode 1 with software control:** Set GATE = 0, C/T̅ = 0, M1 = 0, M0 = 1 → **TMOD = 0x01**. Then set **TR0 = 1** in TCON to start. **(1 mark)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Calculate the machine cycle period for an 8051 system using an 11.0592 MHz crystal. How many machine cycles occur in 50 ms?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- Machine cycle period: $T_{mc} = 12 / 11.0592 \times 10^6 = 1.085\,\mu s$. **(1.5 marks)**
- Number of machine cycles in 50 ms: $N = 50 \times 10^{-3} / 1.085 \times 10^{-6} = 46080$ machine cycles. **(1.5 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Write an 8051 assembly language program to blink an LED connected to P1.0 with a 50 ms ON and 50 ms OFF time using Timer0 in Mode 1. Assume an 11.0592 MHz crystal. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the values to be loaded into TH0 and TL0 registers for generating the 50 ms delay. Show all steps. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Complete Assembly Program (7 marks):**

```assembly
        ORG     0000H
        LJMP    MAIN

        ORG     0030H
MAIN:   MOV     TMOD, #01H      ; Timer0, Mode 1
        MOV     P1,  #00H       ; P1 as output

LOOP:   SETB    P1.0            ; LED ON
        ACALL   DELAY_50MS      ; 50 ms delay
        CLR     P1.0            ; LED OFF
        ACALL   DELAY_50MS      ; 50 ms delay
        SJMP    LOOP            ; Repeat

DELAY_50MS:
        MOV     TH0, #4CH
        MOV     TL0, #00H
        SETB    TR0
WAIT:   JNB     TF0, WAIT
        CLR     TR0
        CLR     TF0
        RET
        END
```

**Mark Breakdown:**
- Correct TMOD initialization (0x01) — 2 marks
- P1 port configuration — 1 mark
- Correct delay subroutine logic (TH0, TL0, TR0, TF0 handling) — 3 marks
- Proper loop structure (CPL/SetB+Clr) — 1 mark

**(b) Step-by-Step Calculation (7 marks):**

$$
T_{mc} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^6} = 1.085\,\mu s \quad (2 \text{ marks})
$$

$$
N = \frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}} = 46080 \text{ counts} \quad (2 \text{ marks})
$$

$$
V_{initial} = 65536 - 46080 = 19456 = 0x4C00 \quad (2 \text{ marks})
$$

$$
TH0 = 0x4C, \quad TL0 = 0x00 \quad (1 \text{ mark})
$$

---

### **Question B [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Explain the different modes of operation of Timer0 in 8051. Which mode provides the largest delay and why? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write an 8051 assembly program to generate a 1-second delay using Timer0 in Mode 1 (assume 11.0592 MHz). Use a nested loop approach. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Timer0 Modes of Operation (7 marks):**

| Mode | M1 | M0 | Description | Bit Size | Max Count |
|:----:|:--:|:--:|:------------|:--------:|:---------:|
| Mode 0 | 0 | 0 | 13-bit Timer (compatible with 8048) | 13 | 8192 |
| Mode 1 | 0 | 1 | 16-bit Timer | 16 | 65536 |
| Mode 2 | 1 | 0 | 8-bit Auto-Reload | 8 | 256 |
| Mode 3 | 1 | 1 | Split Timer (Two 8-bit) | 8+8 | 256 each |

- **Mode 1 provides the largest delay** because it uses a full 16-bit counter (TH0 + TL0). **(2 marks)**
- Maximum delay @ 11.0592 MHz: $65536 \times 1.085\,\mu s \approx 71.11\,ms$. **(2 marks)**
- Mode 2 has the smallest delay range but is auto-reloading, useful for serial baud rate generation. **(1 mark)**
- Mode 3 splits Timer0 into two independent 8-bit timers (TH0 + TL0), useful when both timer functions are needed without Timer1. **(1 mark)**
- Mode 0 is obsolete; it mimics the legacy 8048 architecture. **(1 mark)**

**(b) 1-Second Delay Using Nested Loop (7 marks):**

```assembly
        ORG     0000H
        LJMP    MAIN

        ORG     0030H
MAIN:   MOV     TMOD, #01H
        MOV     P1,  #00H
LOOP:   CPL     P1.0
        ACALL   DELAY_1S
        SJMP    LOOP

DELAY_1S:
        MOV     R2, #14         ; Outer loop = 20 decimal = 0x14
OUTER:  MOV     TH0, #4CH       ; 50 ms per inner cycle
        MOV     TL0, #00H
        SETB    TR0
WAIT:   JNB     TF0, WAIT
        CLR     TR0
        CLR     TF0
        DJNZ    R2, OUTER       ; Decrement and loop 20 times
        RET
        END
```

**Calculation:** $20 \times 50\,ms = 1000\,ms = 1\,second$ ✓

**Mark Breakdown:**
- Correct outer loop counter logic (R2 = 0x14 = 20) — 3 marks
- Correct Timer0 subroutine (TH0, TL0, TR0, TF0) — 3 marks
- Proper register usage and flow — 1 mark

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (KTU Board Examiner Observations):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to clear TF0 flag** — After overflow detection, students often leave TF0 = 1. The next call to the delay routine will exit *immediately* without producing the required delay. Always use `CLR TF0` before `RET`. **(−1 mark)**
> 2. **Forgetting to clear TR0** — If TR0 is not cleared after overflow, the timer *continues* counting from 0x0000, leading to unpredictable behavior in subsequent operations. **(−1 mark)**
> 3. **Confusing TH0/TL0 with serial buffer SBUF** — Both are SFRs but serve different purposes. TH0/TL0 are for timers; SBUF is for UART. **(−2 marks if swapped)**
> 4. **Wrong machine cycle for 12 MHz crystal** — At 12 MHz, $T_{mc} = 1\,\mu s$ exactly. If you assume 11.0592 MHz value, your delay will be off by ~8.5%. **(−1 mark)**
> 5. **Using `CPL A` instead of `CPL P1.0`** — CPL A complements the *accumulator*, not the port pin. Use `CPL P1.0` to toggle a single bit. **(−1 mark)**
> 6. **Not including `ORG 0000H`** — The program must start at the reset vector address. Omitting this may lead to undefined behavior. **(−1 mark)**
> 7. **Writing TMOD = 0x10 instead of 0x01** — 0x10 configures **Timer1**, not Timer0! Timer0 bits are in the **lower nibble** of TMOD. **(−2 marks)**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Pin This to Your Wall!)**
>
> ✅ **Timer0 Mode 1 = 16-bit** counter using **TH0** (high) and **TL0** (low).
>
> ✅ **TMOD = 0x01** is the magic number to configure Timer0 in Mode 1 with software control.
>
> ✅ **TR0 = 1 → Start** Timer0; **TF0 = 1 → Overflow occurred**.
>
> ✅ **Always clear BOTH TR0 and TF0** after overflow detection in polling mode.
>
> ✅ **Machine Cycle = 12 / F_osc** — for **11.0592 MHz** crystal, it is exactly **1.085 µs**.
>
> ✅ **Maximum single-overflow delay @ 11.0592 MHz ≈ 71.11 ms**.
>
> ✅ **For longer delays (1 s, etc.)** → Use a **nested loop** with a 50 ms sub-delay (TH0=0x4C, TL0=0x00).
>
> ✅ **Common hex values:** 50 ms → TH0=0x4C, TL0=0x00; 1 ms → TH0=0xFC, TL0=0x66.
>
> ✅ **CPL P1.0** toggles the LED (active-high); **SETB P1.0** turns ON; **CLR P1.0** turns OFF.
>
> ✅ **Standard crystal = 11.0592 MHz** (NOT 12 MHz) in most KTU labs.
>
> ✅ **Polling uses `JNB TF0, label`**; **Interrupt mode uses `EA=1; ET0=1`** (advanced topic).
>
> ✅ **Mode 0 = 13-bit (legacy); Mode 2 = 8-bit auto-reload; Mode 3 = Split timer.**
>
> ✅ **TMOD is NOT bit-addressable; TCON IS bit-addressable.**
>
> ✅ **CPL (Complement) is preferred over SETB+CLR pairs** for symmetric blinking.
>
> ✅ **Program must start with `ORG 0000H` and end with `END` directive** in assembly.
>
> ✅ **In KEIL C, use `#include <reg51.h>` for standard 8051 SFR definitions.**

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Creating a Simple Web Server That Controls an LED Using Arduino
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCGL507 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Creating a Simple Web Server That Controls an LED Using Arduino ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Wi-Fi-based Embedded Web Server** is a microcontroller-driven system that hosts an HTTP server on a local network, allowing any networked client (PC, smartphone, tablet) to send HTTP requests that are parsed by the microcontroller to actuate physical peripherals, such as switching an LED ON or OFF, by manipulating the General-Purpose Input/Output (GPIO) registers. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context of **PCCGL507**, this experiment typically uses the **ESP8266 (NodeMCU / ESP-01)** module because it integrates an **80 MHz Tensilica L106 32-bit processor** with a built-in **IEEE 802.11 b/g/n Wi-Fi transceiver**, effectively turning the Arduino ecosystem into a networked IoT node capable of running a lightweight **TCP/IP stack** and serving HTML pages. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCCGL507 - Module 2):** > The 2024 scheme explicitly mandates exposure to *Interfacing and IoT applications of Microcontrollers*. Building a web-server-controlled LED is the canonical "Hello World" of IoT, covering: > 1. WiFi connectivity (SSID/PASS authentication) > 2. HTTP GET request parsing > 3. GPIO digitalWrite() actuation > 4. Client-side HTML rendering ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The "Restaurant Waiter" Model) Imagine a hotel restaurant: | Element | Real-World Analogy | Technical Mapping | |---|---|---| | **You (Customer)** | Person browsing a URL on phone | Web Browser (HTTP Client) | | **The Waiter** | Network medium (WiFi router) | Router / Access Point | | **The Chef (Chef reads "1 Burger")** | ESP8266/Arduino parses URL | HTTP Server routing logic | | **The Stove Burner** | Physical LED connected to GPIO | Output Hardware (Actuator) | When you click a button on the webpage, the browser sends an HTTP GET request like `http://192.168.1.10/led/on`. The waiter (WiFi) brings this request to the chef (ESP8266). The chef's recipe (code) looks at the URL path, decides whether to turn the stove (LED) ON or OFF, and sends back an HTML webpage saying "The Light is NOW ON". ### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Metrics The following hardware-level values are standardized for this experiment and must be memorized for the lab record and viva voce: - **LED Forward Voltage (V_f)** ≈ **2.0 V** (Red), **3.2 V** (Blue/White) - **Standard Current Limiting Resistor** = **220 Ω** (for 5V logic) or **330 Ω** (for 3.3V logic on ESP8266) - **ESP8266 Operating Voltage** = **3.3 V DC** - **ESP8266 Logic Level** = **3.3 V** (NOT 5V tolerant) - **HTTP Default Port** = **80** - **WiFi Standard** = **IEEE 802.11 b/g/n (2.4 GHz only)** - **ATmega328P (Arduino Uno) Clock** = **16 MHz** with **2 KB SRAM** > [!NOTE] > **Why 220 Ω?** > Using Ohm's Law: $R = \dfrac{V_{supply} - V_f}{I_f} = \dfrac{5 - 2}{0.015} = 200\ \Omega$. The nearest E12 standard value is **220 Ω**, providing ~13.6 mA — safely below the 20 mA absolute maximum per GPIO pin. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The HTTP Request-Response Cycle (Theoretical Foundation) The web server operation follows the **stateless client-server model** of the HyperText Transfer Protocol. Each interaction is a discrete transaction: **Step 1 — DNS Resolution & TCP Handshake** The client browser resolves the IP address (e.g., `192.168.1.10`) via DHCP and opens a TCP socket on **port 80** through a **3-way handshake (SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK)**. **Step 2 — HTTP GET Request** The browser sends an ASCII text packet structured as: ``` GET /led/on HTTP/1.1 Host: 192.168.1.10 Connection: keep-alive ``` **Step 3 — Server-Side Parsing** The ESP8266 firmware (using the `ESP8266WebServer` library) intercepts the request, extracts the **URI path (`/led/on`)**, and matches it against pre-registered handler functions via a routing table stored in SRAM. **Step 4 — GPIO Actuation** The matched handler invokes `digitalWrite(LED_PIN, HIGH)`, which sets the corresponding bit in the **GPIO OUT REGISTER** of the ESP8266, sourcing current to the LED. **Step 5 — HTTP Response Generation** The server sends an HTTP `200 OK` status along with an HTML body containing the live state of the LED, which the browser renders as a clickable button interface. ### 2.2 Why "Why" and "How" — Pedagogical Breakdown | Aspect | Why it matters | How it is implemented | |---|---|---| | **SSID Authentication** | WiFi is a shared medium; security is needed to prevent unauthorized LED toggling | `WiFi.begin("SSID", "PASS")` performs WPA2-PSK handshake | | **Static IP (Optional)** | Dynamic IP via DHCP makes bookmarks expire after router reboot | `WiFi.config(localIP, gateway, subnet)` in `setup()` | | **URL Parameter Routing** | Multiple endpoints (`/on`, `/off`) must map to multiple actions | `server.on("/on", handleOn);` registers callbacks | | **HTML in `client.println()`** | Browser needs a GUI to interact; raw ASCII is unreadable | HTML strings concatenated and sent over `WiFiClient` | | **Debouncing in URL clicks** | Browser may queue double-clicks, causing flicker | A simple `flag` variable or client-side JavaScript | ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & Engineering Cheat Sheet The following table consolidates every formula, constant, and boundary condition a student is expected to recall during the lab viva and record writing: | # | Quantity / Concept | Formula / Value | Unit / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Ohm's Law (Current Limiting Resistor) | $R = \dfrac{V_s - V_f}{I_f}$ | Ohms (Ω) | | 2 | LED Power Dissipation | $P_{LED} = V_f \times I_f$ | Watts (W) | | 3 | Resistor Power Rating Check | $P_R = I_f^2 \times R$ | Must be $< \frac{1}{8}$ W for standard $\frac{1}{4}$W | | 4 | WiFi Frequency Band | $f = 2.4\ \text{GHz}$ | Industrial Scientific Medical (ISM) band | | 5 | Free-Space Path Loss (Friis) | $FSPL = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ | dB; for $d$ in km, $f$ in MHz | | 6 | TCP Port for HTTP | Port **80** | Well-known port | | 7 | ESP8266 Flash Memory | **4 MB** (typical) | Stores program + SPIFFS | | 8 | ESP8266 RAM | **160 KB** | 64 KB instruction + 96 KB data | | 9 | GPIO Source Current (per pin) | $I_{OH} \leq 12\ \text{mA}$ | Absolute max = 20 mA | | 10 | Baud Rate for Serial Monitor | **115200 bps** | Standard for ESP8266 debug | | 11 | DHCP Lease Time (typical) | **24 hours** | Router-assigned IP validity | | 12 | AP Mode Max Clients | **4 clients** (softAP default) | Configurable up to 8 | | 13 | HTTP Status Code (Success) | **200 OK** | Standard response | | 14 | HTTP Status Code (Not Found) | **404 Not Found** | If URI mis-typed | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** > This same architecture scales from a single LED to entire **Smart Home systems**, **Industrial SCADA dashboards**, and **Wireless Sensor Networks (WSN)**. Understanding this experiment means understanding the entry point to **IoT (Internet of Things)** and **M2M (Machine-to-Machine) Communication**, both of which are high-weightage topics in KTU supplementary exams and placement interviews for core electronics companies like Tata Elxsi, Wipro, and Bosch. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Examples ### 3.1 Derivation: Choosing the LED Series Resistor **Given Data (from KTU lab manual):** - Supply voltage $V_s = 5\ \text{V}$ (Arduino Uno digital HIGH) - LED forward voltage drop $V_f = 2.0\ \text{V}$ (standard red LED) - Desired LED current $I_f = 15\ \text{mA} = 0.015\ \text{A}$ **Step 1 — Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) across the LED branch:** $$V_s = V_R + V_f$$ **Step 2 — Solve for voltage drop across resistor $V_R$:** $$V_R = V_s - V_f$$ $$V_R = 5\ \text{V} - 2.0\ \text{V} = 3.0\ \text{V}$$ **Step 3 — Apply Ohm's Law to find required resistance:** $$R = \dfrac{V_R}{I_f}$$ $$R = \dfrac{3.0\ \text{V}}{0.015\ \text{A}} = 200\ \Omega$$ **Step 4 — Select the nearest standard E12 resistor value:** The E12 standard series gives **220 Ω** as the closest commercially available value above the calculated minimum. **Step 5 — Recompute the actual LED current with the chosen resistor:** $$I_{f,\text{actual}} = \dfrac{V_s - V_f}{R_{\text{chosen}}}$$ $$I_{f,\text{actual}} = \dfrac{3.0}{220} \approx 13.6\ \text{mA}$$ This value is comfortably below the **20 mA absolute maximum** rating of the GPIO pin and provides safe LED operation with extended lifetime. **Step 6 — Verify resistor power dissipation is within rating:** $$P_R = I_f^2 \times R$$ $$P_R = (0.0136)^2 \times 220 \approx 0.0407\ \text{W} = 40.7\ \text{mW}$$ A standard **$\frac{1}{4}$ W (250 mW)** resistor is more than adequate, with a derating safety factor of $\approx 6\times$. ### 3.2 Derivation: IP Address Range and Subnet Mask Logic **Given:** A typical home router assigns IP via DHCP in the range `192.168.1.2` to `192.168.1.254`. **Step 1 — Identify Network ID and Host ID using the Subnet Mask:** Subnet mask: `255.255.255.0` (i.e., `/24` in CIDR notation) $$\text{Network ID} = \text{IP} \ \text{AND} \ \text{Mask}$$ $$\text{Network ID} = 192.168.1.10 \ \text{AND} \ 255.255.255.0 = 192.168.1.0$$ **Step 2 — Calculate Number of Usable Hosts:** $$N_{\text{hosts}} = 2^{(32 - \text{CIDR prefix})} - 2$$ $$N_{\text{hosts}} = 2^{(32 - 24)} - 2 = 2^8 - 2 = 254$$ (The subtraction of 2 accounts for the network address `192.168.1.0` and the broadcast address `192.168.1.255`.) **Step 3 — Confirm Gateway IP:** Typically `192.168.1.1`, which is the router's interface. The ESP8266 must be in the same subnet to communicate without routing. ### 3.3 Worked Example: HTTP Request Routing (Code Flow Trace) Consider the URL typed into a browser: ``` http://192.168.1.10/led/on ``` **Trace Through the Arduino Sketch:** | Line of Execution | Code Snippet | Register / State Change | |---|---|---| | 1 | `WiFiClient client = server.available();` | ESP8266 RX buffer accepts TCP segment | | 2 | `String req = client.readStringUntil('\r');` | Stores `GET /led/on HTTP/1.1` | | 3 | `server.on("/led/on", handleLedOn);` | Routing table matches URI | | 4 | `void handleLedOn() { digitalWrite(LED, HIGH); }` | GPIO OUT register bit `LED` ← 1 | | 5 | `server.send(200, "text/html", page);` | TX buffer flushes HTTP response | --- ## 4. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** *Define the term "IoT" and list any four enabling technologies that made it possible.* **Model Answer:** The **Internet of Things (IoT)** is a network of physical objects ("things") embedded with sensors, software, and communication technologies, interconnected via the Internet, enabling them to collect and exchange data without direct human intervention. Four enabling technologies are: 1. **Wireless Sensor Networks (WSN)** — ZigBee, LoRa, BLE 2. **Cloud Computing** — AWS IoT, Azure IoT Hub 3. **Embedded Microcontrollers** — ESP8266, ESP32, STM32 4. **Standard Communication Protocols** — MQTT, CoAP, HTTP/HTTPS *(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 2 marks for any four technologies)* --- **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** *What is the role of the AT commands when interfacing the ESP8266 with an Arduino Uno? Mention any two commonly used AT commands.* **Model Answer:** AT (Attention) commands are a legacy Hayes command set used to configure and control the ESP8266 Wi-Fi module when used as a slave peripheral with an Arduino Uno that lacks built-in WiFi. They are sent over **UART (SoftwareSerial/HardwareSerial)** and allow the Arduino to issue WiFi and TCP/IP instructions to the ESP8266. Two common AT commands: 1. `AT+CWJAP="SSID","PASSWORD"` — Connects to a WiFi Access Point. 2. `AT+CIPMUX=1` followed by `AT+CIPSERVER=1,80` — Starts a TCP server on port 80. *(3 marks: 1 mark role + 2 marks for AT commands with description)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a Web-Server-Controlled LED system using Arduino/ESP8266. Identify and label all blocks, including the client, network, server, controller, and actuator stages. *(7 marks)* **Model Answer:** The architecture of a Web-Server-Controlled LED system consists of **five functional blocks**: 1. **Client Tier (Browser):** A smartphone, tablet, or PC running a browser. It sends HTTP GET requests via a graphical HTML button interface and renders the received HTML page to display the LED state. 2. **Network Tier (WiFi Router/AP):** Acts as the bridge. Receives packets from the client over WiFi (IEEE 802.11) and forwards them to the server's IP. Implements **DHCP** for IP allocation and **NAT** for traffic routing. 3. **Server Tier (ESP8266 TCP/IP Stack):** Runs a lightweight HTTP server (using the `ESP8266WebServer.h` library). It listens on **port 80**, parses incoming URLs, and routes them to callback handlers. 4. **Controller Tier (Tensilica L106 MCU):** The 32-bit RISC processor executes the sketch. Based on the matched URL, it invokes `digitalWrite(LED_PIN, HIGH/LOW)` which manipulates the **GPIO output register**. 5. **Actuator Tier (LED Driver Circuit):** Consists of the LED in series with a **220 Ω current-limiting resistor**, drawing ~13.6 mA from the GPIO pin. When HIGH, photons are emitted at the LED's characteristic wavelength (e.g., 625 nm red). *(7 marks: 2 marks for block diagram, 3 marks for tier-wise explanation, 2 marks for specific technical details)* **(b)** Write the complete Arduino sketch to create a web server that turns an LED ON when the URL `/led/on` is accessed and OFF when `/led/off` is accessed. The LED is connected to GPIO pin **D1**. The WiFi SSID is `"KTU_LAB"` and password is `"12345678"`. *(7 marks)* **Model Answer:** ```cpp #include #include const char* ssid = "KTU_LAB"; const char* password = "12345678"; ESP8266WebServer server(80); const int LED_PIN = D1; // GPIO5 void handleLedOn() { digitalWrite(LED_PIN, HIGH); server.send(200, "text/html", "

LED is NOW ON

"); } void handleLedOff() { digitalWrite(LED_PIN, LOW); server.send(200, "text/html", "

LED is NOW OFF

"); } void setup() { Serial.begin(115200); pinMode(LED_PIN, OUTPUT); digitalWrite(LED_PIN, LOW); WiFi.begin(ssid, password); Serial.print("Connecting"); while (WiFi.status() != WL_CONNECTED) { delay(500); Serial.print("."); } Serial.println(); Serial.print("IP: "); Serial.println(WiFi.localIP()); server.on("/led/on", handleLedOn); server.on("/led/off", handleLedOff); server.begin(); } void loop() { server.handleClient(); } ``` **Explanation of Key Lines:** - `WiFi.begin()` initiates the WPA2 handshake with the AP. - `while (WiFi.status() != WL_CONNECTED)` is a blocking wait that prints dots until association succeeds. - `server.on()` registers two URL handlers in the routing table stored in flash. - `server.handleClient()` in `loop()` is mandatory — it polls for incoming connections and dispatches them. - `digitalWrite()` directly drives the GPIO register to toggle the LED. *(7 marks: 1 mark for headers, 2 marks for setup with WiFi connect, 2 marks for handler functions, 2 marks for loop and routing)* --- #### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** **(a)** Explain the **HTTP request-response cycle** in detail. Draw the packet structure of a typical GET request and a 200 OK response. *(7 marks)* **Model Answer:** The HTTP cycle is a **stateless, request-driven** transaction between a client and server over TCP. **Request Packet Structure (sent by browser):** ``` GET /led/on HTTP/1.1\r\n Host: 192.168.1.10\r\n User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0\r\n Accept: text/html\r\n Connection: keep-alive\r\n \r\n ``` - `GET` — HTTP method (other methods: POST, PUT, DELETE) - `/led/on` — Uniform Resource Identifier (URI) - `HTTP/1.1` — Protocol version - Headers — Provide metadata about the client - `\r\n\r\n` — Blank line marks end of headers **Response Packet Structure (sent by server):** ``` HTTP/1.1 200 OK\r\n Content-Type: text/html\r\n Content-Length: 42\r\n Connection: close\r\n \r\n

LED is NOW ON

``` - `200 OK` — Standard success status code - `Content-Type` — MIME type tells browser how to render - Body contains the rendered HTML The browser parses the HTML, builds the DOM tree, and displays it as a webpage. *(7 marks: 3 marks for cycle explanation, 2 marks for request structure, 2 marks for response structure)* **(b)** Differentiate between **Station (STA) Mode**, **Access Point (AP) Mode**, and **STA+AP Mixed Mode** of the ESP8266. In which mode would you operate the ESP8266 for the LED web server experiment in this lab, and why? *(7 marks)* **Model Answer:** | Mode | Behavior | IP Address Source | Use Case | |---|---|---|---| | **Station (STA)** | ESP8266 connects to an existing router as a "client" | DHCP from router | Connecting to home/office WiFi | | **Access Point (AP)** | ESP8266 itself creates its own WiFi network | ESP8266 acts as DHCP server (default `192.168.4.1`) | Direct phone-to-ESP connection without internet | | **STA + AP Mixed** | Both modes simultaneously active | Router-assigned IP AND its own subnet | Mesh setups, fallback networks | **Recommended Mode for this Experiment:** For the lab experiment, **Station (STA) Mode** is preferred because: 1. The lab router provides a stable, pre-shared IP address that can be printed to the Serial Monitor and bookmarked by all students. 2. Existing infrastructure (college WiFi) eliminates the need to reconfigure every phone to join the ESP8266's AP. 3. STA mode is consistent with how real-world IoT devices (smart bulbs, Alexa) connect to home routers. If the lab lacks WiFi infrastructure, **AP mode** is the fallback, though limited to ~4 simultaneous clients. *(7 marks: 3 marks for mode table, 2 marks for recommendation, 2 marks for justification)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting `server.handleClient()` in `loop()`**: This is the #1 reason students report "My code compiled but the web page doesn't load!" The server requires continuous polling in the main loop. (-3 marks) > 2. **Confusing `digitalWrite(HIGH)` with `5V` on ESP8266**: ESP8266 GPIOs output **3.3 V**, NOT 5 V. Using a blue LED with $V_f = 3.2\ \text{V}$ directly will produce very dim light. Add an appropriate resistor or use a logic-level MOSFET. (-2 marks) > 3. **Wrong Baud Rate in Serial Monitor**: Using 9600 baud instead of 115200 will produce garbage characters, making IP address reading impossible. (-1 mark) > 4. **Missing `delay(500)` after `WiFi.begin()` in synchronous code**: Without a non-blocking delay or status check, the loop will spin and prevent the system from connecting. (-1 mark) > 5. **Closing the HTML tag**: Forgetting `` in the response makes the browser fall back to "quirks mode," potentially mis-rendering the response. (-1 mark) --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > **Rapid-Fire Memory Bank — Read Before Entering the Lab:** - [ ] **ESP8266 = 3.3 V logic**, never connect its GPIO directly to a 5 V Arduino Uno output without a level shifter. - [ ] **LED Resistor** = $R = \dfrac{V_s - V_f}{I_f}$ → typically **220 Ω** for 5 V / Red LED. - [ ] **HTTP Port** = **80**; **WiFi Band** = **2.4 GHz only** (no 5 GHz support). - [ ] **Routing Command**: `server.on("/path", handlerFunction);` — registered once inside `setup()`. - [ ] **Mandatory Loop Code**: `server.handleClient();` must be present for any request to be processed. - [ ] **SSID + Password** are case-sensitive strings; verify them on a working phone first. - [ ] **Default IP after DHCP**: Print `WiFi.localIP()` to Serial Monitor to discover the assigned address. - [ ] **Static IP** is set via `WiFi.config(local, gateway, subnet)` BEFORE `WiFi.begin()`. - [ ] **Browser Cache**: If the page shows old data, perform a hard refresh (`Ctrl + F5`). - [ ] **Two Implementation Paths**: - **Path 1**: Arduino Uno + ESP8266 (AT commands over SoftwareSerial) — verbose. - **Path 2**: ESP8266 NodeMCU alone — runs Arduino IDE natively; preferred in modern labs. - [ ] **Maximum simultaneous clients** in softAP mode = **4** by default (configurable). - [ ] **Flash before powering**: Always disconnect the LED circuit before serial programming to avoid GPIO contention. - [ ] **GPIO pins to avoid**: `GPIO0`, `GPIO2`, `GPIO15` have boot-mode pull-up/down constraints; use `D1 (GPIO5)`, `D2 (GPIO4)`, or `D5 (GPIO14)` for general LED control. - [ ] **Library requirements**: `#include ` and `#include ` — install via Boards Manager URL `http://arduino.esp8266.com/stable/package_esp8266com_index.json`. - [ ] **Memory tip**: Storing large HTML strings in `PROGMEM` saves RAM on long pages. - [ ] **Security**: Plain HTTP over WiFi without TLS is readable on the network — for production, use HTTPS (which requires the `WiFiClientSecure` library and a chunk of flash for the certificate). ---
